series.series_logline: For the reader who feels trapped in a labyrinth of their own racing thoughts, searching for a single clear thread to follow.
series.series_description: A quiet, introspective manga following a young archivist who learns to bind the chaotic scrolls of her mind into a coherent narrative. The series uses the metaphor of bookbinding and library science to explore focus, mental organization, and self-acceptance for the neurodivergent experience.

series.series_logline: For the reader whose anxiety manifests as a physical tremor, who seeks a craft to steady both their hands and their heart.

series.series_logline: For the reader who can start a hundred tasks but feels a wall of dread when trying to finish just one.

series.series_logline: For the reader who uses complex games and systems to understand and regulate the overwhelming chaos of their own emotions.

series.series_logline: For the reader whose brain feels like a radio picking up every station at once, who longs to tune into just one beautiful signal.

series.series_logline: For the reader whose anxiety latches onto a single, intricate task, using deep focus as a shield from a swirling mind.

series.series_logline: For the reader who rehearses every conversation in their head, trying to pre-write a world that feels safe and predictable.

series.main_character_role: Marketing associate obsessively copying her mentor

series.series_logline: For the reader who replays every conversation, searching for the mistake that proves they don't belong.
series.series_description: A junior architect, paralyzed by the fear her designs are fraudulent, meets Joshin, a retired master builder. Through quiet lessons in observing the structure of her own thoughts, she learns to build from a place of stillness, not anxiety. A seinen series about deconstructing the architecture of self-doubt.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the profound release of a thought finally losing its power over you, like a knot coming undone.
comp_titles: March Comes in Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: For fans of the introspective character studies in 'Blue Period,' this series offers a practical, meditative toolkit for the overthinking professional, framed as a master-apprentice narrative in the world of design.
series.main_character_role: anxious junior architect

series.series_logline: For the reader who feels like a fraud waiting to be exposed, no matter how full their resume gets.
series.series_description: A celebrated young chef, hailed as a prodigy, is secretly convinced each award is a fluke. Mentor Joshin, a humble tea master, guides her through the simple, repetitive art of preparing matcha—a practice where the goal is not to achieve, but to be present. A josei story about filling your worth from an empty cup.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the warm, steady calm of a task with no right answer, where your only job is to pay attention.
comp_titles: March Comes in Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: Blends the culinary introspection of 'Solanin' with the gentle mentorship found in slice-of-life classics. Positions imposter syndrome not as a battle to win, but as noise to quiet through ritual.
series.main_character_role: acclaimed but insecure chef

series.series_logline: For the reader who believes their value is a theorem they must constantly solve and prove to others.
series.series_description: A brilliant but burned-out mathematics PhD candidate is trapped in a cycle of seeking external validation. Her path crosses with Joshin, a gardener who teaches her about growth that requires no witness. A shojo-tinged narrative where equations of self-worth are dissolved in the soil of simple being.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the gentle, surprising relief of being seen not for your achievements, but for the quiet fact of your existence.
series.marketing_angle: A unique fusion of academic pressure and pastoral healing. Appeals to young adults navigating performance anxiety, offering the emotional clarity of 'Blue Period' through a lens of mathematical metaphor and natural wisdom.
series.main_character_role: exhausted mathematics PhD candidate

series.series_logline: For the reader whose every success is immediately shadowed by the dread of the next inevitable failure.
series.series_description: A rising star in the competitive world of shogi is plagued not by opponents, but by a pervasive sense of dread that he is one move away from being exposed as a hack. Joshin, an old player who sees the board differently, becomes his unconventional coach. A psychological shonen sports manga about facing the opponent within.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the tense, gripping shift when a familiar fear is finally faced head-on, losing its formless power.
comp_titles: March Comes in Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: Takes the intense mental battles of 'Ping Pong' and 'March Comes in Like a Lion' and applies them to the specific terror of imposter syndrome in a high-stakes arena. A sports manga for the overthinker.
series.main_character_role: shogi prodigy gripped by dread

series.series_logline: For the reader who curates a mental archive of every past misstep, mistake, and moment of perceived inadequacy.
series.series_description: A meticulous archivist for a historical museum is expert at preserving the past—except her own, which she revisits with punishing detail. When Joshin is hired to restore a neglected garden on the grounds, his work becomes a living lesson in letting things decay and grow anew. A josei workplace story about the freedom of selective memory.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the bittersweet, liberating weight of closing a heavy ledger on your past self, page by page.
series.marketing_angle: A poignant metaphor for rumination, perfect for readers of character-driven josei. Combines the workplace realism of 'Planetes' with the emotional depth of 'Solanin' to explore how we make peace with our personal history.
series.main_character_role: meticulous museum archivist

series.series_logline: For the reader whose mind is a crowded room of conflicting advice, self-criticism, and rehearsed conversations.
series.series_description: A sound engineer, skilled at isolating frequencies, is overwhelmed by the internal noise of her own judgments. Seeking silence, she meets Joshin, a instrument maker who works in a vibration-free studio. Their collaboration becomes a lesson in hearing the space between thoughts. A seinen healing manga about finding clarity not in addition, but in subtraction.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the expansive, awe-filled quiet that arrives when the mental static fades, leaving only pure perception.
series.marketing_angle: Appeals to the technically-minded overthinker. Uses the metaphor of audio engineering to make abstract mental processes tangible, similar to how 'Blue Period' visualizes artistic struggle. A fresh take on mindfulness.
series.main_character_role: overwhelmed sound engineer

series.series_logline: For the reader who feels fundamentally unmoored, as if their accomplishments are just feathers in the wind.
series.series_description: A freelance translator, fluent in five languages but feeling rootless in all of them, struggles with a profound sense of having no inherent worth. Her life changes when she takes a job translating for Joshin, a calligrapher whose art explores the weight of a single, deliberate stroke. A slice-of-life story about finding gravity in simplicity.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the deep, aching pull toward a center you didn't know you had, a quiet anchor in the self.
series.marketing_angle: Captures the global, nomadic experience of modern knowledge workers. Offers the aesthetic contemplation of 'Vagabond's quieter moments with the relatable urban loneliness of 'Solanin,' directly addressing core questions of self-worth.
series.main_character_role: rootless freelance translator

series.series_logline: For the reader who For the reader trapped in a exhausting, silent competition with a phantom version of themselves who is always better.
series.series_description: A competitive programmer sees every colleague as a benchmark, but her true rival is the idealized, flawless coder she believes she should be. Enter Joshin, a debugger from the old school, who teaches her that the most elegant solution often comes from accepting the messy, functional code of the present self. A shonen tech thriller about ending the civil war in your own mind.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the fierce, triumphant rush of calling off a fight that was never winnable, because the opponent was you.
series.marketing_angle: Merges the high-stakes problem-solving of tech thrillers with the internal rivalry themes of sports manga like 'Ping Pong.' A direct, energetic take on imposter syndrome for the achievement-driven shonen audience.
series.main_character_role: competitive programmer

series.series_logline: For the reader who feels their voice disappear in a crowded room, and needs a small, steady ritual to find it again.
series.series_description: A gentle, introspective manga about a young office worker who discovers that tying a specific knot in her scarf before meetings grounds her anxiety. Each chapter explores a different 'small anchor'—a tactile, repeatable action that builds a bridge to calm presence. Aimed at socially anxious adults seeking tangible tools.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the grounding weight of a simple, personal ritual that makes the noisy world feel manageable.
series.marketing_angle: Sells the concept of 'micro-rituals for macro-calm.' Perfect for readers who loved the tactile comfort of 'Barakamon' but need a modern, urban, self-applied version.
series.main_character_role: anxious junior graphic designer

series.series_logline: For the reader who is convinced their success is a fluke, and that a single mistake will reveal them as a fraud.
series.series_description: A supernatural slice-of-life where a brilliant but insecure software engineer is literally haunted by a gentle, persistent 'ghost' that represents her imposter syndrome. The series follows her journey to understand, befriend, and ultimately integrate this spectral part of herself. Blends mild fantasy with deep emotional realism.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the profound relief of externalizing your inner critic and finally seeing it as separate from your true self.
series.marketing_angle: A literal metaphor for imposter syndrome, making the internal struggle visible and negotiable. Appeals to fans of character-driven supernatural drama like 'Fruits Basket,' but for a professional adult audience.
series.main_character_role: haunted lead software developer

series.series_logline: For the reader who meticulously scripts conversations in their head, only to freeze when the moment to speak arrives.
series.series_description: A romance-tinged school drama about a painfully shy member of the drama club who can only express herself fluently when reading from a script. The series charts her growth as she learns to treat real-life interactions as improvisational scenes, finding confidence through the framework of theater.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the exhilarating shift from fearing a blank page to seeing every social moment as a script you can co-write.
series.marketing_angle: Leverages the universal appeal of school clubs and slow-burn romance, while providing a concrete 'framework' for social anxiety. For readers who related to Komi's struggles but want a more active, skill-building journey.
series.main_character_role: shy high school drama club member

series.series_logline: For the reader who believes they must face their social fears alone, and is terrified of being a burden to others.
series.series_description: A workplace healing manga about a group of colleagues from different departments who secretly form a support group for social anxiety. They meet in a disused company rooftop garden, sharing strategies and practicing low-stakes interactions in a safe, judgment-free zone. Focuses on community and mutual growth.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the warm, quiet solidarity of discovering your struggles are shared, and that support can be a collaborative project.
series.marketing_angle: Positions anxiety management as a team sport. A 'found family at work' narrative that combats isolation, ideal for fans of the ensemble warmth in 'Honey and Clover' translated to a corporate setting.
series.main_character_role: quiet IT specialist and group founder

series.series_logline: For the reader whose own negative self-talk is the loudest voice in the room, replaying past social failures on a loop.
series.series_description: A psychological mystery where a freelance writer begins to hear literal, critical whispers that no one else can hear. To silence them, she must consciously collect and catalog evidence of her own small, daily successes—turning her case notes into a weapon against her internal critic. A battle fought with observation and facts.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the powerful shift from being hunted by your thoughts to becoming the detective building a case for your own worth.
series.marketing_angle: Frames the journey to self-worth as a solvable mystery. Appeals to readers who enjoy procedural elements and the introspective, raw honesty of works like 'My Lesbian Experience With Loneliness.'
series.main_character_role: freelance writer plagued by auditory criticism

series.series_logline: For the reader who feels they are perpetually in someone else's shadow, and that their best efforts will never be enough.
series.series_description: A sports manga focusing not on the champion, but on the eternally reliable, anxiety-prone vice-captain of a college kendo team. His journey is about redefining victory, finding worth in consistency, support, and resilience rather than trophies, and learning to lead from a place of perceived weakness.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the deep validation that comes from recognizing the indispensable, quiet strength of being the steady foundation.
series.marketing_angle: Subverts classic sports manga tropes by making the protagonist the supportive 'rock' instead of the star. Offers a new model of masculinity and success for readers who feel overshadowed.
series.main_character_role: anxious vice-captain of a university kendo team

series.series_logline: For the reader who feels a nameless dread before social obligations, a heavy fog that obscures what they're actually afraid of.
series.series_description: A fantasy-tinged healing series where a young librarian has the magical ability to physically pull her amorphous anxieties out of her chest as glowing orbs. To dispel them, she must journey into a symbolic 'archive' within herself to find and speak their true, specific names—transforming vague dread into manageable concerns.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the clarifying magic of watching a blurry, overwhelming fear become a defined, knowable thing you can handle.
series.marketing_angle: Uses gentle fantasy to visualize the process of emotional granularity. Perfect for readers who love the metaphorical worlds of 'Fruits Basket' and seek a creative, hopeful approach to understanding anxiety.
series.main_character_role: magical librarian of internal anxieties

series.series_logline: For the reader who treats every minor social misstep as a catastrophic failure, replaying and magnifying it for days.
series.series_description: A darkly comedic slice-of-life about a perfectionist tax accountant who, after a panic attack, starts to see his perceived social 'failures' as literal, tiny monsters. The series follows his grimly funny journey to calculate the 'acceptable loss ratio' of these creatures, learning that some awkwardness is simply the cost of human connection.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the liberating dark laugh of quantifying your social fears and realizing the numbers are never as bad as you imagined.
series.marketing_angle: Blends deadpan accounting humor with monster-of-the-week style struggles. A unique, pragmatic take on anxiety for readers who appreciate the raw, awkward honesty of 'My Lesbian Experience With Loneliness' but with a more analytical, male-coded lens.
series.main_character_role: perfectionist tax accountant haunted by failure-monsters

series.series_logline: For the reader who believes their art is never good enough, and whose inner critic drowns out every brushstroke.
series.series_description: A gentle, introspective manga about a pottery student learning that the cracks in her work are what make it beautiful. Through tactile creation and quiet mentorship, she discovers that self-worth isn't measured by perfection. A healing story for anyone who creates.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the grounded, warm release of clay in your hands and the quieting of a harsh inner voice.
series.marketing_angle: A tactile, meditative manga that redefines artistic success as presence over perfection. Perfect for fans of 'Barakamon' seeking a softer, more interior journey of creative acceptance.
series.main_character_role: anxious pottery student

series.series_logline: For the reader who feels like a fraud waiting to be exposed every time they share a new idea.
series.series_description: A screenwriter's career skyrockets after a script she doesn't remember writing becomes a hit. As she chases the ghost of her own genius, she must confront whether she ever deserved the praise. A mystery-thriller about authorship and anxiety.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the chilling, curious unraveling of the story you tell yourself about your own capabilities.
series.marketing_angle: A psychological page-turner that uses the framework of a mystery to explore the terror of imposter syndrome. For readers who love the raw honesty of 'My Lesbian Experience With Loneliness' but crave a plot-driven hook.
series.main_character_role: successful but haunted screenwriter

series.series_logline: For the reader who only feels creatively free in the hidden, unjudged hours after everyone else is asleep.
series.series_description: A group of insomniac students form a secret drawing club that meets from 2 to 4 AM. In the silent campus, with no grades or eyes upon them, they rediscover the pure curiosity that first made them pick up a pencil.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the expansive, curious freedom of creating something meant for no one's eyes but your own.
series.marketing_angle: A magical realist slice-of-life about the secret creative life we hide from the world. Captures the 'Honey and Clover' vibe of artistic camaraderie, but with a focus on nocturnal intimacy and personal discovery.
series.main_character_role: architecture student and chronic insomniac

series.series_logline: For the reader who is tired of being the reliable support to everyone else's brilliant solo.
series.series_description: A virtuoso session musician has built a career making other artists sound perfect. When offered a chance to compose her own album, the pressure to be the 'main character' triggers a paralyzing crisis of confidence.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the terrifying, exhilarating pull of stepping into a spotlight you've spent your life carefully avoiding.
series.marketing_angle: A sophisticated workplace drama set in the competitive music industry, exploring the transition from supporter to star. Appeals to professionals who understand the unique anxiety of claiming your own voice.
series.main_character_role: elite session cellist

series.series_logline: For the reader who gets stuck in a loop of starting over, never finishing because the beginning isn't perfect.
series.series_description: A manga artist is cursed with the literal ability to erase her reality and redraw it. Trapped in endless revisions of her own life, she must learn to accept a 'final draft' with all its flaws to break the cycle.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the cathartic snap of a pencil point breaking, signaling the end of an exhausting, perfect loop.
series.marketing_angle: A high-concept supernatural drama that makes the metaphor of creative paralysis viscerally real. For readers who feel the 'Fruits Basket' blend of character pain and magical circumstance, but applied to artistic process.
series.main_character_role: manga assistant with a supernatural curse

series.series_logline: For the reader who feels their creative spirit has withered, and is afraid it will never bloom again.
series.series_description: A once-celebrated floral artist, now believing her talent has died, takes a job restoring a neglected botanical garden. Each struggling plant she nurses back to health slowly teaches her to extend the same compassion to herself.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the gentle, patient unfurling of a new leaf, both on the vine and within your own chest.
series.marketing_angle: A visually lush, therapeutic manga where the process of healing a garden becomes a metaphor for healing self-worth. Sits alongside 'Barakamon' in its rural setting and focus on craft-as-therapy.
series.main_character_role: burnt-out ikebana artist

series.series_logline: For the reader who uses competition as fuel, but fears that without a rival, they have nothing to say.
series.series_description: Two painters have defined their careers and selves in opposition to each other. When one suddenly retires, the other is left adrift, forced to discover if her art—and her identity—can exist without the shadow of her rival.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the disorienting, then liberating, vacuum left when the person you defined yourself against disappears.
series.marketing_angle: A sharp, character-driven exploration of how identity is forged in opposition. For ambitious readers who understand how a rival can be both a prison and a propellant, similar to the dynamics in 'Blue Period'.
series.main_character_role: competitive contemporary painter

series.series_logline: For the reader who For the reader grieving a version of themselves that was once effortlessly, joyfully creative.
series.series_description: A former child prodigy composer, now a jaded music teacher, stumbles upon recordings of her youthful, experimental works. Re-engaging with the ghost of her past talent becomes a journey through grief and towards a new, more authentic creative voice.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the profound, cathartic release of finally mourning the 'genius' you thought you lost, to make space for who you are now.
series.marketing_angle: A poignant story about creative aging and the weight of early promise. Resonates with the emotional depth and autobiographical feel of 'My Lesbian Experience With Loneliness', applied to a different artistic discipline.
series.main_character_role: disillusioned music teacher and ex-prodigy

series.series_logline: For the reader who keeps a mental tally of every kindness given, wondering when their own account will finally be filled.
series.series_description: A burnt-out hospice nurse learns from Sai Ma that true compassion isn't a transaction. Through quiet lessons in a sunlit garden, she discovers that self-worth isn't earned through sacrifice, but is her inherent right. A gentle, poignant josei series about reclaiming the self.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the profound release of closing an invisible ledger you never agreed to keep.
series.marketing_angle: For every caregiver who feels their empathy is running on empty. This series visualizes the emotional math of giving and offers a path to erase the debt you never owed.
series.main_character_role: exhausted hospice nurse

series.series_logline: For the reader who says 'yes' with their mouth while their soul screams 'no', afraid that a 'no' makes them unlovable.
series.series_description: A people-pleasing office manager is mentored by Sai Ma, who teaches that boundaries are not walls but gates—you decide who and what gets your precious energy. A seinen workplace drama where the real battle is learning to protect your own peace.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the quiet, sturdy safety of building a fence around the part of you that is always tired.
series.marketing_angle: Moves beyond simple 'self-care' tropes. This is a tactical guide, framed as a manga, for constructing emotional boundaries in a demanding world, perfect for overworked salarymen and professionals.
series.main_character_role: overwhelmed office manager

series.series_logline: For the reader who measures their value in tasks completed and needs met, forgetting they are a person, not a function.
series.series_description: A young mother feels her identity dissolving into the needs of her family. Sai Ma guides her to see that her worth isn't measured by her productivity or perpetual availability. A shojo-infused slice-of-life about finding the person you were before you became someone's everything.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the gentle shock of recognizing your own reflection after years of only seeing what you provide for others.
series.marketing_angle: A compassionate mirror for young mothers and primary caregivers. This series validates the invisible labor of caregiving while offering a soft, visual path back to self-discovery.
series.main_character_role: stay-at-home mother feeling invisible

series.series_logline: For the reader who loves so fiercely it feels like an illness, convinced that if they just love *harder*, they will finally be enough.
series.series_description: A passionate but insecure artist falls into a toxic relationship, believing relentless devotion is the price of love. Sai Ma intervenes, teaching that love should not feel like a fever. A raw, emotional josei romance about healing from the addiction to over-giving.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the deep, aching relief of letting a harmful love go, and the quiet wonder of the peace that follows.
series.marketing_angle: Tackles 'love addiction' and codependency through a wellness lens. This is for readers who see their own exhausting relationship patterns reflected and need a guide to break the cycle.
series.main_character_role: codependent artist in a draining relationship

series.series_logline: For the reader who For the reader on the brink of compassion fatigue, who gives their last bit of energy away and is left running on fumes.
series.series_description: A dedicated social worker hits absolute burnout. Sai Ma finds him and teaches the radical practice of 'compassion preservation'—how to serve from a renewable source, not a draining well. A seinen drama set in the high-stakes world of social services.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the slow, steady return of a vital force you thought was gone forever.
series.marketing_angle: Goes beyond typical burnout stories. This series provides a philosophical and practical framework for sustainable empathy, ideal for professionals in caregiving fields.
series.main_character_role: burned-out social worker

series.series_logline: For the reader who can champion everyone else's dreams but hears only static when trying to listen to their own inner voice.
series.series_description: A talented but self-effacing music teacher, Sai Ma's newest student, has built her life around facilitating others' talents. Sai Ma's lessons help her tune into her own faint 'worth whisper' and amplify it into a song. A shojo school-life story about audibility of the self.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the thrilling surprise of hearing your own desires clearly for the first time, like a favorite song you'd forgotten.
series.marketing_angle: A metaphor-rich series for young adults who feel overshadowed. It transforms the abstract concept of self-worth into the tangible act of listening to and trusting one's own inner music.
series.main_character_role: self-doubting high school music teacher

series.series_logline: For the reader whose home is a recharge station for everyone else, leaving no quiet corner where they can simply exist.
series.series_description: A freelance designer who works from home finds her apartment constantly invaded by friends and family seeking solace. Sai Ma mentors her in the art of creating physical and emotional sanctuary. A shonen-tinged slice-of-life about the radical act of claiming space for oneself.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the deep, grounding comfort of finally having a place—both in your home and in your heart—that is just for you.
series.marketing_angle: Appeals to young adults navigating shared living spaces and porous boundaries. It frames creating personal sanctuary as an active, almost heroic practice of self-preservation.
series.main_character_role: freelance designer with no personal space

series.series_logline: For the reader who For the reader preparing to give one more piece of themselves away, sensing it might be the piece that breaks them.
series.series_description: A junior doctor faces a crisis when a patient's needs trigger her own unresolved trauma. Sai Ma appears, guiding her through the difference between compassionate service and self-annihilation. A tense, psychological josei thriller set in a hospital, where the mystery is the protagonist's own limits.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the visceral tension of approaching your breaking point, and the shocking clarity of choosing yourself instead.
series.marketing_angle: A high-stakes wellness thriller. It uses the pressure-cooker environment of a hospital to explore the ultimate boundary: the one between saving others and destroying yourself.
series.main_character_role: traumatized junior doctor on the edge

series.series_logline: For the reader who checks their phone for notifications that aren't there, convinced they're missing something vital in the silence.
series.series_description: A young office worker named Ren develops phone-checking tics that disrupt his work and sleep. Miki teaches him to distinguish between digital phantom limbs and real bodily awareness through somatic tracking exercises. A gentle, observational manga about retraining attention in a world of constant alerts.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the slow-motion relief of your shoulders dropping when you realize nothing is actually demanding your response.
comp_titles: March Comes in Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: Tackles the modern anxiety of 'phantom notification syndrome' with practical, somatic exercises. Perfect for readers who feel their phone is an extension of their nervous system.
series.main_character_role: anxious junior office worker plagued by compulsive phone-checking

series.series_logline: For the reader who lies in bed mentally archiving every awkward conversation and unfinished task from the day, unable to shut the internal browser.
series.series_description: Sora, a university student, suffers from severe sleep anxiety where her mind replays the day's digital interactions like a corrupted file. Miki guides her to 'defragment' her thoughts before bed using analog journaling and memory palace techniques, building a peaceful mental archive instead of a chaotic one.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the quiet satisfaction of closing all your mental tabs one by one, until only a blank, restful screen remains.
comp_titles: March Comes in Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: A visually inventive manga that treats intrusive nighttime thoughts as pop-up windows and corrupted files, offering a systematic 'shutdown' ritual for the mind.
series.main_character_role: university student with chronic sleep-onset anxiety

series.series_logline: For the reader who curates their personality from trending topics and algorithmically-approved aesthetics, forgetting what they genuinely like.
series.series_description: Aoi, a graphic designer, feels her identity is entirely constructed from her social media feeds and client feedback. Miki teaches her to create a physical 'buffer zone' in her apartment—a tech-free corner where she rediscovers her own tastes, desires, and quiet thoughts through simple crafts.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the tender, surprising spark of recognizing a preference that came from within you, not from a feed.
series.marketing_angle: Explores the loss of authentic self in the age of personal branding. Offers a tangible, craft-based practice to rebuild an identity separate from the digital performance.
series.main_character_role: graphic designer whose sense of self is tied to online trends

series.series_logline: For the reader who uses doomscrolling as a sleep aid, only to find themselves more wired and anxious in the blue-lit dark.
series.series_description: Kaito, a freelance writer, has trained his brain to associate his phone with bedtime, leading to hours of restless scrolling. Miki introduces him to the deliberate slowness of analog rituals—brewing tea, mending clothes, sketching—to create a new, calming pre-sleep pathway that demands focused presence.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the deep, bodily comfort of a ritual that engages your hands and fully occupies your mind, leaving no room for digital noise.
series.marketing_angle: Positions simple, tactile tasks as powerful antagonists to compulsive scrolling. A manga that finds profound mindfulness in mending a sock or grinding coffee beans.
series.main_character_role: freelance writer with a doomscrolling sleep habit

series.series_logline: For the reader whose mind is a crowded, overgrown garden of 'what-ifs' and rehearsed conversations that never get pruned.
series.series_description: Miki mentors Emi, a florist's assistant whose creativity is paralyzed by cyclical overthinking. Using the metaphor of gardening, Miki teaches her to identify 'weeds' (unproductive worries), 'perennials' (recurring useful thoughts), and to create space for new growth through mindful pruning and composting of mental clutter.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the liberating clarity of uprooting a tangled, invasive thought and placing it on the compost pile to decompose.
series.marketing_angle: A beautiful, metaphor-driven guide to cognitive gardening. Teaches readers to curate their inner landscape with the same care they'd give a physical garden.
series.main_character_role: florist's assistant stifled by mental clutter and overthinking

series.series_logline: For the reader who attends a concert, a meal, or a conversation while mentally composing the perfect post about it.
series.series_description: Ryo, a music student, realizes he experiences life through the filter of how he will document it online. Miki helps him locate his internal 'presence dial,' teaching him to turn down the volume on the narrating, performing self and turn up the volume on raw, unmediated sensory input.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the startling vividness of a taste, a sound, or a moment when you receive it directly, without the buffer of a caption.
comp_titles: March Comes in Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: Addresses 'experience capture' anxiety head-on. A manga for anyone who feels they're living life twice: once in real time, and once for the timeline.
series.main_character_role: music student who lives to document experiences online

series.series_logline: For the reader who mistakes the constant low hum of digital anxiety for their own personality, believing they are just a 'nervous person.'
series.series_description: Miki works with Haruka, a quiet librarian who attributes her constant unease to her nature. Miki guides her to identify the 'ambient signal'—the background noise of news alerts, social comparison, and informational overload—and teaches her techniques to tune her personal receiver to a different, more peaceful frequency.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the profound gentleness of separating your own heart's quiet rhythm from the static of the world's endless broadcast.
series.marketing_angle: A deeply empathetic series that reframes chronic anxiety as a signal-processing issue in a noisy world, not a personal failing. Offers 'frequency tuning' as a wellness practice.
series.main_character_role: librarian who internalizes digital world anxiety as her own temperament

series.series_logline: For the reader who feels a pang of grief when a meaningful online conversation ends, leaving only a silent chat window as a monument.
series.series_description: After a deep, months-long online friendship abruptly vanishes, Yuna is left with intense grief and confusion. Miki helps her process this 'ghost connection'—a relationship that felt intensely real but lacked physical anchors. They explore how to mourn digital intimacy and find closure without a trace.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the bittersweet validation of having your digital grief witnessed and named, allowing it to finally settle.
comp_titles: March Comes in Like a Lion

series.series_description: A young diplomat's only tool for navigating tense negotiations is compulsive apology, eroding her authority and self-respect. Maat, serving as an advisor, teaches her the martial art of 'standing firm without striking'—how to hold her ground with respect, transforming weak apologies into clear, boundaried statements. A seinen workplace drama about diplomatic and personal integrity.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the ground solidify beneath your feet as you replace the wobbly word 'sorry' with the firm, clear language of your own needs and limits.

series.series_logline: For the reader who is grieving not just a person, but the future version of themselves that died with them.

A poignant, visual exploration of carrying grief without being consumed by it. An anthology topic series.

series.series_logline: For the reader who was taught their value is a score to be maxed out, and now audits their own humanity like a failing ledger.

series.series_logline: For the reader who feels their mind racing before a big presentation, seeking a single, steadying breath.

A seinen workplace drama about reframing nervous energy.

series.series_logline: For the reader who practices their pitch in the mirror, not for charm, but to stop their hands from shaking.

series.series_logline: For the reader who performs 'okay' so convincingly at work that even they start to doubt their own silent struggle.

series.series_logline: For the reader who feels their anxiety as a physical tremor in their hands, seeking a single point of control.

series.series_logline: For the reader who feels crushed by the weight of infinite possibilities and the anxiety of choosing just one path.

series.series_logline: For the reader whose anxiety makes crowded spaces feel suffocating, who longs for literal and figurative room to breathe.

series.series_logline: For the reader who overthink's life's impermanence, whose anxiety is rooted in a deep, unspoken grief for moments already passing.

Master Feung teaches him to track the physical sensation of irritation—the 'last nerve'—as a qi signal, not a failure. Transforming dread into data, he learns somatic practices to dissolve tension before it controls him.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the tense, coiled spring in your gut slowly unwind as you learn to meet your own irritation with curiosity instead of fear.

series.series_logline: For the reader who is obsessed with the perfect, unachievable recovery, constantly comparing their slow healing to an idealized past version of themselves.

The tone is soft, introspective, and deeply validating for anyone who feels their worth is tied to their availability.

series.marketing_angle: A therapeutic manga that visualizes the physical and emotional weight of people-pleasing, offering a step-by-step, compassionate guide to reclaiming your time and energy. Perfect for fans of introspective character studies.

The series visually maps his internal storms and the quiet spaces between them, focusing on incremental progress over dramatic cure.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the profound relief of finding a single, steady point of calm in the middle of your own mental hurricane.

series.series_logline: For the reader who rehearses apologies for things that weren't their fault, haunted by the ghost of every perceived slight.

Junko, a bookstore owner with a calming presence, helps her learn to 'clean' these apparitions not by apologizing, but by practicing self-compassion. series.series_logline: For the reader who is so exhausted from performing 'okayness' for their family that they have forgotten what their own face looks like at rest.
series.series_description: A seinen workplace manga about a man in his 30s who manages his family's expectations while climbing the corporate ladder. After a panic attack, he starts visiting a unique 'Safe House' cafe run by Junko, where the only rule is 'no performing.' The series explores the slow, awkward, but healing process of taking off the relational mask and being authentically, quietly himself.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the deep, bone-level comfort of being in a space where you are not required to be anything other than tired.
comp_titles: Wotakoi: Love Is Hard for Otaku
series.marketing_angle: Positions the cafe as a 'third place' for emotional recovery. A grounded, relatable series for overworked professionals yearning for a sanctuary from performance.
series.main_character_role: mid-level salaryman and eldest son

series.series_logline: For the reader who feels a sharp, secret shame whenever they take something for themselves, believing they haven't earned it.
series.series_description: A shonen sports-adjacent manga about a dedicated but self-sacrificing member of a college rowing team. Junko, the team's nutritionist and mental coach, teaches him that taking nourishment—whether food, rest, or praise—is not a reward for performance, but a foundation for being. The series uses the physicality of sport to explore the psychology of worthiness and receiving.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the fierce, defiant pride of claiming what you need, simply because you exist and are trying.
series.marketing_angle: Merges the motivational energy of sports manga with a nuanced therapy narrative. It reframes 'strength' not as endurance, but as the courage to accept support and care.
series.main_character_role: reserve rower on a university team

series.series_logline: For the reader who For the reader trapped in the echo chamber of their own worst thoughts, where every mistake is amplified into a prophecy of failure.
series.series_description: A psychological shojo manga with elements of mystery, where a young illustrator hears literal echoes of her own self-critical thoughts. Junko, an art therapist, guides her to 'redraw' these internal narratives through collaborative art projects. The mystery lies in uncovering the origin of her first, foundational shame, learning to separate the echo from her true voice.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the shocking silence and then the clear, new sound of your own gentle voice speaking after years of internal criticism.
series.marketing_angle: Uses the investigative framework of a mystery to explore the roots of anxiety and shame. The art therapy angle provides a visually rich and active metaphor for healing.
series.main_character_role: freelance illustrator struggling with commissions

series.series_logline: For the reader who longs to be cared for but has built such high walls that kindness now feels like a threat to their independence.
series.series_description: A josei romance that subverts the genre, focusing on the protagonist's fear of intimacy rather than the pursuit of it. Junko, her neighbor and a retired gardener, teaches her about relationships through the metaphor of tending plants—some need space, some need support, all need consistent, gentle attention. The series is about unlearning the belief that needing others is a weakness.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the terrifying, tender ache of letting someone see the fragile part of you that you've hidden for years.
comp_titles: Wotakoi: Love Is Hard for Otaku
series.marketing_angle: A romance where the central conflict is internal, not external. It's for readers who find typical romance overwhelming and crave a story about earning trust in oneself first.
series.main_character_role: self-employed web developer who works alone

series.series_logline: For the reader who feels their partner's anxiety as their own, and needs a quiet space to find their own calm again.
series.series_description: A tender, slow-paced manga about a couple learning to co-regulate their nervous systems. After arguments, they practice simple, wordless rituals of breathing and touch to rebuild safety. This series offers a visual guide to emotional attunement for couples seeking repair.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the grounded, physical sensation of your own breath syncing with another, creating a silent anchor.
comp_titles: Wotakoi: Love Is Hard for Otaku
series.marketing_angle: A manga that visualizes the invisible emotional bridge between partners. Perfect for readers of 'Wotakoi' who crave a deeper, quieter look at relationship repair and nervous system healing.
series.main_character_role: a freelance illustrator prone to anxiety spirals

series.series_logline: For the reader who writes long, perfect arguments in their head but can never say them out loud.
series.series_description: A group of people meet weekly to read letters they've written to their partners—letters they will never send. Through this ritual, they learn to translate their internal monologues of blame and fear into clearer, kinder spoken words. A series about the gap between thought and communication.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the specific relief of giving a voice to your unsaid grievances, and the lightness of letting them go.
series.marketing_angle: For fans of the introspective, character-driven drama of 'Honey and Clover', this manga tackles the paralysis of overthinking in relationships and the path to vocal clarity.
series.main_character_role: a quiet archivist struggling to express needs

series.series_logline: For the reader who is grieving the relationship they thought they had, while trying to build a new one with the same person.
series.series_description: After a betrayal, a couple attempts reconciliation. The story is told through dual perspectives, contrasting the idealized 'phantom' relationship one partner mourns with the awkward, boundary-heavy reality they must now construct. A raw look at grief and rebuilding.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the aching hollow of a lost future, and the tentative, fragile hope of planting something new in its soil.
series.marketing_angle: A poignant and psychologically astute manga for readers who found truth in 'My Lesbian Experience With Loneliness'. It explores the complex grief of letting go of a relationship fantasy.
series.main_character_role: a therapist navigating her own relationship breakdown

series.series_logline: For the reader who needs a pre-agreed rulebook for fights, because in the heat of the moment, all their good intentions vanish.
series.series_description: A practical, almost procedural manga about a couple who drafts a 'Conflict Constitution'—a set of humorous but binding rules for arguments (e.g., 'No kitchen fights', 'Must hold a stuffed animal'). Each chapter tests a new rule under fire, teaching boundary enforcement through structure.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the secure, almost silly comfort of having a playbook for chaos, making repair feel possible and predictable.
comp_titles: Wotakoi: Love Is Hard for Otaku
series.marketing_angle: A unique, rule-based approach to relationship wellness. Appeals to fans of 'Wotakoi's' geeky couple dynamics, but with a focused, therapeutic twist on building safety systems.
series.main_character_role: a competitive gamer who applies game design to his relationship

series.series_logline: For the reader who is terrified their own anxiety is a self-fulfilling prophecy, pushing their partner away.
series.series_description: A psychological slice-of-life about a woman whose fear of abandonment manifests as constant, subtle testing of her partner's loyalty. The story visualizes her anxious thoughts as a literal, crumbling chamber around her, and her journey is learning to stop reinforcing its walls.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the claustrophobic grip of your own worst fears, and the staggering relief when you finally stop feeding them.
series.marketing_angle: Uses subtle magical realism to externalize internal anxiety, similar to the emotional weight of 'Fruits Basket'. A compelling hook for readers who want to see their intrusive thoughts depicted on the page.
series.main_character_role: a university student with relationship OCD

series.series_logline: For the reader who has always been the 'strong one' and is now learning, tremblingly, how to lean.
series.series_description: A romance between a stoic carpenter and a physically disabled gardener. The carpenter is used to fixing things, but must learn that support isn't about fixing his partner—it's about being a steady, present anchor. A story about redefining strength and receiving care.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the profound vulnerability and deep warmth of allowing yourself to be held, after a lifetime of holding everyone else.
series.marketing_angle: A disability-positive romance that focuses on emotional boundaries and interdependence. For readers who love the deep character connections of 'Chihayafuru' and seek a mature, grounded love story.
series.main_character_role: a carpenter who struggles to accept help

series.series_logline: For the reader who is obsessed with being the 'perfect partner,' and is burning out trying to perform a role.
series.series_description: A competitive office worker approaches her new marriage like a high-stakes project, optimizing for harmony. Her breaking point leads her to a coach who teaches her to differentiate between her true self and the 'partner persona' she's created. A story about the boundary of selfhood.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the dizzying disorientation—and then liberation—of realizing the person you've been performing for is not you.
comp_titles: Wotakoi: Love Is Hard for Otaku
series.marketing_angle: A sharp, modern take on performance anxiety in relationships, set against a corporate backdrop. Appeals to fans of the career-driven aspects of 'Nana' and the psychological depth of 'My Lesbian Experience With Loneliness'.
series.main_character_role: a high-achieving management consultant

series.series_logline: For the reader who believes connection is built in grand gestures, but is discovering it's woven in the small, silent repairs.
series.series_description: An anthology series. Each volume contains three self-contained stories about different couples. Each story focuses on a single, micro-moment of post-conflict repair: making tea, a glance, tidying a shared space. It highlights how boundaries and safety are built in mundane, wordless actions.
series.reader_promise: You will feel seen in the tiny, often overlooked acts of care that truly stitch a relationship back together.
comp_titles: Wotakoi: Love Is Hard for Otaku
series.marketing_angle: An accessible, episodic anthology perfect for sampling. Its focus on subtle, non-verbal communication will resonate with readers of the nuanced, everyday romance in 'Honey and Clover'.
series.main_character_role: varies per story (e.g., florist, night-shift worker, retired teacher)

series.series_logline: For the reader who feels their patience is a finite resource, drained by the constant touch and noise of caregiving.
series.series_description: A josei manga about a mother who discovers a forgotten, overgrown garden behind her apartment. The quiet, methodical work of restoring it—plant by plant, stone by stone—becomes her nightly ritual to reclaim her sense of self and tactile peace. The art is soft and detailed, focusing on textures of soil and leaves.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the grounding weight of soil in your palms and the quiet expansion of your own breath returning.
comp_titles: March Comes in Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: A tactile escape for the touched-out parent. This series visualizes the nervous system's reset through the quiet, repetitive art of gardening, offering a blueprint for finding pockets of solitude.
series.main_character_role: A mother and former potter rediscovering her hands through gardening

series.series_logline: For the reader who clocks out of their day job only to clock into the endless second shift of home and family.
series.series_description: A seinen workplace/slice-of-life manga about a salaryman father who secretly works a few nights a week at a 24-hour laundromat. The rhythmic hum of the machines and the transient, anonymous company of night owls provide a stark, peaceful contrast to his demanding roles at the office and at home.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the profound, wordless camaraderie of shared solitude and the bittersweet peace of a responsibility that ends when your shift does.
comp_titles: March Comes in Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: Re-defining 'me time' for the modern parent. This series explores the radical act of choosing a mundane, paid second job as a form of therapeutic boundary-setting and personal reclamation.
series.main_character_role: A corporate worker and father who takes a part-time night job at a laundromat

series.series_logline: For the reader who feels invisible in their own home, their needs constantly drowned out by the louder, more immediate needs of others.
series.series_description: A shojo supernatural-everyday manga about a young mother who discovers she can create small, harmless magical fires that only she can see. These 'signal fires'—a candle flame in an empty mug, a spark in her palm—become her private language for marking her emotional state and claiming tiny moments of visibility.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the gentle, defiant warmth of making your inner state tangible, even if you're the only one who can see it.
series.marketing_angle: A magical realist take on emotional labor. This series uses a subtle, beautiful supernatural metaphor to visualize a parent's internal world and the act of self-witnessing amidst chaos.
series.main_character_role: A new mother navigating isolation who manifests personal, visual emotional cues

series.series_logline: For the reader who fights a daily, invisible war against guilt every time they try to take time for themselves.
series.series_description: A shonen battle manga with a twist: the 'battles' are against metaphorical monsters of exhaustion, overcommitment, and guilt. A single father is recruited by a secret society of caregivers who defend their 'Sanctuary Time'—rigorously scheduled personal hours—from creatures that feed on their self-neglect.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the visceral thrill of violently defending your right to rest, and the validation that your time is worth fighting for.
comp_titles: March Comes in Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: Shonen action meets therapeutic boundary-setting. This series literalizes the internal struggle of prioritizing self-care as an epic, stylized battle, making the concept of 'guilt' a defeatable enemy.
series.main_character_role: A single dad and member of a secret order that battles entities of parental burnout

series.series_logline: For the reader who fears the beautiful, exhausting blur of early parenthood is erasing the person they used to be.
series.series_description: A josei slice-of-life manga about a archivist who becomes a mother. Overwhelmed, she begins a meticulous personal project: documenting one tiny, non-child-related pleasure each day in a detailed scrapbook—the smell of rain, a perfect sip of coffee. The archive becomes a map back to herself.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the slow, accumulating warmth of recognizing yourself again in a curated collection of quiet, personal moments.
comp_titles: March Comes in Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: A love letter to the self, documented. This series frames active self-remembering not as a luxury, but as a vital archival practice to combat the identity dissolution of new parenthood.
series.main_character_role: A new mother and professional archivist who creates a personal scrapbook of selfhood

series.series_logline: For the reader who is tired of the unspoken expectation that they will always be the emotionally absorbent one in the family.
series.series_description: A seinen mystery manga where a father, a therapist, notices his own compassion fatigue manifesting as a literal silence that falls over him at home. He begins to 'investigate' the source of this silence, tracing it through the unspoken dynamics and emotional labor patterns of his household, seeking a new 'truth' to restore his voice.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the chilling clarity of seeing invisible family dynamics mapped out, and the tense hope of disrupting them.
comp_titles: March Comes in Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: A domestic noir about emotional labor. This series uses the framework of a psychological mystery to deconstruct and interrogate the hidden contracts of care within a family, offering a path to renegotiation.
series.main_character_role: A therapist and father investigating the roots of his own emotional muteness at home

series.series_logline: For the reader who grieves the hobbies, dreams, and friendships that quietly disappeared into the background of parenthood.
series.series_description: A josei fantasy manga about a woman who discovers a hidden, dusty room in her own house that contains physical manifestations of her abandoned passions—a still-wet painting, a half-written song. To heal her fatigue, she must decide whether to carefully put these things to rest or find a way to reintegrate them.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the aching sweetness of properly mourning lost versions of yourself, and the quiet power that comes from choosing how to remember them.
series.marketing_angle: A fantasy of reckoning with the selves we leave behind. This series treats lost hobbies and dreams not as failures, but as artifacts to be respectfully curated, allowing for closure or rediscovery.
series.main_character_role: A mother who discovers a magical room in her home containing her abandoned creative projects

series.series_logline: For the reader who is their own harshest critic, constantly competing against an impossible internal standard of 'perfect' parenthood.
series.series_description: A shojo sports manga metaphor where a mother joins a local recreational volleyball league. Her true opponent isn't the other team, but a phantom 'perfect' version of herself on the other side of the net. Each game becomes a lesson in accepting her real, flawed, but capable human performance.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the exhilarating strain and eventual release of finally facing down your own unrealistic expectations and learning to play for joy, not perfection.
comp_titles: March Comes in Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: Sports manga as a metaphor for internal competition. This series externalizes the vicious cycle of parental self-critique into a literal game, teaching readers to spike their own guilt out of bounds.
series.main_character_role: A mother who joins a community volleyball team to battle her internalized ideal of a 'perfect parent'

series.series_logline: A young analyst must navigate the physical landscape of his own anxious mind — one chapter, one map, one thing he has never looked at directly.
series.series_description:
Protagonist (26, data analyst) begins seeing a psychiatrist who gives him an unusual
assignment: draw a map of your anxiety. Each chapter, he externalizes a different
anxious thought as a visual metaphor — a city with no exits, a room where the walls
move, a corridor that branches every time he chooses. Visualization-heavy. The
internal landscape becomes more real than the office he works in. Monthly Afternoon
seinen aesthetic: psychological depth, visual invention, no resolution delivered.
series.reader_promise: You will see the shape of your anxiety before it can hide.
comp_titles: 20th Century Boys (Naoki Urasawa) — visual metaphor scale
series.marketing_angle:
Visualization as genre. The internal CBT concept (externalizing anxious thoughts)
becomes the manga's visual vocabulary — each chapter is a different architectural
metaphor for a cognitive pattern. Polished seinen linework. Target: Monthly Afternoon
or ComicWalker. Comp: Planetes visual intelligence with therapy subject matter.
series.main_character_role: data analyst, 26, referred to psychiatry by his physician

series.series_title: 考えすぎてしまうあなたへ
series.series_logline: Dialogue-heavy therapy manga for the reader whose brain will not stop running scenarios.
series.series_description:
Protagonist overthinks everything. This series maps the mechanism with precision —
4-koma strips that demonstrate specific cognitive loops (pre-mortem rehearsal, second-
order consequence modeling, status-threat scanning) followed by brief essay interludes
from the perspective of a clinical psychologist who consults on the series. CBT-
informed but never clinical in register. Target: Pixiv Comics free chapters as
discovery, tankobon for library and purchase.
series.reader_promise: You will recognize the loop and finally watch it from outside.
comp_titles: Berserk (Kentaro Miura) — visual intensity for internal states, not topic
series.marketing_angle:
Hybrid 4-koma + explanatory essay — the overthinking recognition bank made visual.
Highly shareable individual strips. The CBT techniques embedded in narrative, not
explained. Pixiv as discovery platform for the male overthinking demographic.
series.main_character_role: product manager, 29, sees a therapist for the first time

series.series_logline: A young architect who cannot stop reviewing his work is not afraid of failure — he is afraid of being seen clearly.
series.series_description:
He is good at his work. The team respects him. He receives the commission. At night
he goes back to the drawings and finds everything wrong. This is not perfectionism
about the work — the series slowly makes clear it is the expectation of exposure.
Each chapter ends late, quietly. He does not spiral dramatically. He just stays too
long. Jump SQ or ComicWalker aesthetic: understated, evening-focused, calibrated
for the male imposter syndrome reader.
series.reader_promise: You will stop running from the flaw the perfectionist part of you is so sure is there.
series.marketing_angle:
Imposter syndrome in seinen register — not the female "I don't belong" story but
the male "I am about to be found out" story. Quiet. Evening-paced. The architectural
setting is visually rich. Polished seinen linework with near-architectural precision.
series.main_character_role: architect, 28, junior partner, never satisfied

series.series_logline: He spots every pattern in the market. He cannot stop spotting the same pattern in himself.
series.series_description:
A securities analyst realizes the catastrophic patterns he sees in financial data are mirrors
of his own cognitive loops. The series is a psychological thriller about overthinking, imposter
syndrome, and the difference between analysis and paranoia. The therapy content (cognitive
distortions, pattern-interruption, reality-testing) embedded entirely inside a financial
thriller. Comp: Liar Game's intellectual tension applied to internal psychological state.
series.reader_promise: The pattern you keep seeing is real. The question is whether it means what you think.
series.marketing_angle:
Psychological thriller for the seinen reader who uses analysis as a defense mechanism. The
overthinking content is the mystery mechanics — never announced. Positioned for
Monthly Afternoon.
series.main_character_role: securities analyst, pattern-recognition specialist

series.series_logline: He remembers everything. Every failure. Every word. Every version of himself he has failed to be.
series.series_description:
A man with perfect autobiographical memory cannot escape his past — every mistake,
embarrassment, and failure is perpetually accessible, as vivid as the present. Rumination,
overthinking, and self-critical loops embedded in a psychological horror shell. The horror
is not supernatural — it is the terror of complete self-awareness without self-compassion.
Comp: Evangelion's interior psychological collapse applied to a realistic protagonist.
series.reader_promise: He cannot forget. Neither can you. The series knows.
comp_titles: Neon Genesis Evangelion (Hideaki Anno) — interior collapse
series.marketing_angle:
Psychological horror for the seinen reader who recognizes the character immediately. The
rumination content IS the horror mechanic. Positioned for Monthly Afternoon or Jump SQ.
series.main_character_role: man with perfect autobiographical memory

series.series_logline: In 2031, your emotional state is indexed automatically. She cannot afford to let them see the score.
series.series_description:
Near-future Japan, 2031. Wearable devices automatically index and report employees' emotional
states to management. A woman in a high-performance finance role is burning out but cannot let
her Emotion Index drop without losing her position. Burnout, performance anxiety, and the cost
of hiding distress embedded in a cyberpunk surveillance thriller. The wellness content is the
plot engine — not a detour.
series.reader_promise: The system knows you are burning out. It just does not care.
comp_titles: Ghost in the Shell (Masamune Shirow) — corporate surveillance
Pluto (Naoki Urasawa) — psychological depth in genre
series.marketing_angle:
Cyberpunk / near-future thriller for the seinen reader who has hidden exhaustion at work.
The burnout content is the genre mechanic. Positioned for Monthly Afternoon or
Afternoon Season.
series.main_character_role: finance professional, 2031, Emotion Index subject

series.series_logline: He deleted the glitch. He deleted himself with it.
series.series_description:
A cyberpunk programmer had his ADHD neural patterns medicated away by a mandatory corporate
neuro-compliance program. His productivity scores are perfect. His creative breakthroughs have
stopped. The series is about his attempt to recover the cognitive patterns the system removed.
ADHD identity, self-trust, and the relationship between neurodivergence and creativity embedded
inside a cyberpunk identity thriller.
series.reader_promise: The glitch was always the feature.
comp_titles: Ghost in the Shell (Masamune Shirow) — identity in technology
series.marketing_angle:
Cyberpunk for the seinen reader who was told their brain was the problem. The ADHD content
is the genre mechanic — the "glitch" is both literal (code) and narrative (his cognition).
Positioned as a prestige cyberpunk series.
series.main_character_role: cyberpunk programmer, post-neuro-compliance subject

series.series_logline: She quit her startup after two years of not sleeping. The series is what happened after — the year she spent finding out who she was without the job.
series.series_description:
A 27-year-old IT worker in Seoul quits her startup after two years of burnout.
She expects to feel free. Instead she feels empty. The series follows the first
year of her life without work-as-identity — boredom, unexpected grief, the slow
return of something that might be herself. Aggretsuko meets soft webtoon slice-
of-life. Full color, vertical scroll. LINE Global / Naver distribution.
series.reader_promise: You will find out who you are when the job isn't defining you.
comp_titles: True Beauty (Yaongyi) — platform behavior, webtoon aesthetics
series.marketing_angle:
Korean Gen Z burnout recovery told from the inside — no moralizing, no productivity
advice, no resolution until it arrives honestly. Full-color webtoon format dominant
on Naver/LINE. The empty year is the story. Relatable in Korea, Taiwan, and for
global Asian diaspora.
series.main_character_role: IT worker, 27, quit her startup, currently adrift

series.series_logline: He is 30, a senior developer, and he just discovered he has a body.
series.series_description:
A senior developer (30, Korean, stereotypically desk-bound) has a health scare at
a routine check-up. Nothing serious. But his doctor says: you need to use your body.
He does not know what this means. The series follows his education in embodiment —
somatic therapy, stretching, basic yoga, learning to feel his own hunger — with
the self-aware humor of someone who has never thought about their body as a real
object. Somatic wellness through comedy. Platform: LINE Manga Korea.
series.reader_promise: You will laugh at how badly you have been ignoring your body and then feel something shift.
comp_titles: Cells at Work (Akane Shimizu) — body-as-subject, educational comedy
series.marketing_angle:
Somatic wellness via humor — the most accessible entry point for male audiences
who do not engage with "wellness" framing. The comedy makes it shareable. The
underlying somatic content is real and useful. Platform: LINE Manga Korea or
Kakao for the 28–35 male tech worker demographic.
series.main_character_role: senior developer, 30, just discovered he has a body

series.series_logline: A UX designer who cannot sleep tries one intervention per night and documents it. The series is designed to be read in the dark.
series.series_description:
She designs apps for a living. She cannot sleep. Each episode of this series is
one night: one intervention tried (no screens, white noise, breathing, early wake,
sleep restriction therapy), one result (inconclusive, mostly). The visual aesthetic
mimics phone interface aesthetics — dark background, muted teal and navy highlights,
notification-style chapter headers. It is meant to be read in bed in the dark on
a phone. The irony is intentional. Platform: LINE Manga global distribution /
Bilibili Comics for CN market crossover.
series.reader_promise: You will find the part of your sleep that the phone took.
comp_titles: Night is Short, Walk on Girl (Tomihiko Morimi) — night register, not topic
Sleep (Haruki Murakami short story) — protagonist register
series.marketing_angle:
Sleep anxiety content told from inside the tech worker's specific experience —
someone who makes the apps that are keeping her awake. Visual design mimics
dark-mode phone UI. Cross-platform appeal: KR + CN through LINE/Bilibili.
Extremely high mobile-first optimization.
series.main_character_role: UX designer, 28, product of the apps she builds

series.series_title: 仕事帰り、ひとりごはん
series.series_logline: For the working woman who eats alone and finds that enough.
series.series_description:
A woman in her late thirties moves through the week eating alone — different small
restaurants each night, no particular reason, no destination. Nothing happens. The
series accumulates these moments until they feel like a life fully inhabited.
Wakako Zake-adjacent. No dramatic arc. The food is present. She is present.
That's the whole series.
series.reader_promise: You will find quiet in the middle of the week, one meal at a time.
comp_titles: Yotsuba&! (Kiyohiko Azuma) — emotional register, not topic
series.marketing_angle:
Positioned for the josei adult market on LINE Manga. Near-wordless sequences.
Minimal ink fill. The cooking and eating are depicted with precise, quiet
attention. Visual comp: Yoru Neko (evening cat aesthetic) meets Shinya Shokudō.
Reader is not told how to feel. She just watches.
series.main_character_role: copywriter, late 30s, eats alone by choice

series.series_logline: A therapist's quiet office. A stray cat. A different person's gentle story each chapter.
series.series_description:
In a small countryside clinic, a therapist sees patients one by one. A stray cat
has taken up residence in the waiting room. Each chapter follows a different patient —
their anxiety, their grief, the thing they can't say out loud. The cat watches. The
sessions end. Nothing is resolved. Something shifts. Mental health awareness through
quiet narrative, not clinical explanation.
series.reader_promise: You will understand why the cat stays.
comp_titles: A Man and His Cat (Umi Sakurai)
series.marketing_angle:
Mental health manga without clinical framing — the therapeutic process rendered as
lived experience. ComicWalker (free-to-read) as discovery platform; tankobon collect
for library and purchase market. Comp: A Man and His Cat (warm, adult, quiet).
series.main_character_role: therapist in countryside clinic

series.series_logline: A tech worker rents a tiny plot outside Tokyo. Four seasons. No plot. Pure iyashikei.
series.series_description:
A man in his early thirties who works in tech rents a small plot of farmland an hour
outside Tokyo. He goes on weekends. He plants things. They grow or they don't. The
series follows the four seasons — soil, rain, the first shoots of spring, the quiet
of winter. No dramatic arc. His anxious work brain quiets in the field. He doesn't
know why. The series doesn't explain it. Laid-Back Camp adjacent for the male office
worker demographic.
series.reader_promise: You will remember there is ground under your feet.
series.marketing_angle:
Pure iyashikei for the seinen male burnout demographic. Extremely high silent panel
ratio (55%). Full seasonal arc gives the series natural structure without narrative
stakes. Pixiv Comics as primary — built-in male seinen iyashikei audience.
series.main_character_role: software engineer, early 30s, weekend farmer

series.series_logline: Each chapter: a different person who can't sleep and the quiet ritual that holds them.
series.series_description:
An anthology. Each chapter follows a different person unable to sleep — the night-shift
nurse, the student before exams, the new parent, the person who can't stop the replay.
Each has a ritual: tea, a slow walk, a notebook, the window. The series does not explain
insomnia or offer solutions. It maps the texture of sleepless nights with precision and
tenderness. For anyone who has been awake at 3am knowing they're not the only one.
series.reader_promise: You will feel less alone in the 3am quiet.
series.marketing_angle:
Sleep anxiety through anthology format — high reader identification, natural sharing
behavior ("this is me"). Near-zero ink density. Designed to be read at night on a dim
screen. LINE Manga as primary — strongest late-night reading demographic.
series.main_character_role: rotating — one person per chapter

series.series_logline: A direct therapy manga, co-created with a clinical psychologist, for the reader who has never believed the reassurance.
series.series_description:
A young woman has heard it her whole life — "you're fine, you're doing great" — and
never believed it. This series uses 4-koma panels interspersed with explanatory pages
(written in the voice of a psychologist) to trace the imposter schema: where it comes
from, what it protects, how it keeps running after the reason is gone. Not a workbook.
Not a lecture. A manga where someone finally maps the exact feeling. Direct comp:
"I'm Enough" (Hosokawa + Dr. Mizushima, Last Gasp).
series.reader_promise: You will realize the voice that says you're not enough has a name and a history.
comp_titles: I'm Enough (Tenten Hosokawa + Dr. Hiroko Mizushima)
My Lesbian Experience With Loneliness (Nagata Kabi)
series.marketing_angle:
The only manga in the stillness_press lineup with explicit clinical co-author framing.
4-koma hybrid + explanatory interludes — formats the insight for readers who want
understanding, not just feeling. Standalone tankobon format + LINE digital.
Comp credibility: positions as Japanese equivalent of "I'm Enough" for the domestic market.
series.main_character_role: office worker, late 20s, high achiever who cannot accept she is enough

series.series_logline: After burning out, she forgot how her body felt. This is the series about finding out.
series.series_description:
A woman in her early thirties collapses at work — not dramatically, just quietly,
on a Tuesday. She takes medical leave. She does not know what to do with the quiet.
The series follows her learning somatic practices she has never tried: breathing that
reaches the belly, walking without destination, sitting without a screen. Each chapter
is a practice. Her body responds before her mind understands why. Karoshi-adjacent
burnout recovery for the josei reader who has also been collapsing slowly.
series.reader_promise: You will find the part of your body that remembers stillness.
comp_titles: Yokohama Kaidashi Kikou (Hitoshi Ashinano) — somatic slowness
series.marketing_angle:
Somatic healing rendered as visual manga practice — breathing exercises, body scans,
sensory grounding depicted in slow, near-silent panels. Positions somatic recovery
as a Japanese cultural concept (体の声を聞く — listening to the body's voice) not
Western therapy import. LINE Manga + Nico Nico for digital.
series.main_character_role: marketing manager, early 30s, on medical leave after quiet collapse

series.series_logline: A woman burned out so completely she dissolved into the forest. The series is about returning.
series.series_description:
A woman burned out to the point of dissolution wanders a spirit forest as something between
human and nature. Each chapter she recovers one small physical sensation — cold water, rough
bark, the weight of sleep. The wellness content (somatic healing, body reoccupation after
burnout) travels entirely inside a dark fantasy shell. No clinical language. No therapist
character. The forest is the therapist.
series.reader_promise: You will feel yourself returning to your body, one chapter at a time.
series.marketing_angle:
Positioned as dark fantasy / supernatural healing manga for josei adult market. Comp: Mushishi
emotional register meets Berserk visual weight. The psychological content (somatic healing,
burnout recovery) is never named — readers process it as "a story about a woman returning
to herself."
series.main_character_role: burnout survivor, between human and forest spirit

series.series_logline: After losing everyone, she rides. The dragon only flies when she stops pretending she is fine.
series.series_description:
A female knight who has lost her entire unit rides an ashen dragon across a devastated
landscape. The dragon's ability to fly correlates directly with her emotional state — it
grounds when she dissociates, surges when she grieves fully. The series is about grief
processing and somatic reconnection, embedded entirely inside a dark fantasy adventure.
No therapist. No clinical frame. Comp: Frieren's emotional register with heavier visual weight.
series.reader_promise: The dragon knows what you lost. It has been waiting for you to know too.
series.marketing_angle:
Dark fantasy for the josei reader who loved Frieren and wanted something heavier. The grief
content is the story — not a detour. Positioned as a prestige josei series for LINE Manga.
series.main_character_role: female knight, sole survivor, ashgrey dragon rider

series.series_logline: She can see what everyone is afraid of. It looks like insects. She cannot turn it off.
series.series_description:
An office worker develops the ability to see other people's anxiety as physical insects
swarming around them. The series follows her trying to function while perceiving constant
ambient fear. Social anxiety, sensory overwhelm, and the exhaustion of hypervigilance
embedded entirely in a psychological horror shell. The horror is the anxiety — not a
