metaphor for it, the literal experience made visible.
series.reader_promise: You are not imagining it. The series just makes it visible.
series.marketing_angle:
Psychological horror for readers who found Mob Psycho 100 but wanted a female protagonist
and a more interior emotional register. The anxiety content is the genre mechanic, not
a message.
series.main_character_role: office worker, involuntary anxiety-perceiver

series.series_logline: Every night resets to 3am. Each reset is a different reason she cannot stop.
series.series_description:
A woman moves into a house where time loops at 3am. Each chapter is a different iteration
of the same sleepless night — different year of her life, different anxiety driving the
wakefulness, different quiet ritual she tries. Sleep anxiety embedded in a supernatural
horror loop structure. The house is not malevolent — it's a mirror. The loop only breaks
when she understands what she's been avoiding.
series.reader_promise: 3am again. This chapter knows.
series.marketing_angle:
Supernatural horror / quiet dread for the reader who is already awake at 3am. The sleep
content is the loop mechanic, not announced as wellness. Positioned for the horror-adjacent
josei reader.
series.main_character_role: woman, new tenant, caught in the 3am loop

series.series_title: 燃え尽きた勇者の転生
series.series_logline: He died of overwork. He woke up in a world where the only currency is rest.
series.series_description:
A salaryman who died of karoshi is reincarnated in an isekai world where productivity is
forbidden and rest is the only skill that matters. To level up, he must genuinely slow
down — the system detects and penalizes urgency. Burnout recovery embedded entirely in
isekai game-mechanic shell. The protagonist must unlearn overwork to survive. Comp:
Spy x Family's light action-comedy register applied to burnout content.
series.reader_promise: In this world, rest is the only power that matters.
comp_titles: That Time I Got Reincarnated as a Slime (Fuse) — isekai template
series.marketing_angle:
Recovery isekai for the reader who wants the genre pleasure of leveling up while the
content rewires their relationship to productivity. Positioned as isekai / workplace
comedy for LINE Manga josei and seinen overlap audience.
series.main_character_role: karoshi salaryman, reincarnated protagonist

series.series_logline: For when the anxiety hasn't stopped and you still need somewhere quiet to be.
series.series_description:
An adult woman in her late twenties moves through ordinary days carrying something she can't
quite name — the tight chest, the shoulder that won't drop, the 3am wakefulness that has no
reason. "What the Body Holds" doesn't explain her anxiety or resolve it. Chapter by chapter,
through long wordless sequences, unhurried morning light, and the small rituals of a life
still being lived, it sits with her exactly where she is. Readers finish each chapter quieter
than when they arrived — not because anything has been solved, but because someone finally
understood what it actually feels like.
series.reader_promise: You will find your way back to stillness even when the alarm is still ringing.
comp_titles: My Lesbian Experience With Loneliness (Nagata Kabi)
series.marketing_angle:
Most healing content explains anxiety back to readers who already live it. This series doesn't.
Near-wordless pages (45% silent panels), near-zero ink fill, and earth-tone watercolor space
create a visual grammar the body responds to before the brain does — the pacing IS the
restoration. Distinctive in a crowded wellness-manga market because silence is the product,
not the wrapper.
hook_lines: Your body already knows what you're carrying.
series.main_character_role: copywriter between contracts

series.series_logline: For the long nights when your mind won't stop and your body just needs permission to rest.
series.series_description:
Yuki is thirty-one and hasn't slept well in two years. Not insomnia — just the mind that
won't go quiet, the replay of the day's conversations, the invisible weight that makes sleep
feel like something she has to earn. "The Night Before You Sleep" follows her through the
slow hours of late evening: the lamp she turns on, the tea she makes, the window she sits
beside. Nothing dramatic happens. That is the point. Long wordless sequences and near-empty
ink pages are designed for the exact moment a reader picks up her phone instead of sleeping —
and offers her something that works with her nervous system rather than against it.
series.reader_promise: You will feel your body start to let go, even before the night is over.
series.marketing_angle:
Positioned at the precise reading moment: late night, phone in hand, sleep not coming.
52% silent panels and the lowest ink-fill in the stillness_press catalog mean each page is
visually restful by design. Unlike sleep-hygiene content, this series doesn't instruct —
it demonstrates, chapter by chapter, what it feels like when the body starts to slow down.
hook_lines: Read this instead of your notifications.
The quiet is already here. You just need company.
series.main_character_role: accounts manager with a 6am alarm she keeps missing

series.series_logline: For when your body knows something your words can't reach yet.
series.series_description:
Mei is twenty-eight and has spent a long time living from the neck up. The stomach that
tightens before she speaks, the jaw she unclenches every evening — her body has always been
talking; she just didn't know how to listen. "Hands, Shoulders, Breath" follows her through
the slow relearning: a yoga class she almost leaves, a walk where she notices her own footfall,
mornings where she places a hand on her chest before getting up. Each chapter is built around
one somatic moment — one instance of body-awareness arriving before language does. The visual
grammar does the somatic work: panels pause on hands, on the texture of a floor, on breath
visible in cold air. Readers finish feeling more at home in their own skin.
series.reader_promise: You will remember that your body is not the enemy — it is the way through.
comp_titles: A Gentle Noble's Vacation Recommendation (Misaki Momochi)
series.marketing_angle:
Somatic healing is one of the fastest-growing wellness search categories, but almost no manga
addresses it directly. This series makes body-awareness practice visible through iyashikei
visual grammar — panels that linger on physical sensation rather than explaining it.
The 48% silent-panel ratio creates space for the reader's own somatic experience to surface
alongside the character's. Fills a clear gap between clinical somatic therapy content and
the cozy-healing manga category.
hook_lines: Your body has been trying to tell you something.
Healing that starts below the shoulders.
series.main_character_role: UX researcher who lives in spreadsheets and forgets she has a body

series.series_logline: For the reader who counts heartbeats instead of sheep, whose mind races when the world goes quiet.
series.series_description: A young office worker with chronic insomnia meets Master Sha, who teaches her to use her own breath as a timekeeper for rest. Through gentle somatic exercises and reframing nighttime as a space for restoration rather than performance, she learns to quiet the internal alarm. A tender, methodical journey into the body's natural rhythms.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the grounding weight of your own breath, turning panic into a predictable, gentle tide.
series.marketing_angle: A somatic answer to sleep anxiety, framing the breath not as a technique to master, but as a faithful companion through the night. Perfect for readers who found solace in the quiet observations of 'Mushishi'.
series.main_character_role: overworked graphic designer with severe sleep-onset anxiety

series.series_logline: For the reader who jolts awake at 3 AM convinced they've forgotten something catastrophic, their body buzzing with false urgency.
series.series_description: A freelance writer is haunted by a pervasive sense of impending doom that only strikes at night. Master Sha guides him to investigate these 'phantom alarms' as somatic messages, not psychological failures. Together, they map the tension in his body to unresolved daily anxieties, learning to discharge them before bed. A mystery of the nervous system.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the profound relief of disarming an internal alarm, transforming dread into curious, manageable sensation.
series.marketing_angle: Treats nighttime anxiety as a detective story where the body holds all the clues. Appeals to fans of psychological depth and the eerie, calm investigation found in 'Mushishi'.
series.main_character_role: freelance journalist plagued by adrenaline surges at night

series.series_logline: For the reader who feels like a ghost in their own life, moving through days in a fog of exhaustion and dissociation.
series.series_description: A university student suffering from severe sleep maintenance insomnia and daytime dissociation is invited by Master Sha to a small, secret group—people who are learning to 'inhabit' their bodies again. Through shared, gentle somatic practices at dusk, they reclaim presence. A story about the longing for connection, starting with the connection to oneself.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the warm, gradual return of color and texture to a world that had gone flat and distant.
series.marketing_angle: A gentle, found-family narrative that frames healing as a communal act. For readers who love the heartfelt character connections of 'A Silent Voice' and the cozy group dynamics of 'Laid-Back Camp'.
series.main_character_role: dissociated university student with chronic fatigue

series.series_logline: For the reader whose anxiety feels like being untethered, floating in a void of what-ifs as night falls.
series.series_description: A young nurse, physically and emotionally drained from her shift work, cannot feel 'grounded' enough to sleep. Master Sha teaches her weighted, somatic techniques—a 'gravity blanket' of intentional muscle engagement and pressure—to simulate the safety of being held. A practical, tactile guide to creating somatic anchors.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the deep, cellular sigh of a body finally allowed to be heavy, safe, and still.
series.marketing_angle: Focuses on proprioceptive and deep-pressure techniques for self-soothing. Market as a tangible, physical toolkit for anxiety, similar to the direct, heartfelt approach of 'My Lesbian Experience With Loneliness'.
series.main_character_role: emotionally overwhelmed night-shift nurse

series.series_logline: For the reader who fears sleep because it means confronting chaotic, stressful dreams that feel more real than waking life.
series.series_description: An archivist whose sleep is invaded by vivid, anxious dreams seeks out Master Sha. He teaches her to curate her 'dream library'—using daytime somatic markers to gently influence the subconscious narrative. It's not about control, but about introducing elements of safety and wonder. A journey into the frontier where body memory and dreams meet.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the awe of discovering your mind is not an enemy, but a vast, impressionable library waiting for a gentle librarian.
series.marketing_angle: Positions dream work as a somatic practice. Appeals to the mystical yet grounded sensibility of 'Mushishi', offering a structured yet imaginative approach to night-time consciousness.
series.main_character_role: museum archivist tormented by hyper-realistic anxiety dreams

series.series_logline: For the reader whose sleep schedule is shattered, who lives in a permanent jet lag of their own making, chasing a rhythm they can't find.
series.series_description: A barista with a wildly inverted sleep cycle runs a 24-hour café, serving others while neglecting her own rhythm. Master Sha, a regular, begins teaching her to align with light, food, and movement—not as rigid rules, but as loving signals to her body. A story about harmonizing with the day's quiet music.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the gentle, satisfying click of falling into step with the sun and moon, becoming part of a larger, peaceful rhythm.
series.marketing_angle: Frames circadian health as a graceful, achievable alignment with natural cycles. The cozy café setting and focus on small daily rituals will attract fans of 'Laid-Back Camp' and the comforting slice-of-life in 'Chi's Sweet Home'.
series.main_character_role: nocturnal barista with a completely desynchronized sleep-wake cycle

series.series_logline: For the reader whose insomnia is a silent vigil, who stays awake because sleeping feels like abandoning a memory or a person.
series.series_description: A man who lost his partner finds he can only sleep in brief, fractured moments. Master Sha helps him understand this wakefulness as a form of somatic loyalty, a body keeping watch. Through rituals that honor the grief *in* the body, they slowly transform the vigil into a space where rest and memory can coexist. A tender exploration of embodied loss.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the permission to finally rest, not by letting go, but by learning how to carry your love in a way that allows you to sleep.
series.marketing_angle: Addresses the profound link between unresolved grief and sleep disruption with immense sensitivity. For readers who appreciated the emotional depth and handling of trauma in 'A Silent Voice'.
series.main_character_role: widower whose insomnia is intertwined with mourning

series.series_logline: For the reader who For the reader overstimulated by modern life, whose brain cannot find the 'off' switch because every sense feels dialed to eleven.
series.series_description: A sound engineer with hypersensitivity and burnout comes to Master Sha desperate for silence. Instead, Sha teaches her to orchestrate her sensory input—using specific sounds, textures, and temperatures not to block the world out, but to guide her nervous system down, note by note. A somatic practice of becoming the conductor of your own sensory experience.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the overwhelming static in your nerves resolve into a clear, single tone you can finally follow into sleep.
series.marketing_angle: Treats sensory overload as a practical problem with a somatic solution. The focus on active, almost technical engagement with one's own body will appeal to readers who like methodical processes, similar to the detailed, observant tone of 'Mushishi'.
series.main_character_role: hypersensitive audio engineer experiencing sensory burnout

series.series_logline: For the reader who feels the cold, hollow space where their passion used to burn, and fears the fire is gone for good.
series.series_description: A josei manga about a young architect, Sora, who has lost all creative drive after a project collapse. She encounters Ra, a retired master carpenter, who teaches her to see the beauty and potential in the 'ash' of her burnout—the quiet, foundational materials left behind. It's a gentle, restorative story about rebuilding from the ground up, not with a blaze, but with a steady, sustainable glow.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the warmth of a small, steady flame being carefully rekindled deep within your chest.
series.marketing_angle: A restorative josei manga that reframes creative burnout not as an ending, but as the essential clearing of ground needed to build something truly lasting and personal.
series.main_character_role: burnt-out architect

series.series_logline: For the reader who feels pulled into a deep, silent well by the weight of their own thoughts, unable to climb towards the light.
series.series_description: A seinen manga following Kenji, a salaryman whose depression manifests as a literal, crushing gravity field around him. Ra, a former physicist turned park groundskeeper, becomes his unlikely mentor. Through simple observations of nature—seeds pushing through soil, trees bending in wind—Ra teaches Kenji that the force holding him down can also become a source of stability and rootedness from which to grow.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the profound, aching pull of wanting to surface, paired with the first fragile sense of solid ground beneath you.
series.marketing_angle: A metaphorical and visually striking seinen story that personifies depression as a physical force, offering a unique, science-tinged path to understanding its weight and potential.
series.main_character_role: depressed salaryman

series.series_logline: For the reader who has forgotten how to be brave, and now finds even opening the curtains in the morning to be a monumental act.
series.series_description: A shojo slice-of-life manga about Hana, a high school student who has become agoraphobic after a traumatic event. Her new neighbor, Ra, is a gentle florist who delivers a single, unique flower to her doorstep each day with a simple note about the flower's 'courage'. Through this quiet, persistent ritual, Hana learns that courage isn't a grand feat, but the daily choice to face a world that feels too bright and too loud.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the fragile, blooming sensation of a tiny, personal bravery taking root and unfurling inside you.
series.marketing_angle: A tender shojo story that redefines courage through the lens of botany and daily ritual, perfect for readers who feel overwhelmed by the expectation of grand heroic acts.
series.main_character_role: agoraphobic high school student

series.series_logline: For the reader who For the reader staring at a crossroads in the dark, paralyzed by the fear that every possible choice leads to another kind of exhaustion.
series.series_description: A josei workplace manga about Akira, a mid-career professional who quit her toxic job and now wanders without direction. She meets Ra, a guide for night hikers, who teaches her to navigate not by artificial light or distant goals, but by the subtle, natural markers of the present moment—the feel of the path, the sound of water, the constellations. It's a story about finding your way when the map has burned.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the quiet awe of discovering that the path itself, even in darkness, holds all the guidance you need.
series.marketing_angle: A unique workplace-adjacent josei manga that uses the metaphor of wilderness navigation to address career burnout and decision paralysis, offering a pathfinder's wisdom.
series.main_character_role: directionless former office worker

series.series_logline: For the reader who tends the ashy soil of their past failures with a mix of grief and stubborn, secret hope.
series.series_description: A seinen healing manga about botanist Ren, whose life's work—a rare flower greenhouse—burned down. Consumed by loss, he is visited by Ra, an elderly gardener who specializes in 'fire-follower' plants that only bloom after a blaze. Together, they cultivate a new garden in the scorched earth, learning that the most fertile ground for growth is often what remains after everything seems lost.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the deep, cathartic release of tears watering soil you thought was dead, and the shock of the first green shoot.
series.marketing_angle: A poignant seinen title that merges botany with emotional recovery, visually contrasting the beauty of regenerative flora with the starkness of personal ruin.
series.main_character_role: grieving botanist

series.series_logline: For the reader who watches others chase their dreams with a dull envy, while their own aspirations feel like distant, cold stars.
series.series_description: A shonen sports-adjacent manga about Kaito, a former competitive swimmer who quit due to pressure and now works as a lifeguard, merely watching the water. Ra, the quiet pool maintenance man, teaches him not about winning races, but about the physics of buoyancy, the warmth of sun on water, and the simple aspiration to float before you swim. It's about rediscovering the love of the medium, not the medal.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the old, forgotten thrill of wanting something just for yourself, rising like a bubble in your chest.
series.marketing_angle: A shonen series that subverts sports manga tropes, focusing on the sensory joy and personal reclamation of an activity after competitive burnout.
series.main_character_role: former swimmer turned lifeguard

series.series_logline: For the reader who is desperately, angrily fanning a single remaining ember, terrified that this one, too, will turn to ash.
series.series_description: A seinen thriller-toned manga about a journalist, Makoto, whose obsessive pursuit of a corruption story has cost him his health and relationships. On the verge of collapse, he is found by Ra, a archivist who preserves dying folk tales. Ra teaches him that a story—or a life—doesn't need a blazing finale to have meaning; sometimes, preserving the last, true spark is the most radical act of all.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the intense, protective fury of guarding your last precious light against a world that wants to snuff it out.
series.marketing_angle: A tense, psychologically driven seinen story that frames burnout as a thriller—a race against time to save the protagonist's last shred of passion from total extinction.
series.main_character_role: obsessive, burnt-out investigative journalist

series.series_logline: For the reader who practices smiling in the mirror, trying to remember the muscle memory of joy before facing another day.
series.series_description: A josei romance-adjacent manga about Yumi, a wedding planner who orchestrates happiness for others while feeling utterly hollow inside. Her new client, an elderly man named Ra planning a vow renewal for his late wife, becomes her mentor. He teaches her that courage is not the absence of fear, but the daily rehearsal of hope—the deliberate, sometimes awkward, practice of choosing light, moment by moment, until it becomes real again.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the shuddering, full-body release of finally letting a real, un-practiced smile break through the performance.
comp_titles: Wotakoi: Love Is Hard for Otaku
series.marketing_angle: A josei story set in the world of weddings that explores the profound courage behind 'faking it till you make it,' and the healing power of ritual.
series.main_character_role: disillusioned wedding planner

series.series_logline: For the reader who feels their body is a faulty alarm system, constantly ringing with no clear source.
series.series_description: A high-achieving architect, whose world narrows to panic in boardrooms, learns from Pamela Fellows to trace anxiety to specific muscle tensions and unmet needs. A seinen series blending technical somatic diagrams with quiet, restorative moments of release.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the grounding weight of your own breath as it slowly disarms the internal siren.
comp_titles: March Comes in Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: A polyvagal theory manga for the corporate perfectionist. Teaches somatic 'interrupt' techniques for high-stakes environments through the story of an architect rebuilding her nervous system.
series.main_character_role: architect facing performance collapse during critical presentations

series.series_logline: For the reader who carries old injuries and emotional aches in their posture, long after the cause is gone.
series.series_description: A former athlete turned office worker is guided by Pamela Fellows to reconnect with the 'ghost' pains in his shoulder—a somatic map of past pressure and present neglect. A shonen journey of physical re-education and emotional thawing.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the profound release of a long-held tension you'd stopped noticing, like a forgotten knot finally undone.
series.marketing_angle: Manga as physiotherapy for the soul. Follows a relatable everyman using somatic tracking to heal the disconnect between his athletic past and sedentary present.
series.main_character_role: former high school baseball star now in a desk job

series.series_logline: For the reader who For the caregiver who has given so much their own body feels like an empty, echoing vessel.
series.series_description: A hospice nurse, numb and exhausted, learns from Pamela Fellows to identify the somatic signs of compassion fatigue—the hollow chest, the heavy limbs—and practices micro-restorations to refill her own cup. A josei series about sustainable care.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the gentle warmth of permission to tend to your own vessel, as essential as tending to others.
comp_titles: March Comes in Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: Addresses the global burnout crisis in caregiving professions. Offers a somatic roadmap for recognizing and resetting the nervous system's 'over-give' mode, framed within a poignant human story.
series.main_character_role: hospice nurse experiencing profound emotional and physical depletion

series.series_logline: For the reader who For the perfectionist who holds the shape of every past criticism in their clenched jaw and tight shoulders.
series.series_description: A young violinist plagued by performance anxiety and physical pain discovers, with Pamela Fellows, that her technique is flawless—but her body holds the memory of every harsh teacher. A shojo story about unlearning somatic fear.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the quiet ache and subsequent relief of a muscle finally remembering it can be soft.
comp_titles: March Comes in Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: A unique blend of music manga and somatic therapy. Visualizes the 'body memory' of criticism and guides readers through exercises to release it, perfect for academic and artistic overachievers.
series.main_character_role: conservatory violin student striving for impossible perfection

series.series_logline: For the reader who For the person whose anxiety manifests as mysterious, unexplained pains that doctors can't find a cause for.
series.series_description: A freelance writer's chronic neck pain leads her to Pamela Fellows, who teaches her to become a 'detective' of her own sensations, tracing the pain back to forgotten moments of stress. A seinen mystery of the inner landscape.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the thrilling click of connecting a physical symptom to its emotional origin, solving the mystery of your own body.
series.marketing_angle: Frames somatic healing as an intriguing detective story. Readers learn to 'interview' their bodily sensations alongside the protagonist, making internal work feel active and engaging.
series.main_character_role: investigative journalist plagued by psychosomatic pain

series.series_logline: For the reader who For the over-scheduled achiever who has forgotten how to feel truly rested, mistaking stillness for laziness.
series.series_description: A startup founder on the brink of collapse is taught by Pamela Fellows that rest is not passive, but an active somatic practice with its own rhythms and requirements. A shonen series redefining strength through recovery.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the novel satisfaction of mastering rest as a skill, and the vitality that comes from a nervous system in rhythm.
comp_titles: March Comes in Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: Turns the hustle culture narrative on its head. Presents strategic, somatic-based rest as the ultimate performance hack for young professionals and entrepreneurs.
series.main_character_role: burned-out tech startup CEO

series.series_logline: For the reader who For the person who realizes they've been subtly holding their breath for years, waiting for the next shoe to drop.
series.series_description: A new mother, overwhelmed by constant vigilance for her baby's safety, learns with Pamela Fellows that her anxiety lives in a perpetually caught breath. A josei story about somatic repatterning and finding safety in the exhale.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the deep, shuddering relief of a full, unguarded exhale after a lifetime of bracing.
series.marketing_angle: Speaks directly to parental anxiety and hyper-vigilance. Offers gentle, illustrated breath and body awareness exercises integrated into a relatable narrative about early motherhood.
series.main_character_role: first-time mother experiencing intense postpartum vigilance

series.series_logline: For the reader who For the crisis worker or first responder whose body has absorbed the trauma of others, leaving them heavy and numb.
series.series_description: A disaster relief worker, paralyzed by a sense of helplessness, seeks guidance from Pamela Fellows. She learns to somatically process vicarious trauma—the weight in the hands that helped, the ache in the eyes that saw too much. A seinen anthology on collective healing.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the solemn, necessary release of grief that isn't yours to carry, but was left in your care.
comp_titles: March Comes in Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: Tackles the somatic impact of vicarious trauma in aid workers, journalists, and therapists. Provides a narrative framework for a crucial but often overlooked aspect of compassion fatigue.
series.main_character_role: disaster relief coordinator burdened by witnessed suffering

series.series_logline: For the reader who measures their worth by their productivity, and needs to learn stillness is not a failure.
series.series_description: A burnt-out salarywoman discovers a tiny, forgotten shrine behind her apartment. The gentle god who lives there teaches her that her value isn't tied to her output, through quiet rituals and the simple act of being present. A seinen series about unlearning hustle culture.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the deep, cellular relief of permission to stop, and the quiet joy of being enough exactly as you are.
series.marketing_angle: For fans of 'Barakamon' seeking urban tranquility. This series reframes spiritual practice as micro-moments of presence, perfect for the overworked professional's commute or morning ritual.
series.main_character_role: Exhausted marketing manager

series.series_logline: For the reader who says yes to everything, and is secretly terrified of what will happen if they ever say no.
series.series_description: A people-pleasing artist can suddenly see the 'energy cords' that form when she over-gives. To reclaim her power and creativity, she must learn the delicate, terrifying art of cutting them. A josei magical realism story about energetic boundaries and self-preservation.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the visceral, empowering snap of a boundary firmly set, and the spacious calm that floods in afterwards.
series.marketing_angle: A visually stunning metaphor for burnout and consent. Appeals to readers of 'My Lesbian Experience With Loneliness' who crave narratives about reclaiming agency through a supernatural lens.
series.main_character_role: Freelance illustrator overwhelmed by client demands

series.series_logline: For the reader who feels unworthy of grace, and secretly longs to create something beautiful just for themselves.
series.series_description: A young man who believes he is fundamentally broken joins a community pottery studio. As he learns to center clay on the wheel, he begins to center himself, discovering his inherent worth is not conditional on being fixed. A shonen slice-of-life about craft as healing.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the focused determination of mastering a craft, and the profound surprise of finding self-respect in the mud and the mess.
series.marketing_angle: The therapeutic focus of 'Blue Period' meets the communal warmth of 'Barakamon'. Targets young men with a gentle, non-preachy entry into mindfulness through tangible, creative action.
series.main_character_role: University dropout struggling with self-doubt

series.series_logline: For the reader who feels their kindness is a weakness, and is afraid to build walls for fear of being alone.
series.series_description: A florist with an empathic gift absorbs the emotions of everyone around her, leaving her perpetually drained. An enigmatic landscape architect teaches her to design 'emotional gardens'—beautiful, living boundaries that filter energy without isolating her heart. A shojo romance with a supernatural twist.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the gentle safety of a sanctuary you built yourself, and the warm flutter of connection that respects your limits.
series.marketing_angle: A unique blend of romantic shojo and wellness philosophy. Perfect for readers who love the emotional depth of 'Fruits Basket' but crave a contemporary, practical application of setting healthy limits.
series.main_character_role: Empathic florist shop owner

series.series_logline: For the reader who carries every past mistake like a stone in their pocket, and doesn't know how to put them down.
series.series_description: A grieving hiker meets a mysterious guide on a pilgrimage trail. Each night, they perform a simple ritual: selecting a 'stone' of regret, speaking its truth to the mountain wind, and leaving it behind. A seinen journey about the spiritual practice of forgiveness, starting with oneself.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the aching release of a long-held burden, and the light, uncertain freedom of walking forward without it.
series.marketing_angle: A contemplative, travel-based narrative for the spiritually curious. Appeals to the same audience as introspective, journey-focused stories, offering a structured, ritualistic approach to processing regret.
series.main_character_role: Man on a solitary hiking pilgrimage after a personal loss

series.series_logline: For the reader who craves a sacred connection but finds traditional meditation impossible, and needs a practice that fits a chaotic life.
series.series_description: A single parent and nurse is too exhausted for cushions or chanting. She discovers the magic of 'micro-rituals'—infusing presence into making coffee, washing hands, or tucking in her child. An anthology-style josei series where each chapter is a standalone lesson in finding the divine in the mundane.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the spark of recognition as ordinary moments are gently illuminated, revealing the extraordinary peace hidden within your daily routine.
series.marketing_angle: An accessible, non-dogmatic anthology for time-poor seekers. Markets as 'spiritual snacks'—perfect 5-minute reads that transform daily chores into grounding practices, ideal for the bedside or commute.
series.main_character_role: Overworked nurse and single mother

series.series_logline: For the reader whose inner critic speaks in a shout, and who must learn to hear the softer, truer voice beneath the noise.
series.series_description: A reclusive calligrapher with severe social anxiety is commissioned to transcribe ancient spiritual poetry. Each character she inks begins to glow if her intention—her sense of self-worth—is pure. To complete the work, she must confront the cacophony in her mind and find her own authentic voice. A seinen character study.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the quiet, resonant hum of alignment when a thought finally matches your deepest truth, and the courage to let that truth guide your hand.
series.marketing_angle: A visually poetic and introspective series. Connects with fans of 'Komi Can't Communicate' who are ready for a more mature, spiritually-inflected exploration of social anxiety and self-expression through art.
series.main_character_role: Reclusive master calligrapher

series.series_logline: For the reader who believes they must heal alone, and is terrified of the vulnerability required to sit quietly with another person.
series.series_description: Two strangers—one grieving, one numb—accidentally sign up for the same silent meditation retreat. Forced into proximity without words, they begin to communicate through shared glances, small gestures, and the simple, profound act of witnessing each other's pain. A josei romance built on quiet understanding.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the profound intimacy of a connection that doesn't need to be explained, and the healing warmth of being seen in your silence.
series.marketing_angle: A slow-burn romance where the conflict is internal and the chemistry is built through presence, not dialogue. Targets readers of 'Honey and Clover' who appreciate nuanced, emotionally mature relationships born from shared vulnerability.
series.main_character_role: Architect on leave after a bereavement

series.series_logline: For the reader whose nervous system has been running at red-alert for so long they can no longer remember what calm feels like.
series.series_description: A slice-of-life seinen manga following a port logistics manager whose body is quietly shutting down from years of chronic stress. When a workplace accident forces a medical leave, he begins learning — through a somatic therapist and a harbor maintenance crew — how to read his body's distress signals before they become emergencies. The series draws on maritime metaphor: tides, anchor lines, and safe harbor.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the particular relief of a nervous system that finally, slowly, learns it is allowed to rest.
series.marketing_angle: A rare manga for men navigating burnout recovery — practical, grounded, and never preachy. Uses body-based metaphor to make somatic regulation feel like common sense, not therapy-speak.
series.main_character_role: port logistics manager on forced medical leave

series.series_logline: For the reader who has hit the wall — not dramatically, but quietly, when they realized they had nothing left for anyone, including themselves.
series.series_description: A josei manga about a mid-career social worker who collapses internally without anyone noticing. The story charts her slow, non-linear recovery through practical containment strategies: boundaries, scheduled depletion checks, and the radical act of asking for help. The 'still water' is not peaceful — it is the surface tension before a break, and learning to recognize it.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the exact moment when 'I can't do this anymore' becomes 'I am learning what I actually can do' — and that it is enough.
series.marketing_angle: For burned-out helpers and caregivers who have forgotten they are also people. No inspirational arcs — just honest, incremental recovery drawn with warmth.
series.main_character_role: social worker, 12 years in child welfare

series.series_logline: For the reader who knows all the right things to do for anxiety but whose body refuses to cooperate.
series.series_description: An educational-narrative seinen manga that follows a science journalist researching the vagus nerve after his own panic disorder sidelines him. The story weaves accessible neuroscience with his personal recovery journey, making somatic regulation feel like discovery rather than prescription. Each chapter ends with a visual 'tide chart' mapping the nervous system state explored.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the quiet triumph of understanding your own body well enough to work with it instead of fighting it.
series.marketing_angle: The manga for people who want to understand WHY the breathwork helps — grounded in real neuroscience, told through a compelling personal story.
series.main_character_role: science journalist, panic disorder, research-mode recovery

series.series_logline: For the reader who has rebuilt themselves after collapse and is terrified of going that low again.
series.series_description: A seinen manga about a former stockbroker who had a complete breakdown five years ago and has quietly rebuilt a minimal, grounded life. The story is a quiet maintenance manual — exploring how he keeps himself stable, what early warning signs he watches for, and what happens when his old world tries to pull him back. Recovery is not the plot — it is the setting.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the deep, unglamorous satisfaction of someone who has chosen stability over performance — and means it.
series.marketing_angle: For readers in recovery who are tired of comeback narratives. This is about the quiet discipline of staying well.
series.main_character_role: former trader, now runs a small pottery workshop

series.series_logline: For the reader who functions fine on the outside while quietly drowning in a body that will not stop bracing for impact.
series.series_description: A josei manga about a high-functioning anxiety sufferer — a school librarian who appears effortlessly calm to everyone around her. The story follows her covert survival strategies and the slow realization that managing anxiety and healing from it are completely different things. A somatic therapist introduces her to grounding practices that feel counterintuitive and then, gradually, essential.
series.reader_promise: You will feel seen in the exhaustion of being 'fine' — and then something gentler: the possibility that you do not have to be.
series.marketing_angle: For the high-functioning anxious reader who feels invisible in mental health narratives. Detailed, accurate somatic content with a quiet visual language.
series.main_character_role: school librarian, high-functioning anxiety

series.series_logline: For the reader whose anxiety spikes in winter — shorter days, heavier body, fewer reasons to leave the house.
series.series_description: A seasonal seinen manga set across one winter in Hokkaido, following a fisherman dealing with seasonal depression compounding chronic anxiety. The story uses the rhythms of winter fishing — waiting, cold, sudden action, stillness — as a structural metaphor for nervous system regulation. A taciturn mentor figure embodies regulated presence without ever using therapeutic language.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the specific, wintry comfort of knowing that stillness and dormancy are not the same as failure.
comp_titles: Golden Kamuy (tone reference only — landscape, not violence)
series.marketing_angle: A quiet winter manga about seasonal mental health for readers who find summer wellness content alienating. Visually sparse, emotionally rich.
series.main_character_role: fisherman, Hokkaido, seasonal depression

series.series_logline: For the reader who collapses every evening and cannot figure out why the morning feels impossible.
series.series_description: A short-form josei manga structured around the 6–8pm 'crash window' — the hour when the nervous system drops its performance mask. Follows a working mother who starts keeping an 'evening log' with her pharmacist neighbor, tracking patterns in her exhaustion. The story is episodic and gentle, each chapter covering one evening ritual and what it reveals.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the unexpected intimacy of understanding your own rhythms — and the permission to build your life around them.
series.marketing_angle: Short-form, episodic format perfect for readers who are too tired to commit to a long arc. Each chapter is a complete evening moment.
series.main_character_role: working mother, evening burnout pattern

series.series_logline: For the reader who grew up in chaos and cannot believe in their bones that the current calm is real.
series.series_description: A seinen manga about a man in his 30s from a high-conflict family who has built a stable life but cannot stop waiting for it to collapse. The story follows his work with a somatic therapist learning to send his body 'safe signals' — that this, right here, is not a threat. The central tension is between his cognitive knowledge of safety and his body's refusal to believe it.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the fragile, precious work of teaching a frightened body that the war is over — and the moment, eventually, when it begins to believe you.
series.marketing_angle: For adults from high-conflict homes who have 'done the work' intellectually but whose body hasn't caught up. Rare in manga — addresses developmental trauma without pathologizing.
series.main_character_role: accountant, stable present life, hypervigilant body

series.series_logline: For the reader who knows their anxiety is a false alarm but cannot make their body stop screaming.
series.series_description: A teacher-student josei manga in which Ahjan, a somatic therapist, guides Mio — a school nurse who handles everyone's crises — through the process of learning to read her own body's alarm signals. Each chapter focuses on one sensation, one trigger pattern, one small shift. The art maps internal states visually, showing how the nervous system's 'alarm' softens when it is met with understanding rather than suppression.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the precise moment when your body stops being the enemy and starts being the first place you look for information.
series.marketing_angle: The teacher-student format makes somatic concepts feel like direct mentorship. For readers who want guidance, not just insight — Ahjan's presence is calm, skilled, and never clinical.
series.main_character_role: school nurse, habitual crisis responder, own needs unmet

series.series_logline: For the reader who burned out while helping others heal and cannot figure out how to apply any of it to themselves.
series.series_description: A josei manga following a burnout recovery coach who is secretly in burnout herself. When Ahjan — her former teacher — re-enters her life for a weekend retreat, the story becomes about the particular shame of the helper who cannot help themselves. The arc moves from crisis to containment to a new, honest relationship with her own capacity.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the complicated relief of someone who has finally stopped performing their own recovery and started actually recovering.
series.marketing_angle: For therapists, coaches, and helpers in burnout — a niche but deeply loyal audience who recognize themselves immediately. Ahjan's mentorship is warm and non-shaming.
series.main_character_role: burnout recovery coach, secretly in burnout

series.series_logline: For the reader whose body speaks to them in sensations they have never learned to read.
series.series_description: An anthology-style josei manga where each chapter is a 'letter' from a different part of the body — the throat that tightens before a difficult conversation, the chest that aches after loss, the legs that go heavy under dread. Ahjan appears briefly in each chapter as a guide, helping the protagonist — and reader — decode the somatic vocabulary they were never taught.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the extraordinary intimacy of finally understanding what your own body has been trying to tell you for years.
series.marketing_angle: Anthology format for readers who prefer emotional depth over plot. Each chapter is self-contained and shareable. Ideal for somatic therapy practitioners to recommend to clients.
series.main_character_role: illustrator learning somatic language

series.series_logline: For the reader who gives and gives until there is nothing left, then feels guilty for even noticing.
series.series_description: A teacher-student seinen manga in which Ahjan walks a young environmental researcher through the concept of natural limits — using the forest edge, where trees grow thin and the terrain changes, as a sustained metaphor for healthy boundaries. The student, Sota, must learn that his limits are ecological features, not moral failures.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the grounded, almost physical sensation of understanding that your limits are not a deficiency — they are the shape of you.
series.marketing_angle: Nature-based metaphor for boundary work — rare in therapeutic manga. Appeals to readers who find psychology language alienating but respond to biological or ecological framing.
series.main_character_role: environmental researcher, chronic over-giver

series.series_logline: For the reader who has been told their anxiety is 'all in their head' and needs someone to finally explain why that is wrong.
series.series_description: A josei manga blending personal narrative with accessible neuroscience. The protagonist, a young data analyst, works with Ahjan to map her anxiety responses — literally, with diagrams and body charts — discovering that her 'irrational' reactions are perfectly logical responses to stored somatic patterns. The visual language of data and mapping makes the science feel empowering rather than clinical.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the profound vindication of realizing that what your body does makes complete sense — and the relief of finally having a map.
series.marketing_angle: For analytical readers who want to understand their anxiety, not just manage it. The data-visualization aesthetic is distinctive and shareable on social media.
series.main_character_role: data analyst, analytical approach to her own anxiety

series.series_logline: For the reader who is curious about body-based healing but has no idea where to start or what it actually means in practice.
series.series_description: A gentle educational manga following a skeptical journalist who is assigned to write about somatic therapy and ends up becoming a participant. Ahjan is her guide — patient, precise, never dismissive of her skepticism. The series documents her first year of somatic practice, including the sessions that didn't work and the ones that changed her relationship with her own body.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the intellectual pleasure of learning something genuinely new about your own body — and the surprise of caring about it.
series.marketing_angle: Entry-point manga for readers new to somatic work. The journalist-skeptic frame validates reader doubt and makes exploration feel safe rather than committed.
series.main_character_role: lifestyle journalist, skeptic-turned-participant

series.series_logline: For the reader who holds their breath without realizing it — in meetings, in relationships, in their own skin.
series.series_description: A seinen manga about a translator who works across three languages and has unconsciously learned to hold his breath under cognitive load. When a health scare flags chronic oxygen deprivation, Ahjan introduces him to a breathwork practice that becomes the lens through which he examines every relationship in his life. The breath becomes a metaphor for presence, permission, and self-trust.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the extraordinary release of a body that has finally been given permission to exhale — and the life that opens up around that permission.
series.marketing_angle: Breath as narrative engine — a novel structural choice that makes familiar territory feel fresh. For readers drawn to quiet, contemplative manga with psychological depth.
series.main_character_role: simultaneous interpreter, chronic breath-holder

series.series_logline: For the reader who is somewhere between 'I'm fine' and 'I'm falling apart' and cannot get a clear signal from either direction.
series.series_description: A josei manga about a woman in her late 30s navigating perimenopause while also processing childhood emotional neglect — two nervous system disruptions arriving together. Ahjan appears as a somatic therapist who helps her separate the physiological from the historical, giving her language and tools for both. The story is honest about the complexity and refuses easy resolution.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the strange comfort of complexity being named — and the permission to be exactly as messy and layered as you actually are.
series.marketing_angle: Rare representation of perimenopause and developmental trauma overlapping — an underserved audience with intense loyalty. Ahjan's somatic lens makes both accessible.
series.main_character_role: museum curator, late 30s, perimenopause + childhood neglect

series.series_logline: For the reader who pushed through burnout like it was a weakness and is now wondering why they feel nothing.
series.series_description: A seinen manga about a competitive programmer who maxed out his capacity and won — and then found the victory felt like ash. The story follows his encounter with a martial arts trainer who practices Stoic philosophy not as performance but as a daily discipline. The arc moves from numbness to clarity to a rebuilt sense of purpose that doesn't require collapse to motivate it.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the specific, hard-edged satisfaction of a mind that has been retempered — not softened, but made actually strong.
series.marketing_angle: For high-achieving young men who are burned out but don't have the language for it. Stoic framing makes emotional regulation feel like discipline, not therapy.
series.main_character_role: competitive programmer, post-victory numbness

series.series_logline: For the reader whose anxiety shows up as rage, restlessness, or the inability to stop moving — not as fear.
series.series_description: A seinen manga about a young construction site manager whose anxiety presents as aggression and hypercontrol. When a workplace conflict escalates, he is required to attend anger management — and meets a Stoic-practitioner counselor who reframes anxiety as misdirected vigilance rather than weakness. The series uses the forge metaphor: heat without form destroys, but the same heat with discipline creates.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the clarity of understanding that what you called strength was actually fear wearing armor — and that real strength looks different.
series.marketing_angle: For men whose anxiety doesn't look like anxiety — it looks like anger, control, or restlessness. Stoic framing destigmatizes the exploration.
series.main_character_role: construction site manager, anxiety-as-aggression

series.series_logline: For the reader who has optimized everything except the part where they stop.
series.series_description: A seinen manga about an elite athletic trainer who treats rest as a productivity variable but has never actually rested. A sports medicine doctor introduces him to the Stoic distinction between control and non-control — and to the idea that rest is not the absence of effort but its own form of discipline. The story is structured around training cycles, with rest as the climax of each arc.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the counterintuitive strength of a person who has learned that stopping deliberately is harder — and more powerful — than never stopping at all.
series.marketing_angle: Reframes rest as elite performance behavior. Ideal for high-performance readers who find traditional wellness content too soft. Sports setting broadens appeal.
series.main_character_role: elite athletic trainer, rest-avoidant

series.series_logline: For the reader who avoids thinking about death and notices it makes everything feel vaguely hollow.
series.series_description: A seinen manga following a young ER doctor who encounters mortality daily but has never applied Stoic death-contemplation to his own life. A senior mentor — old-school, pragmatic, deeply serene — introduces him to the daily Stoic practice of memento mori not as morbidity but as the practice that makes the present matter. The story alternates between high-stakes emergency scenes and quiet philosophical conversations.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the unusual, clarifying weight of taking your own death seriously — and the strange freedom that comes with it.
series.marketing_angle: Medical drama meets Stoic philosophy. The mortality confrontation that many readers avoid becomes the series' central gift — handled without sentimentality.
series.main_character_role: ER doctor, death-adjacent daily but personally avoidant

series.series_logline: For the reader who is still fighting something that already happened and losing every round.
series.series_description: A seinen manga about a former Olympic-qualifier sprinter who was injured in a car accident and has been in covert war with his fate for three years. A sports psychologist trained in Stoic methods introduces him to amor fati — the love of fate — not as resignation but as the only form of aggression that actually works against the unchangeable. The arc is about converting fury into fuel.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the extraordinary, almost violent release of finally stopping the fight you cannot win — and the surge of energy that redirects into the one you can.
series.marketing_angle: Sports recovery frame for readers who find acceptance-language too passive. Stoic amor fati reframes surrender as tactical — the strongest move available.
series.main_character_role: injured former sprinter, three-year resistance to fate

series.series_logline: For the reader whose anxiety about future disasters is eating the present — and who wants a framework that actually works.
series.series_description: A seinen manga about a risk analyst whose professional skill (modeling worst-case scenarios) has colonized his personal life. A Stoic-practitioner mentor teaches him the ancient practice of premeditatio malorum — deliberate negative visualization — not to amplify worry but to defuse it. The story explores how controlled exposure to imagined disaster reduces the body's chronic threat response.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the counterintuitive calm of a mind that has deliberately visited its worst fears — and discovered they are survivable.
series.marketing_angle: Stoic anxiety management for analytical, skeptical readers who want a cognitive framework, not emotional processing. The professional risk analyst frame makes the philosophy feel practical.
series.main_character_role: professional risk analyst, personally catastrophizing

series.series_logline: For the reader who wants to be unshakeable but keeps discovering he is not.
series.series_description: A seinen manga following a junior officer in a disaster response unit whose composure shatters during a mass casualty event. His senior — a Marcus Aurelius devotee who has seen worse — begins a rigorous, unsentimental mentorship in building what the Stoics called the inner citadel: the part of the mind that cannot be taken. The series does not minimize trauma — it takes it seriously enough to demand a real response.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the hard, clean satisfaction of a character building real psychological strength — not performance, but the actual thing.
series.marketing_angle: Military/emergency setting for readers who find most mental health content too soft. Stoic framework provides a rigorous, masculinity-compatible path to resilience.
series.main_character_role: disaster response officer, composure shattered by mass casualty event

series.series_logline: For the reader who needs every situation resolved and is exhausted by how often life refuses to cooperate.
series.series_description: A seinen manga about a game designer whose creative output requires tolerating ambiguity but whose personal life is locked in a constant, exhausting demand for resolution. A Stoic mentor introduces him to Keats's concept of negative capability — the capacity to remain in uncertainty without irritable reaching after fact or reason — as a Stoic practice. The story explores how creative genius and psychological stability are related skills.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the unexpected creative freedom of a mind that has learned to stay in the question — and the calm that follows.
series.marketing_angle: For creative professionals with anxiety. Stoic + creative framing makes emotional regulation feel like craft development, not therapy. Distinctive concept in the genre.
series.main_character_role: game designer, resolution-dependent, ambiguity-intolerant

series.series_logline: For the reader whose past has made the present feel like an unsafe place to exist in their own body.
series.series_description: A josei manga about a social work student who begins therapy and discovers that her 'anxiety' is complex trauma — the nervous system responding to historical danger, not present threat. The story follows her EMDR and somatic work with a trauma-trained therapist, charting the nonlinear, fragmented process of trauma integration. The art uses fragmented panel structures that reassemble as healing progresses.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the specific, almost cellular relief of a body that is beginning — slowly, unevenly — to believe that the danger is over.
series.marketing_angle: One of the few manga to depict EMDR and somatic trauma therapy with clinical accuracy and emotional honesty. For readers in trauma recovery who want to see their process reflected.
series.main_character_role: social work student, complex trauma, beginning therapy

series.series_logline: For the reader whose depression feels less like sadness and more like a crushing, senseless gravity they cannot explain to anyone.
series.series_description: A seinen manga about a freelance translator who has functioned through depression for so long he cannot remember what life without it feels like. His therapist specializes in depression as somatic phenomenon — stored grief, frozen energy, the body's version of giving up. The series uses the metaphor of translation throughout: learning to interpret the body's depression language, one phrase at a time.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the strange, underwater recognition of someone who has finally been accurately described — and the tentative hope that description carries.
series.marketing_angle: Depression as somatic phenomenon, not just mood disorder. For readers exhausted by motivational recovery narratives. The translator metaphor makes the psychological work feel like skilled craft.
series.main_character_role: freelance translator, long-term depression, somatic approach

series.series_logline: For the reader who can see their own pain clearly but cannot stop re-experiencing it from inside it.
series.series_description: A josei manga about a documentary filmmaker who covers humanitarian crises and has accumulated vicarious trauma she has never processed. An IFS (Internal Family Systems) therapist helps her learn to be an internal witness — observing her traumatized parts without fusing with them. The manga's visual language alternates between first-person immersion and third-person observation, mirroring the therapeutic shift.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the extraordinary relief of learning to watch your own pain from a safe distance — and discovering that distance, far from being coldness, is what finally makes healing possible.
series.marketing_angle: IFS therapy visualized through documentary filmmaker lens — distinctive and intellectually engaging. The observer-participant dynamic gives the story philosophical depth.
series.main_character_role: documentary filmmaker, vicarious trauma accumulation

series.series_logline: For the reader who lost years to survival mode and is now grieving time they cannot get back.
series.series_description: A josei manga about a woman returning to university at 34 after a decade spent in a controlling relationship and its aftermath. The story is not about the abusive relationship — it is about the next chapter, the strange grief of lost potential, and the slow rebuilding of a self that was systematically dismantled. Her therapist specializes in post-traumatic growth without toxic positivity.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the bittersweet, forward-moving grief of someone who has stopped waiting to get the lost years back — and started building with what remains.
series.marketing_angle: Survivor narrative that starts after the crisis ends — rare and deeply needed. For readers tired of trauma stories that climax at escape. The grief of lost time is central and honoured.
series.main_character_role: 34-year-old returning student, DV survivor, rebuilding phase

series.series_logline: For the reader whose triggers arrive without warning and leave them exhausted, ashamed, and confused about who they are.
series.series_description: A josei manga about a primary school teacher who prides herself on patience but is terrified of her own trauma responses. She begins somatic therapy after a classroom incident triggers a dissociative episode. The story documents her learning to recognize the window of tolerance, map her triggers, and develop a regulated nervous system rather than a suppressed one.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the deep, practical relief of a nervous system that finally has a map — and the growing confidence of someone learning to navigate their own interior.
series.marketing_angle: Window of tolerance framework visualized in manga for the first time. For readers who work in helping professions and have never learned to apply the tools to themselves.
series.main_character_role: primary school teacher, trauma responses emerging in professional setting

series.series_logline: For the reader who is in therapy but finds the week between sessions the longest, hardest part.
series.series_description: An anthology josei manga structured around the days between therapy appointments. Each chapter is one 'between-sessions week' — the homework, the setbacks, the small breakthroughs, the moment when something the therapist said finally lands. The protagonist changes each arc, but the therapist's voice and somatic tools remain consistent throughout. A companion piece to the therapy experience.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the particular, valuable loneliness of therapeutic work being done — and the company of knowing someone else has sat in exactly that feeling.
series.marketing_angle: The manga FOR people in therapy, not about whether to go. Fills a genuine gap: the between-sessions experience is underrepresented and deeply relatable.
series.main_character_role: various — all in active trauma therapy

series.series_logline: For the reader who recognizes their parent's trauma in their own responses and does not know how to stop the chain.
series.series_description: A seinen manga about a 40-year-old man who begins therapy when his teenage daughter's anxiety mirrors his own too precisely. The work becomes epigenetic: tracing not just his own trauma but his father's — and the patterns passed without words, in behavior, in nervous systems synchronized to threat. A family systems therapist helps him be the generation that breaks the chain.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the enormous, grief-tinged dignity of being the person who decides: it stops with me — and the work required to mean it.
series.marketing_angle: Intergenerational trauma for men who discovered their own through their children. Rare framing that brings fathers into therapeutic manga narratives.
series.main_character_role: 40-year-old father, intergenerational trauma recognition

series.series_logline: For the reader who has processed the obvious trauma and is now confronted by the quieter damage underneath.
series.series_description: A josei manga about a woman who completed three years of trauma therapy for childhood abuse, believed she was healed, and then discovered a second, quieter layer — the grief of neglect, the absence of attunement, the wounds with no named incident. Her therapist introduces the concept of 'developmental trauma' and they begin again, more slowly. The tidal flat is the landscape of what is revealed when the obvious water recedes.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the quiet shock of recognizing a wound you did not know you had — and the grace of finding it is also treatable.
series.marketing_angle: For readers who have 'done therapy' and still feel something is missing. Developmental trauma is underrepresented in manga. This fills a gap for the therapy-literate audience.
series.main_character_role: office manager, post-abuse therapy, developmental trauma emerging

series.series_logline: For the reader who wants to be calm under pressure but freezes, panics, or explodes instead — and cannot figure out why discipline alone doesn't fix it.
series.series_description: A teacher-student shonen manga in which Master Wu — a martial arts master who treats emotional regulation as a combat discipline — trains Riku, a young firefighter who falls apart under real crisis despite perfect drills. Iron Gate explores the difference between controlled training environments and genuine threat response, teaching nervous system regulation through physical practice. The title refers to the gate between trained composure and lived composure.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the hard, precise satisfaction of a body and mind that have finally been trained together — not just one or the other.
series.marketing_angle: Martial arts framing makes nervous system regulation accessible to readers who find wellness content unmasculine. Master Wu's teaching is rigorous, demanding, and deeply effective.
series.main_character_role: young firefighter, trained composure / real crisis dissociation gap

series.series_logline: For the reader who has driven themselves to excellence and arrived at something that looks like burnout but feels like defeat.
series.series_description: A seinen manga about a kendo champion who retires at peak performance — not from injury but from a profound exhaustion he cannot name. Master Wu, now in his 70s, takes him as an informal student during his recovery period. The teaching is about rebuilding competitive fire on a foundation that doesn't require burning yourself down to sustain it. Ash and steel: what remains when the unnecessary has been consumed.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the specific, hard-won quiet of a competitor who has learned the difference between fighting from hunger and fighting from fullness — and chosen the latter.
series.marketing_angle: Post-career burnout for elite performers. Master Wu's approach reframes recovery as warrior discipline — the hardest training, not the absence of it.
series.main_character_role: retired kendo champion, performance burnout

series.series_logline: For the reader who knows what needs to be done and cannot make themselves begin.
series.series_description: A shonen manga about a college student with severe initiation anxiety — the paralysis before beginning any significant action. Master Wu teaches him the martial concept of the first strike: not aggression, but the discipline of the deliberate beginning. The story explores courage not as the absence of fear but as the trained capacity to act despite it, using physical practice as the laboratory.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the electric, almost physical release of a body that has finally learned to begin — and the discovery that the beginning was the hardest part.
series.marketing_angle: Procrastination and initiation anxiety reframed as martial training problems. Master Wu's first-strike teaching is visceral and immediately applicable. Strong social media excerpt potential.
series.main_character_role: college student, severe initiation paralysis

series.series_logline: For the reader who is always reacting and longs for the pause before the reaction — the place where choice lives.
series.series_description: A seinen manga about a high-frequency trader whose job is pure reaction velocity. When a bad trade causes significant harm, he seeks out Master Wu's dojo not for fighting skills but for what he has heard described as 'the still point' — the fraction of a second between stimulus and response that can be trained to expand. The series is about converting reactive intelligence into responsive intelligence.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the extraordinary, almost luxurious sensation of discovering that the space between stimulus and response is real, trainable, and yours.
series.marketing_angle: Mindful response for finance/high-performance readers who reject mindfulness language. Master Wu's martial framing makes the still point feel like a tactical skill, not a spiritual one.
series.main_character_role: high-frequency trader, reactive intelligence needing recalibration

series.series_logline: For the reader who is strong enough to carry everything but has never learned how to put it down.
series.series_description: A seinen manga about a veteran police detective who has carried every case, every death, every unsolved loss for twenty years without knowing he was carrying them. Master Wu — his neighbor, a retired martial artist — begins teaching him about what the Japanese call 'mushin': the empty mind that is full presence. The series is about the discipline of release, which turns out to be harder than the discipline of endurance.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the counter-intuitive strength of a person learning to put things down — and discovering that empty hands can hold more than full ones.
series.marketing_angle: For strong, stoic men in high-stakes professions who have never been offered a language for release. Master Wu's mushin teaching gives them one without compromising their identity.
series.main_character_role: veteran detective, 20 years of carried weight

series.series_logline: For the reader who confuses giving up with letting go and cannot find a way to stop fighting what cannot be changed.
series.series_description: A seinen manga about a corporate lawyer fighting a case he knows he will lose but cannot stop fighting. Master Wu teaches him the martial concept of yielding — the judo principle that the hardest throw uses the opponent's energy, not yours. The story explores how strategic surrender is not defeat but a form of mastery unavailable to those who only know force.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the unexpected power of a person who has learned to yield — and the realization that what they let go of was never theirs to control.
series.marketing_angle: Legal/corporate setting. 'Letting go' reframed as strategic mastery through martial arts lens. For readers who need permission to stop fighting something and want that permission to feel like strength.
series.main_character_role: corporate lawyer, losing case, cannot stop fighting

series.series_logline: For the reader who wants to be doing the meaningful thing but keeps choosing the urgent one instead.
series.series_description: A shonen manga about a young surgeon who is brilliant in emergency situations but cannot make himself focus on the long, slow, unglamorous work of research that will matter most over a career. Master Wu teaches him kata — the long forms of martial arts — as a philosophy of sustained commitment: that mastery is built in the unremarkable repetitions, not the crisis moments. The title is both literal and metaphorical.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the quiet, building power of a person who has finally chosen the important over the urgent — and the calm that comes with that alignment.
series.marketing_angle: Urgency-addiction for high-achieving young readers. Master Wu's kata teaching reframes slow, sustained effort as the elite discipline. Medical setting broadens appeal.
series.main_character_role: young surgeon, brilliant under crisis, avoids sustained focus

series.series_logline: For the reader who has mastered watching others but cannot bear to turn that same attention on themselves.
series.series_description: A seinen manga about a behavioral analyst who profiles others with uncanny accuracy but has built an elaborate blind spot around his own patterns. Master Wu recognizes him immediately and offers a specific teaching: the martial arts concept of zanshin — remaining awareness that continues after a technique is complete. The story is about applying to the self the same unflinching attention one gives to others.
series.reader_promise: You will feel the vertiginous, ultimately freeing experience of finally being seen — including by yourself — with the same clarity you have always given others.
series.marketing_angle: For analytical and intellectually-dominant readers who use cognitive skill as emotional avoidance. Master Wu's zanshin teaching makes self-observation feel like a martial skill.
series.main_character_role: behavioral analyst, expert at others, blind to himself

series.series_logline: A Gen Z 7-Eleven clerk sees the small fluorescent creatures riding each anxious customer's shoulder; she stocks the shelf that fits.
series.series_description: Mio works the graveyard shift at the only 7-Eleven open in her neighborhood. The fluorescent lights buzz on a frequency she's stopped hearing. The first customer of the night is a paralegal who can't sleep, and Mio can see the small flickering thing perched on his shoulder. She rings up his decaf, his lavender candy, and slips him a folded square of paper with one sentence on it — the thing to say to himself when the panic comes back. The creature stays. He leaves. Each night brings a new customer and a new creature: rent-doom, dissertation-spiral, the ex's new partner. Mio doesn't fight them. She finds the shelf-item and the pocket-ritual that lets the customer go home carrying it a little better. A24-quiet. Ghibli-warm. Convenience-store-fluorescent.
series.reader_promise: Your anxiety has a shape and a clerk who knows your shelf.
series.audience: Gen Z 23–27 first-job urban knowledge workers with named-and-claimed anxiety
series.marketing_angle: If Convenience Store Woman had a wellness ghost story inside it — and the ghost was kind.
series.long_arc_spine: Each anxious customer who walks in at 3 a.m. carries a small fluorescent creature only the clerk can see; she stocks the right product AND the right pocket-ritual, and slowly learns whose creatures these were before they were anyone's.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: First customer: the paralegal whose creature is the size of a moth and won't stop flapping. Decaf. Lavender. A sentence about the body coming home.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The art student whose creature is invisible until she opens her phone — comparison-anxiety as a small mirrored thing.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The night-shift nurse whose creature is going dark from being held too long. Mio learns she can stock for grief-fatigue too.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The first repeat customer. The paralegal returns. His creature is smaller. Mio sees one perched on her own shoulder for the first time.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The manager-on-call review. Mio is told she's too slow on inventory. The store's own creature — corporate-burnout-yokai — is named. Mio decides to stay.

series.series_logline: A university student inherits a small leather notebook that writes down what the nearest person is afraid of — and slowly learns to give the fear back.
series.series_description: When Iris's grandmother dies she inherits a notebook the size of a deck of cards and a single instruction: keep it close, never read it on the bus. The first time she opens it on a bus she sees her seatmate's panic written in her grandmother's handwriting — a girl who is failing her chemistry exam tomorrow. Iris cannot stop the writing, but she can answer it. Each volume she learns one small practice — a breath, a 5-4-3-2-1 grounding, a re-frame, a permission — that, when said aloud, lets the page erase itself and the fear leave the seatmate's body. The ledger fills, then empties, then fills. The mystery: the ledger has a final page that has been blank Iris's whole life. Whose name is going to appear on it? Slow-burn campus iyashikei. Pomeranian roommate. A library that closes at midnight.
series.reader_promise: Every fear has a practice. The practice fits in a notebook.
series.audience: Gen Z 18–22 university students with mental-health vocabulary and a dead grandmother
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
