series.marketing_angle: A24-college slow-burn meets Frieren's grief-permission via a tiny inherited notebook.
series.long_arc_spine: A pocket ledger inherited from a dead grandmother shows the anxious thought of any person it touches; each volume the protagonist learns one practice to give the thought back.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The girl on the bus. Chemistry exam. The breath. The page erases.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The bus driver. A father-of-three terrified of being laid off. A different practice. The ledger fills, then empties.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Iris's roommate, Pomeranian-loud. The ledger writes itself without being opened. RSD made visible.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A stranger Iris cannot place. The fear is grief — for someone Iris doesn't know yet.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The grandmother's handwriting changes. The first hint that the ledger has been writing back.

series.series_title: The Roommate Who Can See My Anxiety
series.series_logline: Asha moves in with a stranger from a Discord listing and discovers the stranger is a witch-of-the-mundane who can see the small grey creature Asha has lived with since age fourteen.
series.series_description: Asha is a 24-year-old non-binary illustrator who has been managing the small grey creature since high school. The creature follows them everywhere — into showers, onto buses, into bed. They have never told anyone. The new roommate, Nour, a queer Lebanese-American forensic accountant who knits, looks up from the cereal box on day three and says, casually, 'Hi, little friend.' Each volume Nour introduces Asha to one more friend whose creature she can also see — a transmasc barista with an octopus on his back, a deaf grad student with a tiny crow in her hair, a polyamorous polyglot with three small companions. The roster grows. The creatures soften. The long arc: who taught Nour to see them, and why is Nour's own creature the largest of all? Queerness, neurodivergence, and intergenerational trauma are baseline; the case-of-the-week is not coming-out but coming-into.
series.reader_promise: Your anxiety is real, it has a shape, and your people see it and stay.
series.audience: Gen Z 22–28 queer and neurodivergent urban creatives, mental-health-literate
comp_titles: I Think Our Son Is Gay
series.marketing_angle: Heartstopper's chosen-family register applied to clinical-adjacent Gen Z anxiety, with a witch-of-the-mundane lead.
series.long_arc_spine: A queer 24-year-old's anxiety manifests as a small house-yokai only her new roommate can see; the roommate is a witch-of-the-mundane who introduces the protagonist to a slowly-growing roster of similarly-yokai-burdened friends.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Move-in day. The cereal box conversation. Asha cries in the bathroom in relief, not shame.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The transmasc barista. The octopus on his back is named. Coffee shop becomes a third-place.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The deaf grad student. The crow in her hair speaks ASL. The roster is four.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Asha's family Zoom call. The creature triples in size on screen. Nour stays in frame.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Nour's own creature, glimpsed once at 2 a.m. in the kitchen, taller than Nour. The mystery opens.

series.series_logline: Yui Ito had her last panic attack on the F train and discovered a station that isn't on any map — and that she can come back to it whenever someone else needs it.
series.series_description: Yui is 26 and had her first panic attack in college, her worst at 23, her last on the F train at 2:11 a.m. She got off one stop further than her usual exit and the station was wrong: tiled in a color she had never seen, lit by a moon-shaped bulb, manned by a clerk who handed her a paper cup of warm barley tea and said, 'You can come back here whenever.' She did not believe it. The next time someone on her line had a visible panic attack she rode past her stop and the moon-station opened. Each volume she finds a new in-between station on a new line in a new city — the F, the Yamanote, the London Northern, the Taipei Brown, the LA Metro Gold. Each station is staffed by someone who once stepped off there. Each volume she helps one person step off. The long arc is who built the line, and what the last station looks like.
series.reader_promise: The worst night had a door, and you can come back through it for someone else.
series.audience: Gen Z 24–29 transit-city first-job knowledge workers
series.marketing_angle: Spirited Away on the subway, for the generation that names panic out loud.
series.long_arc_spine: A 26-year-old's last panic attack stranded her on a subway platform that runs one stop further than the map shows; each volume she finds a new station that exists only when someone on the line is mid-attack — and helps the person there step off.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The F train. The first return. A teenage boy mid-attack. Barley tea. The first sentence: 'You can come back here whenever.'
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The Yamanote. A salaryman on the Sunday-night-train. The station is named. Yui meets the clerk who handed her the cup.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The London Northern. A grad student mid-thesis. Yui learns the stations are also exits — you can stay if you want.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The Taipei Brown. A creator influencer mid-collab-meltdown. Yui's first multi-person case.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The LA Metro Gold. Yui's own old roommate, mid-attack, recognizes Yui. The personal stakes open.

series.series_logline: Open Sundays only. Each customer leaves with one tiny ritual. Each ritual was once someone else's, and someday will be someone else's.
series.series_description: Reni inherited a single key and a single instruction: the shop opens Sundays only, between 11 and 5. The shop is the size of a walk-in closet, on the second floor of a converted brownstone in Brooklyn, and the inventory looks like nothing — a jar of folded notes, a small drawer of smoked tea leaves, a tray of knotted threads. The customers find their way. Each Sunday Reni listens, asks one question, and reaches for one item. The customer leaves with a pocket-ritual they can do in 30 seconds anywhere — at a desk, on a train, in a bathroom stall. The shop's stock replenishes only when a customer returns and donates back the ritual that worked, slightly altered. The long arc: Reni's grandmother — the original keeper — wrote down one ritual she never gave to anyone. It is folded in the back of the cash drawer. The book Reni is, slowly, becoming.
series.reader_promise: Every Sunday, one small thing you can put in your pocket and use Monday.
series.audience: Gen Z 24–29 urban Gen Z wellness-curious first-job
series.marketing_angle: The Apothecary Diaries reframed for Gen Z urban wellness — one pocket-ritual at a time.
series.long_arc_spine: A 25-year-old runs a Sunday-only apothecary shop where each anxious customer leaves with one tiny ritual — a folded note, a smoked tea, a knotted thread — and the protagonist slowly accumulates the master ritual that calmed her own grandmother fifty years ago.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: First Sunday open. A woman in her 50s. A folded note. The note that calmed her father in 1982.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: A college freshman. A smoked tea. The protocol: brew, smell, do not drink. The ritual leaves with her.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: A high-school teacher in a school shooting drill aftermath. A knotted thread. The thread is named.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: First return-customer. The woman from vol_1 brings back the note, altered. The shop's inventory grows.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Reni opens the cash drawer for closing. The grandmother's folded note is visible for the first time, but not yet read.

series.series_logline: Each Tuesday at 11 p.m. she records the podcast. Each Tuesday at 3 a.m. the sleep-spirit visits one of her listeners. They never meet her. They sleep.
series.series_description: Wren is 23, third-year journalism, runs a niche sleep-podcast (270 listeners, growing) where she reads slow-paced public-domain texts and the small grey sleep-spirit she has always seen perches on the mic. One Wednesday a listener emails: 'I dreamed about a small grey creature last night and slept for the first time in three weeks.' Wren realizes the spirit is leaving the recording. Each volume the spirit visits one listener — their insomnia story is mapped, the dream-visit is rendered, the listener sleeps. The listener never knows it was Wren. The long arc: the spirit is aging. Wren is learning to take care of it. There is a 271st listener whose story the spirit will not visit. Whose story is it?
series.reader_promise: Someone is helping you sleep. You will not know who.
series.audience: Gen Z 19–25 insomnia-named-and-claimed, podcast-listening
series.marketing_angle: Mushishi for the podcast generation — quiet, episodic, devotional.
series.long_arc_spine: A 23-year-old sleep-podcast host's recurring sleep-spirit mascot starts visiting individual listeners in their dreams — each volume one listener's insomnia case is mapped and resolved.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: First listener-email. The med student's three-week insomnia. The spirit visits. He sleeps.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The night-shift nurse. The spirit teaches Wren about shift-work circadian disruption.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The high-schooler whose parents fight at night. The spirit's intervention is larger.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The first listener who recognizes the spirit on the podcast. Their email is just one line: 'Thank you.'
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The 271st listener. The story the spirit will not visit. The mystery opens.

series.series_logline: One stop further than the last station, in every city, there is a clinic. The dream-walker works the night shift. Each volume she restores one sleeper.
series.series_description: Imogen, 26, lost a year of sleep to a breakup-then-grief cycle and ended up one stop too far on the F train at 4 a.m. The moon-station clinic was waiting. They hired her. She is the night-shift dream-walker now — each shift one sleeper from any city in the world arrives in a dream-zone she has to visit, map, and gently restore. The dream-zones are locales: an underwater bus stop, a forest of clocks, a kitchen that is also a beach, a library where every book is the reader's own unfinished thought. Each volume one zone, one sleeper. The long arc: Imogen's own dream-zone has been closed for visitors since she started. It will not open until she's ready. She is not yet.
series.reader_promise: Your sleep has a city, and someone is working the night shift there for you.
series.audience: Gen Z 23–28 urban insomniacs, sleep-app users, transit-city
series.marketing_angle: Spirited Away for the insomnia generation — episodic, devotional, transit-mediated.
series.long_arc_spine: A spirit-world sleep clinic operates one stop further than the last subway station in every major city — the protagonist works the night shift, and each volume is one dream-zone visited and one sleeper restored.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The underwater bus stop. A teenager with grief-insomnia. Restored.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The forest of clocks. A new parent's sleep-cycle. Restored.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The kitchen-beach. A senior nurse, shift-work. Restored.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The library of unfinished thoughts. The protagonist's college roommate, by accident.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Imogen's own zone, briefly visible at end-of-shift. The reader sees it once.

series.series_logline: The premium tier of the sleep-tracking app costs $4.99/month and includes one yokai. The yokai visits. The user sleeps. She is the moderator who keeps it humane.
series.series_description: Quinn, 25, took a contract role as a 'content moderator' for a sleep-tracking app and discovered on day three that the premium tier is staffed by sleep-yokai — small, professional, slightly bored — who visit users' dreams when invoked. Quinn's job is to review the visit logs and ratify or veto each intervention. Most are fine. Some are not. Each volume one case: a yokai that was a little too aggressive with a teenager's intrusive thoughts, a yokai that fell asleep on duty and the user dreamed of her instead, a yokai that became attached. Quinn is learning to manage them. The long arc: the company is going public. The yokai do not want to be on the S-1. Quinn has to choose.
series.reader_promise: Your sleep app has employees you don't see. Someone makes sure they're kind.
series.audience: Gen Z 24–28 wellness-tech industry, content-moderator-adjacent
series.marketing_angle: Aggretsuko's workplace iyashikei applied to the wellness-tech industry — humane labor inside the sleep-app boom.
series.long_arc_spine: A sleep-tracking app's premium tier is staffed by yokai who visit users' dreams — the protagonist is the only human moderator, and each volume she ratifies one yokai's intervention with one user.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: First veto. The over-aggressive yokai. The teenager's case.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The sleeping yokai. The user dreams of her. The HR conversation.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The attached yokai. The user is a single dad. Quinn negotiates.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The first IPO meeting. Quinn meets the CEO and his yokai (he has one).
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Quinn drafts her resignation. Or her negotiation. The choice opens.

series.series_logline: Open 1 to 5. One bowl per customer. The bowl is warm. The customer sleeps. The cook does not.
series.series_description: Mei, 26, runs a tiny basement kitchen in a brownstone in Brooklyn — no sign, no Yelp page, finds you on the worst night via a referral you can't quite remember receiving. Open 1 a.m. to 5 a.m. One bowl per customer. The bowl is always warm grain — porridge, rice, oatmeal, congee — and one small additive Mei chooses based on a single question. Each volume one customer arrives with one sleeplessness pattern (rumination, grief, hypervigilance, screen-fried, anticipatory anxiety) and one bowl resolves it. The customer leaves and sleeps. They never return — sleep restored, no need. The long arc: Mei has not slept herself in 4 years. Each customer she serves she is also serving herself, indirectly. The 200th customer will be the one whose bowl, finally, is also her own.
series.reader_promise: One bowl. One night. You will sleep.
series.audience: Gen Z 23–28 urban insomniacs, food-and-warmth-attentive
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: Midnight Diner for the insomnia generation — episodic, warm, devotional.
series.long_arc_spine: A 24-hour kitchen open only 1 a.m. to 5 a.m. serves one bowl of warm grain to each insomniac who finds the door; each volume one customer's sleep is gently restored over one bowl.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: First customer. Ruminator. Porridge with cardamom. Sleeps.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Grief insomnia. Congee with ginger and salt. Sleeps.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Hypervigilant new mom. Rice porridge with miso. Sleeps.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Screen-fried teen. Oatmeal with cinnamon and dark honey. Sleeps.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: First customer who almost recognizes Mei. The kitchen flickers. The long arc opens.

series.series_logline: Ila can dream someone else's dream for them. She's been doing it accidentally for years. Now she's learning to do it on purpose.
series.series_description: Ila is 22, art-school, has always had vivid dreams she could remember in detail. She realizes in her senior year that the friend she dreamed about last night actually had the dream — they emailed her a description that matched her own. The dreams she has on someone else's behalf are the dreams they need to have but cannot. Each volume Ila acquires one new dream-collaborator yokai (a small fox who helps with grief-dreams, a heron who helps with creative-block dreams, a moth who helps with closure-dreams) and dispatches one dream per night. Receivers do not know. The cost: Ila herself sleeps lightly, dreams thinly. The long arc: who taught her, and what is the dream she has not yet dreamed for herself?
series.reader_promise: Someone is dreaming the dream you needed. They are quiet about it.
series.audience: Gen Z 20–25 dream-journaling, art-school, creative-claimed
series.marketing_angle: Mushishi for the dream-journaling generation — quiet, devotional, gift-economy.
series.long_arc_spine: A 22-year-old discovers she can dream the dreams that other people need to have — and slowly accumulates a roster of dream-collaborator yokai who help her dispatch one dream per night to one needful sleeper.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The grief-dream for a friend. The fox arrives.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The creative-block dream for a classmate. The heron arrives.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The closure-dream for an ex. The moth arrives.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The first dream Ila dispatches to a stranger. The roster grows.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Ila's own dream — the one she has been saving for herself — is hinted. The arc opens.

series.series_logline: Every new client walks in with a small yokai-trainer attached. The yokai shows Bea where the body is holding what the mouth won't say.
series.series_description: Bea is 25, was a serious ballet dancer until 19, runs the 6 a.m. and 9 p.m. Pilates classes at a small studio in Portland. The yokai started appearing in her second year of teaching — small companions perched on each new client's shoulder, sometimes a small bird, sometimes a small fox, sometimes a shape Bea has no name for. Each yokai shows her where the body is holding something the client has not said. Each volume one client's somatic story is gently unfolded — through Pilates cues that are also psychological cues, through a hand on a shoulder blade, through a question asked at the right moment. The yokai softens. The client returns. The long arc: Bea's own yokai — never seen by anyone else — has been with her since 19. It will not let her dance again. Whether it should is the long question.
series.reader_promise: Your body has a yokai, and the right trainer can see her.
series.audience: Gen Z 23–28 movement-practice (Pilates / yoga / climbing), body-aware
series.marketing_angle: Somatic-Pilates iyashikei for the body-literate Gen Z — A Silent Voice attention applied to the studio.
series.long_arc_spine: A 25-year-old former-dancer Pilates instructor sees a small yokai trainer-spirit attached to each new client; the yokai shows where the client's body holds shame, and each volume one client's somatic story is gently unfolded.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: First new client. Bird-yokai. Held breath. Unfolded over one session.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Fox-yokai. Lower back. The story is grief. Unfolded over two.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The unnamed shape. Pelvis. The client has not told her therapist yet. Bea is gentle.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: First returning client. Smaller yokai. Bea's reward.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Bea catches her own yokai in the studio mirror. The reader sees the shape. Bea looks away.

series.series_logline: The archive has been growing since 1932. Each letter is written from one body-part to another. Each volume she reads one aloud.
series.series_description: Salah, 27, is finishing a master's in somatic therapy in Chicago and inherits an archive from her late great-aunt — a wooden filing cabinet of letters, each written, in a different hand, from one body-part to another. 'Dear Wrist, I am the Shoulder, I have been holding for both of us.' 'Dear Throat, I am the Belly, the words you swallowed arrive here.' The archive has been growing since 1932 — Salah's great-aunt collected them from clients across four cities. Salah's job, in volume one, is to figure out what to do with them. By volume three she is reading them aloud to current clients whose somatic story matches. The body part lights up. The held thing loosens. The long arc: there is one letter in the archive addressed to Salah herself. It has been there since before she was born.
series.reader_promise: Your body has been writing letters. Someone is reading them aloud.
series.audience: Gen Z 24–29 somatic-therapy-curious, queer-coded, body-literate
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: Somatic-therapy iyashikei for the body-literate generation — archive as practice.
series.long_arc_spine: A 27-year-old somatic-therapist-in-training inherits an archive of letters written from one body-part to another; each volume she visits the locale (the wrist, the throat, the back of the knee) and helps one client read one letter aloud.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The wrist. The shoulder's letter. A first client's chronic pain.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The throat. The belly's letter. A client's swallowed grief.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The back of the knee. The hip's letter. A client's withheld choice.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The collarbone. The jaw's letter. A client's withheld voice.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Salah finds her own envelope in the archive. The arc opens. She does not yet open it.

series.series_logline: She rents a 200-square-foot clinic above a bakery. The roster of yokai she works with grows volume by volume. The clients sleep on the table.
series.series_description: Naomi, 26, took her LMT certification two years ago and rents a single-room clinic above a bakery in a small city in upstate New York. The first yokai showed up in week three — the shoulder-yokai, who helps her find the right knot. By volume one she has three on the roster (shoulder, jaw, lower-back). Each volume one client arrives with a stuck somatic story and the corresponding yokai helps Naomi work it out. The clients sleep on the table. They go home rested. The yokai do not follow them. The long arc: a new yokai joins each season — pelvis (vol 4), diaphragm (vol 7), neck (vol 11) — and Naomi's roster grows. The mystery: the yokai are not in fact assigned to body parts. They are assigned to held emotions, and each one has a story Naomi does not yet know.
series.reader_promise: Your tension is shaped, named, and worked out.
series.audience: Gen Z 24–28 bodywork-curious, urban, post-grad
series.marketing_angle: Massage-clinic iyashikei for the body-literate Gen Z — workplace + companion-roster.
series.long_arc_spine: A 26-year-old massage therapist's small clinic is staffed by a slowly-growing roster of yokai-of-held-tension (the shoulder-yokai, the jaw-yokai, the lower-back-yokai), each of whom helps with one client's stuck somatic story per volume.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: First client. Shoulder-yokai. Computer-job tension. Resolved.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Jaw-yokai. Public-speaker client. Resolved.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Lower-back-yokai. Grief-carrying client. Resolved.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Pelvis-yokai joins the roster. First client: a postpartum new mom.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Naomi notices the shoulder-yokai is also a grief-yokai. The mystery opens.

series.series_logline: Each Tuesday she ships a new 90-second breath practice. Each Tuesday it fits exactly one follower's stuck pattern. The fit is not coincidence.
series.series_description: Tomi, 24, certified in three breathwork modalities, has 80k TikTok followers and a Tuesday-night ritual: read the DMs from the past week, find the one stuck breath-pattern that keeps surfacing, invent the 90-second practice that exactly targets it. She does not know how she knows. The practices are precise — box-breathing variants, physiological-sigh variants, hum-into-fingertip cues — and they work. Each volume one practice is invented, one pattern is named, one follower (chosen by the DMs themselves) is gently helped. The long arc: someone is sending Tomi the DMs. Not the followers — the DMs themselves are arriving from a source she cannot trace. The 100th DM is the one she will not be able to answer with a 90-second practice.
series.reader_promise: Your stuck breath has a 90-second practice. It will be on your FYP Tuesday.
series.audience: Gen Z 22–27 breathwork-curious, chronically-online, somatic-named
series.marketing_angle: Breathwork-creator iyashikei for the somatic-literate TikTok generation.
series.long_arc_spine: A 24-year-old breathwork instructor's TikTok DMs surface one stuck breath-pattern per follower; each volume she invents one new short practice that targets one pattern and ships it as a single 90-second video.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: First Tuesday. The held-breath-during-Zoom pattern. The practice. The video.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The hyperventilation-while-scrolling pattern. The physiological sigh.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The sleep-onset breath-hold. The hum into fingertip.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The grief-shallow-chest pattern. The four-six exhale.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The DM Tomi cannot trace. The source begins to appear.

series.series_logline: Every chalk bag at the gym carries one small held memory of the body that bought it. The route-setter is learning to read them.
series.series_description: Yumi, 22, is a route-setter and assistant manager at a bouldering gym in Seattle. The chalk bags belong to the regulars — each clipped to the wall at the bouldering area, each with the regular's name on it. Yumi realizes in volume one that when she rests her hand on a chalk bag she briefly experiences a fragment of the body-memory of the climber it belongs to: the day they fell from V3 and stopped trying for two months, the morning they came to the gym to escape a fight, the year they were 11 and first wanted to climb. Each volume one chalk bag, one regular, one memory mapped and gently honored — Yumi sets a route for them, or doesn't say anything, or has the right conversation at the right grade. The long arc: Yumi's own chalk bag is on the wall. She has not picked it up in a year. The reason is in it.
series.reader_promise: Your body remembers, and your chalk bag is keeping count.
series.audience: Gen Z 20–26 climbing/bouldering/movement-sport, body-literate
series.marketing_angle: Bouldering iyashikei for the somatic-literate Gen Z — climbing-gym chosen-family.
series.long_arc_spine: A bouldering gym's chalk bags each carry a small somatic memory; the protagonist (a route-setter) maps one bag, one climber, one held memory per volume.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The first chalk bag. A new regular. The day they fell from V3.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The morning-fight regular. Yumi sets her a V4 problem that is the right shape.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The 11-year-old memory. Yumi has the right conversation, accidentally.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A regular's chalk bag goes missing. The memory is at risk. Yumi tracks it.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Yumi's own chalk bag, still on the wall. The reader feels the weight. Yumi does not yet pick it up.

series.series_title: The Inn That Keeps the Score
series.series_logline: A nine-room inn in the Hudson Valley. Every guest checks in with a body-memory they have not been able to put down. The innkeeper is learning to help them do it.
series.series_description: Rin is 29, a former pediatric ICU nurse who left the hospital after a difficult three years and inherited a nine-room inland inn from her aunt. Each room has a small companion yokai bound to it — the one who handles tight-shoulder memories (room 3), the one for swallowed-grief (room 5), the one for held-breath rage (room 7). Guests do not book the room. The room books the guest — Rin's aunt's old reservation system arranges them. Each volume one guest checks in for two nights, eats one dinner Rin prepares, sits on a porch and is finally able to say — or feel without saying — the body-memory the room is set up to host. The yokai do small quiet things: warm a tea, dim a lamp, place a stone in a pocket. The guest leaves lighter. The long arc: Rin has not slept in room 9. She has not opened it since she arrived. The aunt's letter said: 'It is the one we share.'
series.reader_promise: Whatever your body has been carrying, there is a room for it and a yokai who handles its weight.
series.audience: Millennials 28–34, Gen Z 25–28, somatic-therapy-literate, burnt-out helpers
series.marketing_angle: Mushishi for the trauma-informed Millennial — somatic iyashikei with a Hudson Valley inn aesthetic.
series.long_arc_spine: Each guest who checks into a small inland inn carries one body-memory their body still holds; the innkeeper (a former trauma nurse) hosts a quiet roster of memory-yokai who help unfold one held thing per stay.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Room 3. A construction supervisor with a tight right shoulder. The shoulder-yokai shows the held year.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Room 5. A widow who has not cried in fourteen months. The grief-yokai sits beside the bathtub.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Room 7. A 40-year-old man with held jaw-rage. A bowl of clay arrives at dinner.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Room 3 again. The construction supervisor comes back with his daughter. The yokai meets a second body.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Rin walks past room 9. The door has unsealed itself. She does not open it yet.

series.series_logline: Every scar holds a tiny ghost. The ghost knows one note. The artist is learning to let the song be heard.
series.series_description: Wren, 24, queer, runs a small tattoo-and-scarwork studio in Oakland — fine-line, scar-cover, top-surgery aftercare. She realized in her apprenticeship that when she holds her tuning fork near a client's scar she can hear a single sustained note — clear, specific, the body's. Each scar holds a tiny ghost-of-the-injury and the ghost knows only that one note. Each volume one client comes in for one piece of work and Wren helps them, with their permission, hear the chord their body has been holding — sometimes a four-note minor, sometimes a held augmented triad. The client cries, or laughs, or asks for the work in a new place. The body is heard. The long arc: a regular client returns each year. Their chord has been changing. Wren's own scar — the one she has never inked over — has not yet been struck. The first time she lets a co-worker hold a fork to it, the studio goes quiet.
series.reader_promise: Your scars have a song. Someone trained will help you hear it without forcing the listening.
series.audience: Gen Z 20–28 queer / trans, scar-literate, somatic-therapy-curious
series.marketing_angle: Heartstopper-tender meets Witch Hat Atelier craft — somatic queer iyashikei with tuning-fork as ritual instrument.
series.long_arc_spine: Each scar on a body holds a tiny ghost that can sing exactly one note; a young tattoo-and-scar artist learns to listen, and each volume she helps one client compose the song of their body and finally hear it.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: First client. A trans man's top-surgery scar two years out. The chord is the relief he has not let himself feel.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: A burn survivor. The minor third. Wren learns when to stop the listening.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: A self-harm survivor. The augmented triad. A long careful conversation. The client chooses what to do with the song.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A C-section scar. The mother brings her toddler. The chord includes another person.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Wren's own scar. The studio goes quiet. The arc opens.

series.series_logline: Inside every patient is a country. The cartographer walks it volume by volume — heart-province, lung-mountains, gut-river-delta — and brings back the map.
series.series_description: Tova is 23, second-year medical anatomy, lonely, and inherits an unfashionable role: cartographer of the inner body. When she sleeps with the inherited brass compass beside her bed she walks the body of one patient — a stranger she has never met — as if it is a country. Heart-province is a walled city with watchtowers. Lung-mountains are colder than they should be. Gut-river-delta is full of small barges of incomplete digestion. Each volume one province visited, one map drawn, one patient slowly understood. Tova returns in the morning and sends the map, anonymously, to the patient's primary care doctor. The doctor uses it. The patient gets better. The long arc: there is one country — her own — she has not been allowed to walk. The compass will not point. Mapping anatomy as soft-fantasy quest; warmly drawn; quasi-medieval village register; the body is sacred geography. Volumes are arranged by organ system.
series.reader_promise: Your body is a country. Someone is making the map. The map will be used.
series.audience: Gen Z 19–25 pre-med / nursing / anatomy-curious, fantasy-positive, somatic-literate
series.marketing_angle: Witch Hat Atelier craft applied to anatomy — soft-fantasy somatic adventure, both formats.
series.long_arc_spine: A 23-year-old anatomy-student inherits the role of cartographer of the inner body — a soft-fantasy quasi-medieval world where each organ system is a province and the cartographer maps one new province per volume in service of an unseen patient.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Heart-province. A 50-year-old with anxiety chest-tightness. The watchtower locked from the inside.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Lung-mountains. An asthmatic, recently grieving. A pass closed by an old snow.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Gut-river-delta. A young woman with IBS. Barges, sediment, the dam at the second bend.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Spleen-orchard. A blood disorder. Apples missing from a particular row. The map is incomplete on purpose.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The compass twitches toward Tova herself. The first hint of her own country.

series.series_logline: Every phantom-limb pain is a small haunting. There is someone who can give the haunting a chair to sit down in. She works two jobs, and the second one is not on her résumé.
series.series_description: Eli, 27, certified prosthetist at a rehab clinic in Detroit by day and quiet medium-of-the-body by night, has discovered that phantom-limb pain is not random nerve fire but a specific geometry of cosmic-residual haunting. The lost limb leaves a tiny doorway. Things gather there. Eli does not exorcize — she invites the haunting in, gives it a chair, asks what it wants, and most often gives it a small offering and a polite goodbye. Each volume one client. Each haunting is named, hosted, sometimes released. The horror is quiet, not gory — A24 horror, not slasher. The cosmic stakes are real, though: by volume four Eli realizes some of the hauntings are connected to a much larger geometry. There is a master door. Someone — something — opened it. The mystery is whose hand. Carries a horror-genre register but stays in the brand's therapeutic anchor (somatic body-memory) the entire time.
series.reader_promise: What your body has lost is not gone. Someone trained can sit with it without trying to fix it.
series.audience: Gen Z 23–28 chronic-pain / amputee / phantom-limb-aware, horror-literate
series.marketing_angle: A24-quiet horror meets somatic recovery — Mononoke for the chronic-pain generation.
series.long_arc_spine: Phantom-limb pain is not a glitch — it is a small geometry of haunting. A 27-year-old prosthetist-by-day and a quiet medium-by-night learns to give each phantom a room to rest, and slowly uncovers the master haunting behind them all.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: First client. An amputee veteran. The phantom is a small bright shape that asks for one specific song.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: A post-mastectomy client. The phantom is grief shaped like a bird. A polite goodbye.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: A diabetic amputation. The phantom does not want to leave. Eli negotiates.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Two clients with linked phantoms. Eli realizes the geometry.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: A regular client's phantom has been replaced by something larger. The master door is named for the first time.

series.series_logline: Their hands knew first. The story moves at the speed of the body's permission. Each volume one new touch, one new returning memory.
series.series_description: Devi, 26, queer, somatic-therapy-experienced, meets Saif at a community-garden volunteer day in Queens. They are slow. Both of them have body-memory their hands keep — Devi's hands know how to flinch from a previous partner, Saif's hands know how to apologize before he speaks. The relationship is real. The pace is the pace the body allows. Each volume covers one season and one body-memory: the first time their hands meet at a vegetable stall (vol 1, summer), the first time Saif's hand returns to a hand-position his ex-girlfriend used (vol 2, fall), the first time Devi can sleep with someone in the bed (vol 3, winter), the first time Saif laughs in his sleep next to her (vol 4, spring), the first time Devi's hand remembers her grandmother's hand (vol 5, summer two). The magical register is small: in this universe, body-memory is visible as a faint silver shimmer on the hand for a half-second, and only the person whose memory it is can see it. The long arc is the relationship; the volumes are seasons; the runway is the rhythm of their life. A24-tender. Body-trust as the slow plot.
series.reader_promise: Your hands remember. Someone who is paying attention will move at your hands' speed.
series.audience: Gen Z + young Millennial 24–30 queer / somatic-trauma-recovery, slow-romance-positive
series.marketing_angle: Honey Lemon Soda's slow tempo applied to adult somatic-romance — A24-quiet, queer-default.
series.long_arc_spine: Hands hold body-memory the brain forgot first. A 26-year-old's slow first real relationship advances one body-memory at a time — each volume one new touch, one new returning memory, one new piece of trust.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Summer one. Community garden. Hand at the vegetable stall. The faint shimmer Devi sees on her own thumb.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Fall. Saif's hand returns a remembered position. He sees the shimmer. He tells Devi.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Winter. Devi sleeps in the same bed. The body's first new permission.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Spring. Saif laughs in his sleep. Devi's hand finds his and remembers.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Summer two. Devi's hand remembers her grandmother's hand. The relationship is also intergenerational.

series.series_logline: Every burnt-out worker carries a small ember-spirit that is going out. The inn opens Sundays. The relight is slow.
series.series_description: Hana, 32, was a Tier-1 strategy consultant for six years and walked into a small inland inn on a Sunday at 33 and never went back to the firm. The inn opens Sundays only. Guests arrive carrying a small ember-spirit on their chest — Hana can see it; sometimes others can too. The ember can be flickering, over-bright (devouring fuel), guttering, or split in two. Each volume one guest, one ember pattern, one slow restoration over a single Sunday. The companion roster grows: a returning guest learns to be a relight-mentor; the inn's old caretaker-yokai (who has been there since 1972) takes occasional residency in the basement; an ember-grandmother who is half-myth visits in winter. The long arc is the slow accumulation of the relight-mentor roster — by volume 30 the inn is staffed almost entirely by people whose own embers were once nearly out. The exemplar from the V1.2 framework brief: this is the operator's 'Ember Inn' archetype, finally as a series.
series.reader_promise: Your ember has not gone out. There is a place that knows how to relight it, slowly.
series.audience: Millennials 28–36 burnt-out former high-performers; Gen Z 26–28 in fast-burnout lanes
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: Phoenix's flagship V1.2 'Ember Inn' archetype — burnout-recovery iyashikei with a companion-roster engine that compounds for 200 volumes.
series.long_arc_spine: Burnt-out workers each carry a small ember-spirit that is going out; the inn-keeper protagonist runs the off-hours repair shop where ember-spirits relight, and her roster of returning relight-mentors grows volume by volume.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The first guest. A manager whose ember has not flickered in two years. Hana learns the slow relight.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The second guest. A founder whose ember is over-bright and devouring fuel. Different protocol.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The third guest. A parent-employee whose ember is split in two. The longer Sunday.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Hana's own ember is revealed in the mirror. The inn's caretaker yokai sits with her.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: First returning guest. They want to learn to host. The roster of relight-mentors begins.

series.series_logline: Every panic attack is in a foreign language. There is a translator. She is taking apprentices.
series.series_description: Luca, 25, works the late shift at a 24-hour crisis-text line and has been apprenticed (without exactly agreeing to it) to a small translator-yokai who can read the language each panic attack speaks. There are eight known languages: somatic, intrusive-thought, dissociative, hyperventilatory, derealization, anticipatory, post-event, and the rarer trauma-flashback dialect. Each volume one caller, one language identified, one practice taught to the caller in their own first tongue. The yokai teaches Luca slowly — by volume five Luca is fluent in three languages and stumbling in two more. The long arc is the eighth language, which the yokai will not teach until Luca's own panic has spoken its full sentence. Phone-line case-of-the-week pacing; quiet warmth; the yokai is small and patient.
series.reader_promise: Your panic is a language. Someone fluent will translate, and your body is welcome on the line.
series.audience: Gen Z 22–28 panic-disorder named-and-claimed, crisis-line-aware
series.marketing_angle: Crisis-line iyashikei for the panic-literate Gen Z — a translator-yokai instead of a script.
series.long_arc_spine: Every panic attack speaks a different language — somatic, intrusive, dissociative, hyperventilatory — and a 25-year-old translator-yokai's apprentice learns one language per volume by interpreting one panic attack for one stranger.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: First caller. Somatic language. The chest tightness. The grounding practice that fits.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Intrusive-thought language. The teenage caller. The yokai shows the redirect technique.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Dissociative language. A new mom in her car. Luca translates back to the body.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Hyperventilatory language. A college student mid-finals. The physiological sigh.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: First repeat caller. Their language has changed. Luca learns a language has stages.

series.series_logline: The logbook started in 1908. Every entry stabilizes one person. The latest hand is hers. The question is whose hands held it before.
series.series_description: Wen, 22, is a senior at a small liberal-arts college in Western Massachusetts who finds a leather logbook in the campus archive while doing work-study. It has been hand-passed for 117 years. Each entry is the same shape: name (initials only), date, presentation, intervention, outcome. The earliest entries are panic and 'the shakes' on factory floors in 1908; the latest is undated and in a hand Wen does not yet recognize. The instruction tucked inside is one sentence: 'You will know the next one when you see them.' Each volume Wen identifies one current case — a roommate's panic, a stranger on the campus green, a TA mid-crisis — and writes the next entry: the intervention that actually worked. The logbook helps. The mystery: each entry seems to be writing itself slightly back at her, in hands that change with each generation. Who keeps the logbook? The answer is hinted at slowly. The book-format companion edition is a fictional facsimile of the logbook itself — readable both ways.
series.reader_promise: Whatever you are stabilizing, someone has been keeping notes for 117 years. The notes work.
series.audience: Gen Z 19–24 university, journaling-community, mental-health-literate
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: The Apothecary Diaries' case-book engine applied to mental-health stabilization — both webtoon and a facsimile book companion.
series.long_arc_spine: A 22-year-old inherits a leather logbook of stabilizer interventions written by anonymous predecessors going back to 1908; each volume she completes one entry on a current case — and slowly the question of WHO has been keeping the logbook for a hundred and seventeen years opens.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Wen finds the logbook. The first current case: her roommate's first panic attack. Wen's first entry.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: A 1932 entry rewrites itself in the night. Wen sees her own handwriting echo a stranger's.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: A stranger on the campus green. The intervention that comes to Wen is from a 1960 entry.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A TA mid-crisis. The intervention is new — Wen invents it. The logbook records it for the next holder.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Wen finds the holder before her, alive, in town. The mystery deepens.

series.series_logline: The Stabilizer Guild's first-aid adventurers travel the quasi-medieval world relighting burnt-out heroes. Iris is rank 1. There are nine ranks.
series.series_description: Iris, 23, is the newest member of the Stabilizer Guild in a soft-fantasy city that looks like a cross between Witch Hat Atelier's drawing-rooms and Delicious in Dungeon's guildhalls. The Guild's job is to dispatch first-aid adventurers to relight burnt-out heroes — paladin who can no longer pray, ranger who can no longer name birds, healer who has stopped sleeping. Each arc Iris masters one new technique (the Grounded Compass, the Slow Bellow, the Held Stone, the Three Names) and earns one new visible badge sewn into her cloak. The runway is the ladder of ranks: 1 to 9. The therapeutic anchor is intact — every technique is a real panic-first-aid or stabilization technique re-skinned as a guild discipline. Visible competence ladder. Visible character growth. Soft-fantasy iyashikei with stakes that stay personal, not world-ending. Strong fit for both vertical webtoon and traditional volumes.
series.reader_promise: Your stabilization is a craft. There is a guild for it. The competence ladder is visible and real.
series.audience: Gen Z 20–27 fantasy-positive panic/burnout audience, both webtoon-vertical and traditional manga
series.marketing_angle: Frieren-soft-fantasy meets Solo Leveling's visible-rank engine — for the panic-first-aid generation.
series.long_arc_spine: In a quasi-medieval guild city the Stabilizer Guild dispatches first-aid adventurers to relight burnt-out heroes; the protagonist ranks up one tier per arc by mastering one new panic-first-aid technique — visible badges, visible competence, soft-fantasy register.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Iris joins the Guild. First mission: a paladin whose ember-prayer has gone out. The Grounded Compass. Badge 1.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: A ranger who has stopped naming birds. The Slow Bellow. Badge 2. Iris meets her future partner.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: A healer who has stopped sleeping. The Held Stone. Badge 3. Iris's first mistake.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A returning hero — the paladin from vol_1, mid-relapse. Iris learns the Three Names.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Iris is promoted to rank 2. The cloak has a new color. Her partner is ranked higher.

series.series_title: Year One at Steady Heart Academy
series.series_logline: Steady Heart Academy trains stabilizers. The students are 16 to 19. The storms are small but real. Reni is in her first year.
series.series_description: Reni, 17, has been admitted to Steady Heart Academy — a soft-fantasy boarding school in a small valley town where the talented students learn to handle the small visible weather-storms that other people's panic attacks generate. Each storm is small (a localized rainstorm, a flicker of indoor wind, a sudden cold). The discipline is to stand near it, name it, and breathe with the affected person until it passes. Each volume covers one term of one year and one new technique mastered: term 1 the Compass, term 2 the Slow Hand, term 3 the Quiet Question. The academy is queer-default, neurodivergent-default, ensemble-school. Reni has three classmates who matter: her dorm-mate Vivi (who panics herself and is also a student), her seatmate Kareem (whose storms are larger because he is a hereditary stabilizer), and a senior named Hua who is about to graduate into the field. The runway is the academy's three-year structure plus a probable two-year apprenticeship after — life-stage rhythm. School-genre register meets brand's stabilizer anchor.
series.reader_promise: Your panic is a small storm. There is a school. You can learn the disciplines.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 14–17 and Gen Z 17–22 school-genre readers; bridge audience for academy iyashikei
series.marketing_angle: Witch Hat Atelier's school-of-craft applied to panic-first-aid — Gen Alpha bridge with Gen Z school-genre register.
series.long_arc_spine: A soft-fantasy academy trains young stabilizers to handle panic and crisis in a world where panic attacks manifest as small visible storms; the protagonist ages through three years, year one volume one, learning one stabilization discipline per term.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Admission. First storm in the dorm. The Compass. Reni meets Vivi.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Term 1 finals. A classmate's hereditary storm. Kareem demonstrates the family technique.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Term 2. The Slow Hand. Reni stabilizes a stranger in the village for the first time.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Term 3. The Quiet Question. The senior Hua graduates. Reni receives Hua's notebook.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Summer. Reni's own first storm. Vivi is there. The arc opens.

series.series_logline: A burnt-out consultant who quit at 31 works the 11 p.m.-7 a.m. shift at a 24-hour Kinko's, where she can read the thought-loop printed in every customer's job — and slip a second page that lets them stop.
series.series_description: Hana had a director title at 30 and a stress fracture in her foot from pacing during conference calls. She quit, moved back to her parents' duplex, and took the overnight shift at the only 24-hour print shop in the strip mall. The first customer of the night is a PhD candidate uploading her dissertation for the eleventh time, and Hana sees, behind the file, the loop: *one more pass, one more pass, one more pass*. She prints what the student asked for. Then she prints a one-page handout — a small CBT decentering, written in the register of a friend, no clinical varnish — and slips it into the bag. The student takes it home and the loop closes. Each night brings a new customer carrying a new loop. A founder iterating a pitch deck for the 40th time. A dad printing his daughter's eulogy revision #7. A grad student re-printing a passive-aggressive email she hasn't sent. Hana cannot print the second page until she has read the loop precisely. The long arc: who taught her to read them, and what is the loop she cannot yet read in her own job. A24-quiet. Fluorescent. Toner-and-coffee specific.
series.reader_promise: Your thought-loop has a one-page intervention and a clerk who knows which one.
series.audience: Millennial 28-35 former corporate knowledge workers in recovery from optimization culture
series.marketing_angle: If The Apothecary Diaries was set in a 24-hour Kinko's and the prescriptions were one-page CBT handouts.
series.long_arc_spine: A 31-year-old former management consultant works the overnight shift at a 24-hour print shop and discovers that every customer's most-printed document is a thought-loop they cannot escape; she learns to print a one-page intervention that lets the loop close.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The PhD candidate. Dissertation pass #11. The first second-page. The loop closes.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The founder. Pitch deck revision #40. Hana writes a second page about sunk-cost and the founder leaves the shop a different person.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The dad and the eulogy. Hana cannot find the right second page. She tries three. The third works.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Hana's old colleague walks in, doesn't recognize her in the fluorescents. She prints his presentation and his loop. She does not give him the second page. She thinks about why.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The shop's own loop, printed faintly in the margin of every receipt. Hana sees it for the first time. The manager who hired her hands her a key.

series.series_logline: A glitchy productivity app lets Mira read replies from the version of herself who didn't burn out — and slowly more users discover their own letters arriving from the lives they almost picked.
series.series_description: Mira beta-tests a journaling app called FutureSelf — write a letter to who you'll be in twelve months, the app holds it, the app delivers it. She writes one in a panic on the bathroom floor of her ad agency. Twelve months should pass. Six days later a reply arrives in her notifications, from a sender labeled `mira@1.0`, in her own voice, describing a Tuesday morning Mira has not lived: 'I quit on a Wednesday. The first month was bad. I started running. Here is the sentence I wish you would read now.' The reply is real. The app is a doorway, not a journal. Each volume a new beta tester surfaces whose letter has started writing back — a high-school senior writing to her college self, a divorced dad writing to the version of him who left earlier, a third-year resident whose other-self is a baker. Mira becomes the unofficial moderator of a forum where these letters get parsed. The long arc: the app's lead engineer has been writing letters to herself for six years and has the most replies of anyone. She is missing. Slow-burn. Lo-fi. Notification-sound-specific.
series.reader_promise: The version of you who chose differently is writing back. Here is the inbox.
series.audience: Gen Z 24-28 first-job knowledge workers with productivity apps and existential drift
series.marketing_angle: Before the Coffee Gets Cold meets Severance through a glitchy journaling app.
series.long_arc_spine: A productivity app called FutureSelf, designed to let you write letters to yourself one year out, glitches — and starts delivering replies. A 26-year-old beta tester realizes the replies are real, written by the version of her who chose differently, and she becomes the unofficial relay for a growing roster of users whose future-selves are trying to write back.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The bathroom-floor letter. The reply six days later. The Tuesday that hasn't happened yet.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The high-school senior. Her other-self went to community college and is happier. The senior changes her common-app essay.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The divorced dad. His letter says: 'I learned to make pancakes the way she liked. I send them in photos. She replies.' He cries on the train.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The resident-and-baker. Two letters cross. Mira moderates her first conflict between selves.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The lead engineer. Her six-year archive of replies. Mira finds the last letter, marked unread.

series.series_logline: A 34-year-old indie dev ships a Pomodoro timer with a small marshmallow-shaped mascot only she can see — and the mascot keeps pausing the timer for affect-states the user hasn't named yet.
series.series_description: Yuki ships a Pomodoro timer called Mochi at 11 p.m. on a Tuesday. The mascot is a small marshmallow with a tail. Three days after launch users start emailing: *my timer pauses when I cry. my timer pauses when I have a panic attack. my timer pauses when I think about my dad.* Yuki cannot see the mascot pause on users' screens, but she can see it on her own — and when she opens user analytics she can see, behind the data, a small marshmallow on each user's shoulder, watching. Each volume one user surfaces whose mascot has paused in a specific way: a recently-bereaved illustrator whose mascot will not let her work past 4 p.m.; an ADHD writer whose mascot extends the focus blocks the writer is enjoying and shortens the ones she's white-knuckling; a postpartum new mom whose mascot will only run during the baby's naps. Yuki cannot direct the mascot. The mascot can only be addressed by being named. The long arc: who built the mascot's recognition layer (Yuki didn't), and what happens when the mascot on Yuki's own shoulder pauses for something she has not yet named.
series.reader_promise: Your focus app sees you. You will not be optimized through grief.
series.audience: Millennial 30-38 late-diagnosed ADHD knowledge workers and adjacent indie devs
series.marketing_angle: Heartstopper's chosen-family register applied to ADHD-late-diagnosed adult productivity software.
series.long_arc_spine: A focus-timer app develops a small sentient mascot only visible to its 34-year-old developer; the mascot pauses the timer whenever the user is in a recognizable affect-state (grief, panic, dissociation) and refuses to restart until the user has named it. The developer's user base grows. The mascot's roster grows.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Launch. The first three emails. Yuki realizes the mascot is real and refunds nobody.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The bereaved illustrator. Mochi will not start past 4 p.m. The user names it: 'this is the time my dad died.' The mascot bows.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The ADHD writer. Hyperfocus hours and white-knuckle hours, named in the language of demand-avoidance.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The postpartum mom. Mochi runs only during nap windows. Yuki writes a feature: 'micro-Pomodoro for caretaker hours.'
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Yuki's own Mochi pauses on a Sunday afternoon for the first time. She cannot name it. The roster shows up at her apartment.

series.series_logline: A burnt-out therapist takes index cards through a bakery's back-door mail slot — each card a thought-loop, each reply a single practice — and slowly the loops start touching.
series.series_description: Iris quit therapy practice at 37 after one panic attack between sessions. She moved into the apartment above her cousin's bakery on Greene Street and would not have started reading cards again except that the bakery's back-door mail slot kept receiving them. A single index card, no return address, one paragraph: *I cannot stop checking if I sent that email last August. I check 30 times a day. The email was fine.* Iris reads it, drinks her coffee, writes a reply on the back: *This is checking-compulsion masquerading as conscientiousness. The unwinding practice is …* She tapes the card to the bakery's community board for the writer to retrieve. The card is gone by morning. A new card arrives. By the third week there are five new cards a day. Each volume she diagnoses one loop and prescribes one practice; the practices interlock — the rumination-loop client and the procrastination-loop client are the same person, two weeks apart. The mystery: somebody is collecting Iris's replies into a small zine that gets distributed to subway readers. Cozy. Bakery-warm. Index-card-tactile.
series.reader_promise: Your thought-loop has a name. The name has a practice. The practice fits on an index card.
series.audience: Millennial 35-42 therapy-literate readers who lapsed out of formal practice
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: Apothecary Diaries-style case-of-the-week with CBT distortions instead of medicinal herbs.
series.long_arc_spine: A 38-year-old former therapist runs a side practice from a back-of-bakery office; clients write a single anonymous index card describing a thought-loop and she diagnoses which cognitive distortion it is and what one practice unwinds it. Cases stack. The protagonist is unknotting a city's collective cognitive load, one card at a time.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The August email card. The first reply. The card is gone by morning.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The rumination loop. The procrastination loop. Iris realizes they are the same handwriting.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The all-or-nothing card. Iris's reply runs across two cards for the first time. The bakery's community board is full.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The cousin who owns the bakery has a card on the board, in her own handwriting. Iris reads it without knowing.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The zine surfaces. A teenager hands one to Iris on the subway. She is the anonymous author.

series.series_logline: A 25-year-old falls asleep at her desk mid-spiral and wakes in a Studio-Ghibli fantasy realm where every town is run by a different cognitive distortion — and the guild she joins specializes in unwinding them.
series.series_description: Ren falls asleep during a stack-trace at 2 a.m. and wakes in a meadow outside a town called Catastrophica, where every NPC speaks in worst-case scenarios and the mayor is a small dragon that grows when fed predictions. She is met by a guild: a paladin who handles black-and-white-thinking towns, a bard who runs personalization, a healer who only takes shoulds. They recognize her: 'Oh — you're a checker. We've been short a checker.' Each volume the guild liberates one town, one distortion, one practice. Catastrophica falls when Ren teaches the citizens to write down the worst-case AND the most-likely-case. Shouldsburg falls when the bard sings the citizens through a needs-vs-shoulds exercise. The realm is the inside of Ren's nervous system, slightly externalized. The long arc: home is the next town. To leave the realm she has to liberate the town she comes from — and the town is the cubicle she fell asleep in. Recovery-isekai. No combat darker than a sparring match. Companion-creatures gentle.
series.reader_promise: Your spiral has a fantasy map. The guild will walk it with you.
series.audience: Gen Z 22-27 first-job knowledge workers fluent in CBT vocabulary and isekai grammar
series.marketing_angle: Frieren's slow-walk register applied to a recovery-isekai of CBT distortions; Witch Hat Atelier's gentle apprenticeship for the work.
series.long_arc_spine: A 25-year-old code-bootcamp grad falls asleep mid-spiral and wakes in a gentle fantasy realm where each town is governed by a single cognitive distortion; she joins a guild of fellow-overthinkers and they liberate towns one CBT skill at a time. The long arc: home is the next town.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The fall-asleep. The meadow. Catastrophica. Ren is named a checker. The dragon shrinks one size.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Shouldsburg. The bard's needs-vs-shoulds song. A retired baker reopens her shop because she wants to.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Mind-reading Hollow. The paladin teaches the town to ask. Three weddings happen in three days.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: All-or-Nothing Ridge. Ren leads her first town. She teaches gradients via a paint-mixing scene that will get reblogged.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The guild map reveals one more town no one will visit: Ren's home town. The guild votes to go anyway.

series.series_logline: A burnt-out engineer inherits a six-room inn that knows which room each guest needs — and learns to be the innkeeper her own collapse needed two years ago.
series.series_description: Naomi had a staff-engineer title at a hyperscaler and a quiet collapse on a Sunday afternoon she still cannot fully remember. Eighteen months later her aunt's lawyer calls: she has inherited a six-room inn on a fog-soaked stretch of coastal highway. She drives up to sell it. The inn is full when she arrives. The current guests are a divorce-recovery retreat she did not book. The first morning a man arrives without a reservation, looking like Naomi looked eighteen months ago, and the inn — somehow — opens Room 2 for him, which is the room with the heaviest blackout curtains and the largest bath. The inn knows. Each volume one guest stays one week and Naomi learns one room. Room 1 (Just Stopped): blackout curtains, tea, no questions. Room 2 (First Sleep): bath, weighted blanket, soup. Room 3 (Bodies Come Back): walk, swim, garden. Room 4 (Old Feelings Return): journal, fire, dog. Room 5 (First Idea): sketchbook, hot drink, view. Room 6 (Re-entry): suitcases, conversation, a returning-to-the-world meal. The long arc: who Naomi's aunt was, and which room Naomi herself slept in, eighteen months ago, without remembering. Slow-burn. Coastal. Wood-stove specific.
series.reader_promise: Burnout has six rooms and an inn that knows which one you need.
series.audience: Millennial 32-40 burnt-out senior individual contributors and managers on de-facto sabbatical
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: Studio Ghibli's coastal-iyashikei register applied to American knowledge-worker burnout in six exact stages.
series.long_arc_spine: A 36-year-old former senior engineer inherits a small inn off the Pacific Coast Highway from an aunt she barely knew; the inn's six rooms each correspond to a stage of burnout recovery, and guests are quietly routed to the right room by the inn itself. Each volume a new guest stays one week.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Naomi arrives. The unbooked man. Room 2 opens itself. The first soup.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: A teacher checks in. The inn opens Room 1. She sleeps for fourteen hours. Naomi learns the rule: do not knock.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: A founder checks in, restless. Room 3. He hates the walk on day one. He loves the swim on day four.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A nurse checks in. Room 4. The old feelings. The dog is named. The nurse cries on a Wednesday.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: A returning guest — the man from vol_1 — comes back for Room 5. The inn rearranges itself. Naomi opens her aunt's letter.

series.series_logline: A YouTuber takes a hard off-season after a public meltdown and starts hosting other crashed-out creators at a cabin; she can see each guest's metric-lantern, and learns which practice dims which lantern.
series.series_description: Mei has 1.2M subs and a video pinned to the top of her channel that says 'I'm going dark for a year.' She isn't dark. She's running a five-cabin AirBnB compound in the foothills of the Catskills, and the only people she takes are other creators in collapse. The first guest is a beauty creator whose sponsorship-anxiety lantern is bright enough to read a book by. Mei serves dinner. The lantern dims one notch. Each volume a different creator-shape arrives — a gaming streamer whose lantern is view-velocity, an autofic novelist whose lantern is Goodreads star average, a TikTok dancer whose lantern is the algorithm itself. Mei learns: each lantern dims to a different practice. Some need a walk. Some need a hard deletion of an app. Some need a phone call to a parent. The long arc: Mei's own lantern is still on, visible only in mirrors, and she has been pretending not to see it. Cabin-quiet. Phone-off specific. Loamy.
series.reader_promise: Your metric has a shape. Here is the practice that dims it.
series.audience: Gen Z 24-30 mid-tier creators in or near burnout; Millennial creator-adjacents
series.marketing_angle: A24's quiet retreat register applied to the post-creator-economy crashout, with a metric-as-lantern visual hook.
series.long_arc_spine: A 27-year-old YouTuber with 1.2M subs takes a hard off-season after a public crashout; she ends up running a small mountain cabin AirBnB where other creators come to recover — and each guest's first day she can see the metric (subs, view-velocity, sponsor-anxiety) hovering above their head as a small lantern that dims as they unwind.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The beauty creator arrives. The sponsor-anxiety lantern. Dinner. The first dim.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The gaming streamer. View-velocity lantern. The hard rule: no phones at the fire.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The novelist. Goodreads-star lantern. The practice is a long letter to her editor she never sends.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The dancer. The algorithm-lantern. Mei has no practice for this one. She and the dancer co-write a manifesto.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Mei's own lantern, visible at last in her bathroom mirror. The current guest names it for her over breakfast.

series.series_logline: After quitting at 33 with no plan, Aisha bakes one sourdough loaf a week for a year — and the bread, slowly, teaches her a metabolism she has not lived in since college.
series.series_description: Aisha quit on a Tuesday. The director-of-marketing email went out at 4:12 p.m. and she drove to her parents' house in central New Jersey with a duffel and her sourdough starter — a gift from a friend three years ago that she had kept barely alive on the back of her counter, refreshing it only in panic, like an oblique apology to the better life she meant to live. Week 1: the starter is barely alive. Week 2: the first loaf is dense and sad. By week 12 she is making decent country boules and reading three pages of a novel before bed. By week 26 she has started a small CSA-style bread route for four neighbors. By week 52 she has not gone back to a screen-job and is not sure she will. There is no magic. The series is a graphic-novel diary, 52 loaves, 52 weeks, an honest account of a Millennial's first real off-ramp from optimization culture. The bread is the unit. The bread is the metabolism. Slow. Specific. Floured.
series.reader_promise: Quitting has a metabolism. The metabolism has a unit. The unit is one loaf a week for a year.
series.audience: Millennial 30-40 mid-career quitters and quit-curious knowledge workers
comp_titles: How to Be a Person in the World (Havrilesky)
series.marketing_angle: Fun Home's confessional graphic-novel register applied to the Millennial post-corporate-decade off-ramp.
series.long_arc_spine: A 33-year-old who quit a marketing director job with no plan starts a sourdough practice on her parents' kitchen counter; the bread, slowly, teaches her to operate at a metabolism she has not lived in since college. No magic. One year. 52 loaves.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Weeks 1-10. The starter wakes up. Loaves 1-10. The first decent crust. Aisha sleeps eight hours for the first time in years.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Weeks 11-20. The first sourdough that her father — a hard man — eats two slices of. Aisha cries in the car.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Weeks 21-30. The bread route begins. Four neighbors. A small spreadsheet she refuses to expand.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Weeks 31-40. The old company calls. Director-level offer, 20% raise. She says no on a Saturday. The next loaf is the best so far.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Weeks 41-52. The year closes. Loaf 52. Aisha writes one page about what she has learned and sends it to her younger sister. The series ends.

series.series_logline: A 35-year-old archivist catalogs the one object each client kept from their burnout — a laptop, a lanyard, a notebook — and writes them a deaccession plan.
series.series_description: Reiko keeps a basement studio under a community garden in Queens. The studio has acid-free boxes, white gloves, a small camera on a copy stand, and three chairs. Clients come with one object. A staff laptop they were never asked to return. The lanyard from the last job. The notebook from the first weekly 1:1 with the manager who broke them. Reiko catalogs the object — provenance, condition, the date it stopped being neutral — and writes a deaccession plan. Sometimes the plan is *keep it in this acid-free box; revisit in six months*. Sometimes it is *bury it in the garden upstairs; the soil composition is documented*. Sometimes it is *send a photo to the person and let the object stay here*. Each volume is one object, one client, one deaccession. The mystery, slow-built: a recurring donor leaves objects anonymously on the studio's stoop. The objects, lined up, tell a single life. Cozy. Vellum. Acid-free.
series.reader_promise: Your burnout has an object. The object has a deaccession plan. The plan dignifies what you went through.
series.audience: Millennial 32-42 archive-literate burnouts in the New York / DC / Chicago corridor
series.marketing_angle: Apothecary Diaries-style case work applied to the literal archiving of American burnout artifacts.
series.long_arc_spine: A 35-year-old former archivist runs a small basement studio where clients bring one object that represents the moment they burnt out; she catalogs it, files it, and writes a deaccession plan — and the client's nervous system rests one notch.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The unreturned laptop. The first deaccession: kept in the box. The client sleeps for a weekend.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The lanyard. Garden burial. The lawyer-client cries in the soil.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The 1:1 notebook. Photo, sent. Object stays. The notebook teaches Reiko something about the client's old manager.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The anonymous stoop-donor. Object 1: a coffee mug. Reiko catalogs it without a client.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The stoop-donor returns. Object 2: a security badge. Reiko begins reconstructing whose life this is.

series.series_logline: A 39-year-old gardener places burnt-out adults in the plot they need for the next twelve months — and the garden, going back four owners, has always done this.
series.series_description: Mae has run the half-acre community garden behind the laundromat for six years. She has thirty-six plots. Twenty-eight are stable, four are training, four are off-ramp. The off-ramp plots take a single person for twelve months: assigned a plot in week 1, harvest in week 36, write a single page about what they learned in week 52. The waiting list is long. The first off-ramper of the new season is a recently-fired-then-publicly-thanked tech VP whose plot is in the back row near the compost. Mae walks her through the first month — tomatoes are not for her, she gets root vegetables. Each volume one new off-ramper joins. The garden roster — a former trauma surgeon now growing sunflowers, a former hedge-fund analyst growing dahlias, a former showrunner growing pumpkins — slowly becomes a chosen family. The mystery: there is a 37th plot, marked only by an old wooden sign, and Mae has never assigned it. She inherits the records from the previous owner and learns it has been waiting forty years for the right person. The garden's previous four owners were also off-rampers. So is Mae. So, possibly, is the reader.
series.reader_promise: Burnout has an off-ramp. The off-ramp is a plot. The plot is yours for a year.
series.audience: Millennial 35-45 mid-career exiters with green hands and a year to spend
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: Frieren's slow-walk register applied to a Brooklyn community garden as twelve-month corporate-burnout halfway house.
series.long_arc_spine: A 39-year-old gardener tends a community garden in a Brooklyn lot; each plot is held in trust for one person on a 12-month off-ramp from a high-burnout career. She places people in the right plot at the right month. The roster grows. The lot's secret: it has always been an off-ramp, going back four owners.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The fired VP arrives. The back-row plot. Root vegetables. Mae explains the rule of the season.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The trauma surgeon plants sunflowers. Mae walks him through pollinator-spacing while he tells her about his last shift.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The analyst plants dahlias. Tubers. The metaphor lands without anyone naming it.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The showrunner and the pumpkins. Mae's first late-night phone call to an off-ramper.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The 37th plot. The wooden sign. Mae finds the previous owner's notebook in the shed wall.

series.series_logline: A late-diagnosed ADHD designer ships a body-doubling app whose algorithm is matching not the users — but the small invisible companion-creatures every user has and only she can see.
series.series_description: Eun-ji ships Room at 28 after a year on a waitlist for a formal ADHD diagnosis. The premise is simple: pair two strangers, both opt-in to body-doubling, both work silently in parallel for fifty minutes. The first wave of users sticks. The retention numbers are strange. Then a user emails: 'I think the woman you paired me with is my next-door neighbor. I have never told anyone I body-double.' Then another: 'My partner used the app on the same day I did, in the same apartment, and you matched us with each other.' Eun-ji looks at the matching code. She did not write it like this. Each user has, in the corner of their camera frame on her monitor, a small companion creature — and the algorithm she did not write is matching the creatures. Each volume one user's pairing tells a story: a recently-divorced dentist matched with a recently-divorced bartender, the same creature on both shoulders, smaller after a week; a recently-diagnosed teen matched with a 60-year-old retiree, the same exact creature, four decades apart. The long arc: who wrote the matching layer, and what Eun-ji's own companion looks like to other users. Lo-fi. Notification-soft. Webcam-window specific.
series.reader_promise: Your focus has a partner. The app sees what you cannot. The match is real.
series.audience: Millennial 28-35 late-diagnosed ADHD remote knowledge workers, plus Gen Z early-career parallel
series.marketing_angle: Heartstopper's chosen-family register applied to body-doubling apps — with the ADHD-companion-as-creature visual hook.
series.long_arc_spine: A 29-year-old late-diagnosed ADHD designer launches a body-doubling app called Room, in which two strangers do parallel solo work over video for 50-minute blocks. The app's matching algorithm starts producing eerily perfect pairs — and she realizes the algorithm is picking up something humans can't see: each user has an invisible companion creature, and the algorithm is matching the creatures.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Launch. The dentist and the bartender, paired by their creatures. The first email.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The teen and the retiree. The same creature, four decades apart. The retiree cries on camera.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The two partners. Eun-ji has to disclose the matching layer to one user. She picks the wrong one.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A user's creature is missing one Tuesday morning. Eun-ji audits her own creature, which she has never looked at.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The matching-layer engineer is named in a commit history Eun-ji didn't write. She finds the engineer.

series.series_logline: A 41-year-old ADHD librarian catalogs other people's abandoned projects — the half-knitted scarf, the dissertation chapter 4, the screenplay second act — and the act of cataloging changes which projects come home.
series.series_description: Ngozi runs a tiny branch library in a college town and a back-room called the Unfinished Collection, by appointment only. Patrons bring one unfinished thing. A half-knitted scarf. A dissertation chapter 4. A screenplay second act. A weight-lifting program week 14. Ngozi catalogs it — provenance, last touched, the precise sensation the patron had when they put it down. Some patrons take it home after the catalog session, finished or not. Some leave it.
