The shelf grows. Ngozi has ADHD diagnosed at 39 and built this collection to honor what executive-function research keeps missing: the moral weight of the unfinished. Each volume is one patron, one object, one cataloging. The mystery: a single book has lived on the top shelf for thirty years, marked *DO NOT CATALOG*. Ngozi inherits the librarian's notes. She reads the book. Cozy. Tactile. Card-catalog specific.
series.reader_promise: Your unfinished thing has moral weight. A librarian will catalog it with care.
series.audience: Millennial 35-45 late-diagnosed ADHD adults with a closet full of abandoned projects
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library meets a small back-room dedicated to ADHD's most painful asset: the unfinished.
series.long_arc_spine: A 41-year-old ADHD librarian runs a small back-room called the Unfinished Collection — a single room of objects representing other people's abandoned projects. Patrons bring their unfinished thing in; the librarian catalogs it; sometimes they take it back, sometimes they don't. The long arc: the room is at capacity.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The half-knitted scarf. The first cataloging. The patron takes the scarf home and finishes it on a Sunday.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Chapter 4. The patron leaves it on the shelf. Ngozi adds it to a private rotating display.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The screenplay second act. The patron — a recently-out queer screenwriter — re-writes the entire thing in three weeks.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The week-14 lifting program. The patron — a dad of three — joins a small gym and never reopens the file.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The do-not-catalog book on the top shelf. Ngozi reads it. It is the previous librarian's own unfinished thing.

series.series_logline: A 24-year-old falls into hyperfocus and wakes in a gentle archipelago where each island is one executive-function skill — and the eighth island, home, is missing from every map.
series.series_description: Talia is a 24-year-old who has been told her whole life she 'just needs to focus.' On a Tuesday at 11:47 a.m. she falls into hyperfocus on a Figma file and wakes in a coastal town on an island called Task-Initiation, where every NPC is doing the next-smallest thing and the small dragon at the harbor only grows when fed first-steps. She is met by a small party: a paladin from Working-Memory, a bard from Time-Perception, a healer from Emotional-Regulation. They have been short a self-monitoring class. They recognize her. Each arc opens one island. Task-Initiation falls when the citizens are taught the two-minute rule. Working-Memory falls when the bard composes a song that is also a checklist. Time-Perception falls when the citizens learn to set timers. The realm is the inside of Talia's nervous system, slightly externalized, and her job is not to win — it is to map it. The long arc: home is the eighth island, called Self-Monitoring, and it is missing from every map. The party will have to build it. Recovery-isekai. Coastal. Gentle. Companion-creatures soft.
series.reader_promise: Executive function has eight islands. You will visit them. The party will walk with you.
series.audience: Gen Z 22-28 first-job knowledge workers with productivity-app fatigue and an isekai-grammar habit
series.marketing_angle: Frieren's slow-walk and Delicious in Dungeon's cozy party-quest applied to a recovery-isekai of executive-function skills.
series.long_arc_spine: A 24-year-old falls into hyperfocus on a Tuesday and wakes in a small gentle archipelago where each island is governed by a different executive-function skill — task initiation, working memory, time perception, emotional regulation, sustained attention, self-monitoring. She joins a small party and they unlock one island per arc. The long arc: home is the eighth island and it is missing.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The Tuesday hyperfocus. The harbor. The party. The two-minute-rule arc on Task-Initiation.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Working-Memory island. The checklist-song. A retired baker reopens her shop because she now remembers her own recipes.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Time-Perception island. Timers. The bard makes the citizens dance to a 25-minute song; the island is healed.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Emotional-Regulation island. The healer's hardest case. Talia learns to name affect.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The party studies the maps. The eighth island is missing. They will need to build it. The arc closes.

series.series_logline: A 16-year-old ADHD junior finds the after-hours study hall in the back of a Korean grocery — run by an aunt-figure who teaches one focus practice a night.
series.series_description: Hana is 16, diagnosed ADHD in seventh grade, top of her math class but failing English because she cannot finish an essay. She walks past the Korean grocery on the way home from cram school and one Wednesday night sees a soft light in the back. The auntie at the register waves her in. There is a small back room with four desks, four lamps, four small bowls of rice crackers, and a handwritten sign: *Study Hall, 7-10. Phones off. One practice a night.* The first night the practice is *body-doubling.* The second is *pomodoro with a clock you can hear.* The third is *write the worst version first.* The roster grows: a baseball-team senior with undiagnosed ADHD, a freshman with anxiety, a Korean exchange student a year ahead in math but a year behind in English. The aunt — Mrs. Oh — does not lecture. She teaches by example. The long arc: Mrs. Oh's own son refused to attend a study hall like this, and the students slowly piece together why. School-cozy. After-dark. Rice-cracker specific.
series.reader_promise: Your focus has a study hall and an aunt-figure who knows the practice.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 14-18 ADHD high-schoolers; secondary Gen Z 18-22 early-college parallel
comp_titles: I Want to Eat Your Pancreas
series.marketing_angle: Heartstopper's tender high-school register applied to an after-hours ADHD study hall behind a Korean grocery.
series.long_arc_spine: A 16-year-old ADHD high-school junior discovers a small after-hours study hall in the back of a Korean grocery, run by an aunt-figure who teaches one focus practice per night. The roster of students grows. The mystery: the aunt is a former teacher whose own son refused to attend.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The grocery. The back room. The first practice: body-doubling. Hana finishes a paragraph.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The audible-clock pomodoro. The baseball senior joins. He cries during the third block.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Write-the-worst-version-first. Hana finishes her first essay. Mrs. Oh circles two sentences.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The freshman. The exchange student. The roster is four. The grocery is full.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Mrs. Oh tells the story of her son. The students vote to invite him to one session.

series.series_logline: A 37-year-old ergonomic consultant builds tiny moss-and-stone gardens for ADHD employees' cubicles — and the moss tells the truth about the employee's nervous system that week.
series.series_description: Junko was a corporate ergonomic consultant for ten years; she now runs a quiet practice for one specific client type — ADHD knowledge workers whose companies have referred her after a near-quit. She visits the cubicle. She measures light, sound, sightlines, and what she calls the affect-load. She returns with a small moss-and-stone garden, twelve inches by six. The garden is calibrated. The moss tells the truth: green when the client is regulated, brown at the edges when overload begins, slightly luminescent when the client has experienced a small win. The client cannot fake the moss. The client does not need to. Each volume is one cubicle, one garden, one client. The mystery: HR at the largest of her client companies has begun photographing the gardens. The photographs are being used to predict attrition. Junko writes a letter. Office-cozy. Fluorescent-and-moss specific.
series.reader_promise: Your nervous system has a small garden. The moss tells the truth. The truth has a consultant who will defend it.
series.audience: Millennial 32-42 ADHD knowledge workers still inside the building but on the edge of leaving
series.marketing_angle: Apothecary Diaries case-of-the-week applied to corporate-cubicle moss as honest nervous-system telemetry.
series.long_arc_spine: A 37-year-old corporate ergonomic consultant builds tiny moss-and-stone gardens for ADHD knowledge workers' cubicles; each garden is calibrated to the user's specific dysregulation pattern, and the moss grows or browns according to how the user's nervous system is doing that week. Each volume she designs one new garden. The long arc: HR is watching, and HR is wrong.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The first cubicle. A senior PM whose moss browns within a week. Junko adjusts the light.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: An engineer whose moss goes luminescent on Thursdays. They identify the Thursday meeting. They cancel it.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: A designer whose moss is uniformly brown for a month. The garden is not the problem. Junko refers her out.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: An HR partner photographs three gardens. Junko notices. The partner does not know she has noticed.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Junko writes the letter to HR. The letter is firm. The garden program is moved out of HR's hands.

series.series_logline: A 15-year-old ADHD sophomore solves small neighborhood mysteries by following the side-tangents nobody else can; a hidden guild of grown-up sideways-thinkers is recruiting.
series.series_description: Devon is fifteen, ADHD-and-AuDHD, and cannot follow a class lecture for more than seven minutes without one tangent. His tangents are accurate: he notices a stranger's wristwatch is set thirteen minutes fast and that turns into a real lead. The first case of the series is the lost dog of a Korean grandmother three doors down, and Devon solves it by noticing the dog's water dish in the photo has a specific brand-label that is only sold at one bodega four blocks away. Each volume is one neighborhood case — the missing wedding ring at the laundromat, the recurring petty theft of a single brand of yogurt, the cousin who has been ghost-attending the family dinner. Devon is, slowly, recruited by a guild — a folder appears in his backpack with one page: a list of solved cases, none of them his, all signed by the same initials, half a century old. The guild is real. The guild is everywhere. The guild thinks sideways and is winning. Shonen-quiet. School-cozy. Backpack-notebook specific.
series.reader_promise: Your tangent is a clue. Your brain is a detective. The guild has been waiting.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 13-17 ADHD/AuDHD readers; secondary Gen Z 18-23 nostalgia parallel
series.marketing_angle: Apothecary Diaries' case-of-the-week applied to ADHD/AuDHD sideways thinking, with a Heartstopper-tender chosen-family register.
series.long_arc_spine: A 15-year-old ADHD high-school sophomore solves small neighborhood mysteries by following the side-tangents nobody else notices. Each volume one case. The long arc: the side-tangents are themselves coming from somewhere — and a guild of sideways-thinking adults is watching.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The lost dog. The wristwatch. The bodega brand-label. The first case.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The wedding ring at the laundromat. The dryer-timer pattern. The patron in the third chair.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The yogurt thief. A 70-year-old retired teacher with selective memory. Devon protects her dignity.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The ghost-attending cousin. Devon's first case inside his own family. The grandmother knew.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The backpack folder. The half-century of cases. Devon meets one of the initialed seniors at a coffee shop.

series.series_logline: A 36-year-old freshly-diagnosed ADHD adult receives an invitation envelope only late-diagnosed adults can see — and joins a guild that has been quietly running for forty years.
series.series_description: Priya gets her ADHD diagnosis at 36, after a year on a waitlist. The letter from the clinic arrives in a thick brown envelope. Inside that envelope is a second envelope, smaller, hand-addressed, that the clinic assistant did not put there. The second envelope contains one card: an invitation to a guild, a phone number, a single password. Priya is suspicious. She calls. A woman answers and explains: every late-diagnosed adult, going back four decades, has received this envelope; only late-diagnosed adults can see it; the guild meets every other Sunday at varying locations. Priya goes. The roster: a 52-year-old paralegal, a 41-year-old conductor, a 33-year-old dad of three, a 60-year-old retired postal worker, a 28-year-old fresh-out-of-grad-school designer. Each volume one member tells the story of their diagnosis-arrival and one specific compensatory strategy they built in the years before. Priya keeps notes. The long arc: who has been writing the invitations, and what they want from the guild. Warm. Sunday-meeting specific. Coffee-and-folding-chair textured.
series.reader_promise: Your late diagnosis has a guild. The guild has been waiting forty years.
series.audience: Millennial 35-45 late-diagnosed ADHD adults and their AuDHD peers; secondary Gen X parallel
series.marketing_angle: Apothecary Diaries' guild register applied to the late-diagnosed-ADHD-adult support group, with a forty-year invitation mystery.
series.long_arc_spine: A 36-year-old freshly-diagnosed ADHD adult joins an invitation-only online support group for late-diagnosed knowledge workers; each member's diagnosis letter arrived along with a small invitation envelope only late-diagnosed adults can see. The roster grows. The long arc: someone has been writing the invitations for forty years.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The double envelope. The phone call. The first Sunday meeting in a church basement.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The paralegal's compensatory strategy: the rolodex of routines. Priya begins one of her own.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The conductor's strategy: the score-as-checklist. Priya tries it on a Sunday.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The retired postal worker. The forty-year history of the guild. The first member is named.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The handwriting on the invitations changes, very slightly. The guild votes to investigate.

series.series_logline: A 14-year-old AuDHD middle-schooler falls into a special interest and wakes in a gentle fantasy realm where each region is a domain of intense expertise — and a party of fellow-deep-divers is recruiting.
series.series_description: Lila is fourteen and her special interest is freshwater turtles. She knows 113 species by Latin name. On a Tuesday at 3:18 p.m. she falls into a research spiral and wakes in a forested coastal region called Cheloniana, where every NPC is also a turtle-deep-diver and the small dragon at the harbor only grows when fed accurate species facts. She is met by a small party: a paladin whose interest is mineral classification, a bard whose interest is 1970s-era Japanese city pop, a healer whose interest is regional dialect drift. Each arc opens one region. Cheloniana, Mineralia, City-Pop-Heights, Dialectland. The party walks through, deep-dives, and unlocks the region's gift to the realm. The realm is the externalized inside of an AuDHD nervous system, slightly utopian. The long arc: home is the region of Self-Acceptance, and the dragon there does not eat facts — it eats the act of being unmasked in front of someone safe. Recovery-isekai. Gentle. Companion-creatures soft. No combat darker than a sparring match.
series.reader_promise: Your special interest has a kingdom. The party is waiting. The dragon eats facts.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 12-16 AuDHD middle-schoolers; secondary Gen Z 17-22 high-school-into-college parallel
series.marketing_angle: Frieren's slow-walk applied to AuDHD special-interest as recovery-isekai, with Witch Hat Atelier's apprenticeship for the work.
series.long_arc_spine: A 14-year-old AuDHD middle-schooler falls into a special interest on a Tuesday and wakes in a fantasy realm where each region is a domain of intense expertise; she joins a party of fellow-deep-divers and they unlock one region per arc. The long arc: home is the region of self-acceptance, and it is harder than the rest.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The Tuesday research spiral. Cheloniana. The party. The species-fact dragon. The first deep-dive.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Mineralia. The paladin's domain. Lila learns to deep-dive a domain she did not pick.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: City-Pop-Heights. The bard sings. The healer dances. Lila records 9 songs.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Dialectland. The healer's domain. The realm-wide map fills in.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The Region of Self-Acceptance. The dragon does not eat facts. Lila's first unmasking. The arc closes.

series.series_logline: A 44-year-old AuDHD woodworker runs a Saturday-only workshop where mid-career neurodivergent adults learn to work with their hands — and the firehouse is for sale.
series.series_description: Mara bought the firehouse in 2019 for $1 and a five-year promise to keep one bay open to the public. She runs a Saturday workshop, 10-2, six tools, one project per quarter. Her students are mid-career adults discovering they are neurodivergent and re-thinking their next twenty years through their hands. The first student is a 38-year-old corporate lawyer who has hyperfixated on chair joinery for six months and finally signed up. The second is a 47-year-old former teacher building a shelf for her granddaughter. The third is a 29-year-old AuDHD coder making a coffee table. Each volume is one quarter, one student, one project. No magic. The graphic-novel diary register: panels of hands, of cuts, of mistakes, of correction, of one good joint. The long arc: the firehouse's roof needs $42,000 in repair, the city has a five-year purchase option, and Mara has to decide whether the workshop is a gift or a business. Saturday-cozy. Sawdust specific.
series.reader_promise: Your hands have a second career. The workshop is on Saturdays. The firehouse can be saved.
series.audience: Millennial 38-50 neurodivergent mid-career exiters; secondary Gen X late-diagnosed parallel
comp_titles: How to Be a Person in the World (Havrilesky)
series.marketing_angle: Fun Home's confessional graphic-novel register applied to a Saturday neurodivergent woodworking co-op.
series.long_arc_spine: A 44-year-old AuDHD woodworker runs a Saturday-only workshop in a converted firehouse; her students are mid-career adults discovering they are neurodivergent and looking for a second career using their hands. Each volume one student. No magic. Six tools. One year. The long arc: the workshop's own books don't balance, and the firehouse is up for sale.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Q1. The lawyer's first chair joint. Three failed cuts. One success. He cries in the bathroom.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Q2. The teacher's shelf. Mara teaches grain-reading. The shelf is for the granddaughter and Mara meets her.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Q3. The coder's coffee table. AuDHD precision applied to mortise-and-tenon. The table is exhibited.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Q4. The firehouse roof. The $42K. Mara calls a meeting. The students vote.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The five-year mark. The city's option. The workshop becomes a co-op. The students are co-owners.

series.series_logline: A 28-year-old AuDHD orchardist grows nine varieties of tomato — each named for a work-rest pattern — and weekend visitors harvest the one their nervous system needs.
series.series_description: Theo runs a four-acre tomato orchard outside Hudson, NY, and grows nine specific varieties: Sprint, Long-Block, Soft-Focus, Hyperfixation, Maintenance, Recovery, Boundary, Refusal, and Off-Day. Each tomato is real. The names are a joke and not a joke. Visitors come for a one-day or weekend retreat, walk the rows, and slowly identify which variety they are. They harvest accordingly. They take home a small wax box of tomatoes calibrated to their week. Each volume is one visitor. A founder discovers she is a Refusal tomato. A high-school senior discovers she is Soft-Focus. A grandmother discovers she has been a Maintenance her whole life and is sad about it. Theo cooks. Theo listens. Theo does not interpret. The orchard is the interpretation. The long arc: the next zoning meeting threatens to convert the orchard into a horse property, and Theo has to decide whether to fight or to relocate. Pastoral. Slow. Tomato-warm.
series.reader_promise: Your work-rest pattern has a tomato. The orchard knows which one is yours.
series.audience: Gen Z 25-30 burnt-out knowledge workers with retreat budgets; Millennial AuDHD parallel
series.marketing_angle: Frieren's pastoral slow-walk applied to AuDHD-named heirloom tomatoes as work-rest typology.
series.long_arc_spine: A 28-year-old AuDHD orchardist runs a small organic pomodoro tomato orchard in upstate New York — the tomato is also a metaphor for the work-rest unit. Each volume one visitor comes for a weekend retreat and harvests the tomato their nervous system needs. The long arc: the orchard is being threatened by the next zoning meeting.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The founder. The Refusal tomato. The wax box. The Sunday she does not check email.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The high-school senior. Soft-Focus. Theo cooks for her parents.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The grandmother. Maintenance, all her life. The conversation at dusk on the porch.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The zoning meeting is announced. Theo's first phone call to a former visitor.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The orchard sells out for the season. The zoning meeting is delayed. Theo writes a letter.

series.series_logline: A 27-year-old first-time manager inherits a wooden letter-box that delivers a letter each morning from a future-her — and slowly realizes someone else is writing them too.
series.series_description: Aya is promoted to manager at 27 after the previous manager — a quiet senior named Lin — left abruptly. On her first day, her predecessor's desk holds a small lacquered letter-box, no return address, with one note inside: *Open me at 7:50 a.m. on workdays. Trust the letters. — L.* Aya opens it at 7:50 the next morning and a letter is inside, in her own handwriting, written by a version of herself who has already done what Aya is about to do that day. The letter describes the staff meeting, what her senior IC will say, the small kind sentence that will land. Aya is suspicious. She follows it anyway. It works. Each volume one imposter-syndrome scenario gets a letter and a resolution: the first hard 1:1, the first promotion negotiation, the first time she has to disagree with her own manager, the first time she has to manage her former peers, the first time she has to lay someone off. The mystery: Lin is alive, working at a coffee shop two neighborhoods over, and has been writing the letters too — in her own hand. Office-cozy. 7:50-a.m. specific.
series.reader_promise: Your imposter syndrome has a letter from a future-you. The letter arrives at 7:50.
series.audience: Gen Z 25-30 first-time managers; secondary Millennial parallel for new senior roles
series.marketing_angle: Before the Coffee Gets Cold's future-self letters applied to corporate first-time-manager imposter syndrome.
series.long_arc_spine: A 27-year-old first-time manager inherits a small wooden letter-box from her predecessor's desk; each morning a letter appears, written by a future-her who has navigated one specific imposter-syndrome moment. The letters teach her, in order, how to be a competent manager. The long arc: the predecessor is alive and the letters are also being written by her.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The first morning. The letter. The staff meeting. The small kind sentence.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The first 1:1 with a senior IC. The letter says: 'Ask twice. Listen for the answer to the second one.'
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The first promotion negotiation. The letter is two pages. Aya gets the bump.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The first disagreement with her own manager. The letter says: 'Bring data. He likes a table.'
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Aya finds Lin at the coffee shop. Lin writes her one final letter, in person, on a napkin.

series.series_logline: A 34-year-old senior PM runs an unofficial after-hours open office on her company's empty fourteenth floor — Wednesdays 7-10, anyone with a career question, no notes.
series.series_description: Iman has been at the company seven years and has watched too many talented Gen Z hires quit because no one would tell them how to ask for the next role. So she runs the Fourteenth Floor: Wednesdays from 7 to 10 p.m., she sits at a conference table near the south windows with a thermos of black tea, and anyone in the building can come in with one career question. No notes. No HR. Iman tells the truth. The first visitor is a 25-year-old data analyst who wants to know if the senior analyst role is reachable in 18 months. Iman tells her: yes, here is the actual list of skills that will be visible to the promo committee. The analyst leaves with a plan. Each volume is one visitor. A junior PM who is being given second-class projects because his manager doesn't believe in him. A senior engineer who is being underpaid and doesn't know it. A staff designer who has been doing the manager's job for free. No magic. Confessional graphic-novel register. The long arc: HR finds out about the Fourteenth Floor, and the company has to decide whether to formalize it or shut it down. Iman writes a letter to the CEO.
series.reader_promise: Your career has a Fourteenth Floor. Wednesdays at 7. The PM there will tell you the truth.
series.audience: Millennial 30-40 senior individual contributors and managers; secondary Gen Z 25-30 high-potential parallel
comp_titles: How to Be a Person in the World
series.marketing_angle: Fun Home's confessional graphic-novel register applied to corporate-America after-hours career mentorship as a quiet form of labor solidarity.
series.long_arc_spine: A 34-year-old senior product manager runs an unofficial after-hours open office on her company's empty 14th floor — Wednesdays 7-10 p.m., anyone in the building can come in with a career question, no notes taken. Each volume one visitor. The long arc: HR finds out.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Wednesday 7:14 p.m. The data analyst. The promo-committee skill list. She gets the bump in nine months.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The junior PM. Iman tells him to ask for a different manager. He does. He gets her.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The underpaid engineer. Iman gives him the comp band. He negotiates. He gets paid.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The staff designer. Iman tells her to title-promotion-or-leave. She picks leave. She goes to a better company.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: HR sends Iman an email. The CEO sends Iman an email. The Fourteenth Floor goes formal.

series.series_logline: A 33-year-old product designer can see the small grey creature that perches on whichever colleague is mid-imposter-syndrome at 3 p.m. — and she learns the practice that lifts it off.
series.series_description: Akiko is a senior product designer at a mid-size tech company. At 3:08 p.m. on her first day she sees, on the engineer two desks over, a small grey creature the size of a tabby cat, breathing slowly. The creature is perched on his shoulder. Nobody else sees it. By 3:42 the creature has hopped to a PM in the next pod. By 4:11 it is gone for the day. Akiko has been seeing yokai since she was eight. She has never seen this one. She watches it for a month. The creature picks whichever colleague is mid-imposter-syndrome that afternoon — the engineer who got pulled into a Staff-level review without warning, the PM who was told her roadmap was wrong, the designer who was passed over for a promo. The creature does not hurt them. It just sits. Akiko learns: a specific small practice — a kind sentence from a peer, a re-frame, a five-minute walk to the lobby — will lift the creature off. Each volume is one colleague. The long arc: there is a much larger creature, the size of a Saint Bernard, that lives in the C-suite, and Akiko has not yet decided what to do about it. Office-cozy. 3-p.m. fluorescent specific.
series.reader_promise: Your imposter syndrome has a small grey creature. Someone at the office can see it and knows the practice.
series.audience: Millennial 30-40 mid-level knowledge workers; secondary Gen Z 26-30 first-promo parallel
series.marketing_angle: Natsume's Book of Friends register applied to corporate-American imposter syndrome as a literal office yokai.
series.long_arc_spine: A 33-year-old corporate office has a 3 p.m. yokai that only one employee, an Asian-American product designer, can see — a small grey creature that perches on whichever colleague is in the throes of imposter syndrome that afternoon. She learns the practice that lifts the creature off each one. The long arc: the company-wide creature is the largest, and it lives in the C-suite.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Day 1. The engineer. The creature. Akiko walks over with a coffee and a kind sentence. The creature lifts.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The PM and her roadmap. The re-frame. The creature lifts. The PM doesn't know.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The passed-over designer. Akiko sees the creature for three days. She brings a fourth-floor walk.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Akiko's own creature, glimpsed in a window reflection. She sits with it. She names it.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The C-suite Saint Bernard. The CEO sees Akiko looking. The CEO calls her into the office.

series.series_logline: Two co-workers get promoted on the same day and start dating that night — a year of two competent adults trying to be in love while both their imposter-syndrome decades close.
series.series_description: Jia gets the engineering-manager promo on a Wednesday at 4:30 p.m. Sam gets the senior-PM promo at 4:42. They have been quietly attracted to each other for two years and would have done nothing about it except that the company-wide promo email goes out at 5:00 p.m. and they end up at the same wine bar at 5:30 by accident. They have been each other's most trusted peer. They are both terrified of the new jobs. They kiss on the way to the subway. They spend the night on Sam's couch talking and they do not sleep together. The series is a graphic-novel diary across one year: their first month dating while also doing their first months as new senior people; the first time Jia's report is also Sam's stakeholder; the first time they fight about work; the first time they decide together what to disclose to HR; the first time they consider whether one of them should leave the company. No magic. Slow. Specific. Romance for adults who lead teams. The long arc: the year closes with one of them taking a job at a different company, and they have to decide what their relationship is, separately from the workplace that birthed it.
series.reader_promise: Office romance has a competent-adult version where both people are terrified of their new jobs and in love anyway.
series.audience: Millennial 30-38 mid-career knowledge workers in or near new senior roles; secondary Gen Z 27-30 parallel
series.marketing_angle: Heartstopper's tender register applied to two Millennial knowledge workers in their first year as senior people, dating each other through it.
series.long_arc_spine: Two co-workers — a 33-year-old engineering manager and a 31-year-old senior PM — get promoted on the same day and start dating that night. The series is a year of two competent careerist adults trying to be in love while both their imposter-syndrome years simultaneously close. No magic. Twelve months. 26 chapters.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The Wednesday. The promo emails. The wine bar. Sam's couch. The first month.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Months 2-4. Jia's report becomes Sam's stakeholder. The first work fight. The first resolution.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Months 5-7. The HR disclosure. The summer. Jia's parents visit.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Months 8-10. The first time one of them considers leaving. The other one votes for it. Then against.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Months 11-12. One of them takes the other job. They have a year-end conversation about what they are to each other now.

series.series_logline: A former HR business partner senses layoffs twelve hours before they happen and runs an anonymous DM channel that gives the right employees one night to prepare — and someone in leadership is hunting the leak.
series.series_description: Naila was an HR business partner for nine years and quit after one bad layoff. She has kept a guest-account in her old company's Slack workspace and at 7:14 p.m. on the night before a layoff she gets a specific feeling — a small cold spot at the back of her neck. She has learned to trust it. She runs an anonymous DM channel called *seven-p-m-whisper* and at 7:30 she sends one message to the employees she knows are at-risk: *Update your LinkedIn tonight. Pull your contracts off your work laptop tonight. The morning will be hard. You will be okay.* The first save is a 29-year-old engineer who has $11 in savings and a partner without health insurance; he gets the message at 7:31 and is offered a job at a better company on the second day. Each volume is one save. The horror register is not gore — it is the specific dread of corporate-America 9-to-9: the cold-spot at the back of the neck, the predator-app, the boardroom decision made over salads. The long arc: the new CHRO is tracking the leak, and Naila has to decide whether to disappear or to surface and make it formal. Office-horror. Slack-notification specific. Cold.
series.reader_promise: Corporate-America has a dread. The dread has a whisper. The whisper will warn you the night before.
series.audience: Millennial 32-45 corporate-tenured knowledge workers who have survived 1-3 layoff rounds; Gen X parallel
series.marketing_angle: Dandadan's gentle-horror register applied to corporate-layoff dread, with a former-HR-BP whisper-network protagonist.
series.long_arc_spine: A 35-year-old former HR business partner can sense, twelve hours in advance, when a layoff is coming through her old company's Slack workspace; she runs a quiet anonymous DM channel that warns the right people to update their LinkedIn the night before. Each volume one save. The long arc: someone in leadership is tracking the leaks back to her.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The cold-spot at 7:14. The engineer. The DM. The morning. The second-day offer.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: A second layoff. A pregnant PM. Naila's message. The PM gets out two weeks before her water breaks.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The third save. A 55-year-old VP. The harder calibration of how to give an older worker dignity in the message.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The new CHRO. The pattern. The cold spot at the back of Naila's neck is now permanent.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The CHRO finds Naila. The conversation is at a coffee shop. The cold spot is reciprocal.

series.series_logline: Iris kept her husband's Sunday grief-letter circle alive after he died, and started receiving answers nobody else can see.
series.series_description: Iris is thirty-three and seven months a widow when she decides to keep her husband Daniel's Sunday grief-letter circle running out of their second-floor apartment in Cambridge. Six strangers come — a woman whose son drowned, a man whose mother is alive but unrecognizable, a divorced father whose ex still gets the holiday card. They each write one letter to whomever they have lost and read it aloud over weak coffee. Iris does the dishes after. The third Sunday she finds a sentence in Daniel's handwriting on the back of the woman's letter — a sentence the woman's son used to say. Nobody else can see the writing. Each episode a different member's letter is answered in a different hand. Iris does not tell them. She learns instead what each answer asks of her: keep this person coming back. Bring this kind of cookie. Move the lamp. The slow arc is the empty chair Daniel used to sit in, and the one Sunday it is not empty. A24-quiet, Frieren-tender, no afterlife metaphysics — just one chair and one hand.
series.reader_promise: Your dead can answer you, just not in the room where you wrote.
series.audience: Millennials 30–38 newly bereaved spouses and adult children, alt-spiritual without doctrine
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: Before the Coffee Gets Cold for the chair your person used to sit in.
series.long_arc_spine: A 33-year-old widow inherits her late husband's grief-letter circle — a Sunday meeting where each member writes a letter to their dead and reads it aloud — and discovers the letters are being answered, one at a time, in handwriting only she can see.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Iris keeps the circle. First Sunday alone. The mother whose son drowned reads her letter. Daniel's handwriting appears on the back. Iris washes the cup he used to drink from.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The man with the unrecognizable mother. The handwriting that answers is his mother's pre-diagnosis. Iris learns the answers come in the dead's own hand — except when the dead are not yet dead.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The divorced father. The handwriting is his ex-wife's mother. Iris learns the circle's geography: every person you grieve has a hand.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A new member arrives — a woman who lost a friend in a car crash. The hand that answers her is Iris's own husband's. Iris does not breathe for a page.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Iris writes her own letter to Daniel for the first time. The page stays blank. Daniel's chair is empty. But the cookie tin moves one inch. The circle continues.

series.series_logline: Each of Grandma's recipe cards comes with one hour. Make the meal, sit at the table, and she is there.
series.series_description: Maya is twenty-six and a line cook in Providence when her grandmother dies and the only thing in the will addressed to her is a battered tin recipe box. The first time she makes the morning-after biscuits her grandmother is sitting at her own kitchen table — translucent, kind, fully present, and clear about the rules: one hour per recipe, the recipe must be made exactly as written, you do not get to choose when. Maya works through the box. Each volume is one recipe and one hour with one version of her grandmother — at twenty, at forty, at eighty. Some recipes have ingredients Maya cannot find. Some require company. Some are not her grandmother's — they are her grandmother's mother's, her grandmother's lover's, her grandmother's enemy's. The long arc is the last card at the bottom of the tin, in handwriting Maya does not know, that asks her to cook one meal for a person not yet born.
series.reader_promise: Your grandmother is one biscuit away, for exactly one hour at a time.
series.audience: Gen Z 25–29 grandchildren who outlived a grandparent and inherited the kitchen
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: Like Water for Chocolate as iyashikei: one hour with the dead, per recipe.
series.long_arc_spine: A 26-year-old inherits her grandmother's handwritten recipe box and discovers each card carries a specific memory — and that cooking the recipe lets the cook spend exactly one hour with the person who used to make it.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Morning-after biscuits. Grandmother at sixty. The rules are explained. Maya cries through the second batch.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Tomato gravy with sausage. Grandmother at twenty, before she knew Maya's grandfather. A version Maya has never met.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Lemon icebox cake. Grandmother at eighty, with dementia. The hour is the hour she was lucid for one afternoon in 2019.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A recipe Maya cannot read — the handwriting is her great-grandmother's, in Polish. Maya finds the cousin who can translate.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The blank card at the bottom. The instruction: cook for a child not yet born. Maya does not know whose child.

series.series_logline: Wren answers a pet-loss hotline at night. The dead pets call back. They live in her hallway now.
series.series_description: Wren is twenty-seven, non-binary, and a hospice social worker by day. At night they volunteer for the pet-loss hotline because their own cat died two years ago and they needed something to do with the hours between midnight and four. The first dead pet they hear from is a chihuahua named Pierogi, who calls collect from the apartment's landline at 2:47 a.m. and says the caller earlier needs to know he forgave her. Wren delivers the message. Pierogi stays. Each volume a new dead pet calls and stays — a senior tortoise, a cockatiel, a hamster, a barn cat — and joins the small roster that now lives in Wren's narrow Brooklyn hallway. The cases vary: a guardian whose dog is still alive but failing, a teenager whose hamster was their only witness in a hard year, a man whose service dog saved his life and then did not save itself. The arc: Wren's own cat has not called. Wren does not understand why. The hallway keeps growing.
series.reader_promise: Your dead pet is still here, just down the hall, waiting for the right call.
series.audience: Gen Z 24–29 queer and neurodivergent urban pet-bereaved, on the hospice/social-work pipeline
series.marketing_angle: The Travelling Cat Chronicles meets a queer late-night grief hotline.
series.long_arc_spine: A 27-year-old runs a 24-hour pet-grief hotline from her apartment; the dead pets call back, and slowly assemble themselves into a phantom roster that lives in her hallway.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Pierogi calls. The first message gets delivered. The chihuahua stays in the hallway.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The tortoise from a guardian whose mother is dying. The pet teaches Wren about anticipatory grief.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The hamster who was the only witness. The teenager comes off the line laughing for the first time in eight months.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The service dog. The man cries on the line. Wren's hallway is now five animals deep.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Wren's own cat does not call. Wren reads the cat's old vet records. The hint that the cat has not called because the cat is waiting elsewhere.

series.series_logline: First funerals are for the family. Second funerals are for the person who actually died. June officiates both.
series.series_description: June is thirty-five and a former hospice chaplain who left the church and started a one-person business: she performs second funerals, by appointment, at any location the bereaved chooses, for any person they feel was not properly mourned the first time. A father whose son died of overdose in 2003 and was given a sanitized funeral. A daughter whose mother was buried in the wrong dress. A husband whose wife's funeral was hijacked by an estranged sister. Each volume is one second funeral, planned and held. June does not bring religion; she brings a small black book, a candle, one prop the family supplies, and three sentences she will not say in advance. The dead, in this register, are not present — but the family is, finally, and one specific concrete thing about who they actually lost arrives. The long arc: June has never performed a second funeral for her own dead. She does not say who. The book has one blank page at the back, with a name only she can read.
series.reader_promise: The funeral that was too small the first time can be held again.
series.audience: Millennials 32–40 bereaved adults whose first funeral did not fit
comp_titles: The Order of the Good Death (Caitlin Doughty)
series.marketing_angle: Frieren's vigil register applied to American funeral practice. Caitlin Doughty for fiction.
series.long_arc_spine: A 35-year-old funeral celebrant runs a small society that performs second funerals — the one the family didn't get to have — and each second funeral surfaces the person the first one was too small or too rushed to honor.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The father whose son died of overdose. The first second funeral. The candle is lit in the boy's old bedroom. June reads one of his Spotify playlists aloud.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The daughter whose mother was buried in the wrong dress. The funeral is held in the dress that fit. The mother's name is said in the way only the daughter said it.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The hijacked funeral. The husband. The estranged sister is not invited. The wife's actual best friend reads the eulogy that was cut.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A repeat client. The father from vol_1 returns for his other son. The second funeral becomes the start of a third reckoning.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The blank page. The name June will not read. The first hint of who she is owes a second funeral to.

series.series_logline: Lila is a grief doula. She holds you for one year. The grief does not leave, but it becomes someone you can walk with.
series.series_description: Lila is thirty-two and trained as a death doula before pivoting — there is a smaller, less recognized profession that holds the bereaved through the first twelve months after the death. She has six clients at a time, one for each season-and-a-half. Each volume she takes one client through one quarter. A woman who lost her father in October. A trans man who lost his chosen mother in March. A teacher who lost a student in the worst way. A man whose adult son died in another country and the body was not returned. A daughter whose mother died of long-term mental illness and whose grief is, mostly, relief. Lila's tools are small: a candle, a calendar, a piece of clothing, a specific kind of walk. Each volume names one ritual the client invents, the only one for them. The arc: a new client signs up for a first-year contract whose 'who died' field is blank. Lila accepts.
series.reader_promise: There is one ritual that fits your grief. Lila finds it.
series.audience: Millennials 28–38 bereaved in the first year, alt-spiritual, urban, vocabulary-rich
comp_titles: The Year of Magical Thinking (Joan Didion)
What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: Year of Magical Thinking as iyashikei manga: one small daily practice per quarter, for twelve months.
series.long_arc_spine: A 32-year-old grief doula in the West Village holds one client at a time through the first year after a death; each volume is one client, one season, one small ritual that turns the grief from a haunting into a companion.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Autumn. The woman whose father died in October. The first ritual: a daily walk past his last apartment, the wrong way around the block.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Winter. The trans man who lost his chosen mother. The ritual: cook one of her recipes for one friend, every two weeks.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Spring. The teacher who lost a student. The ritual: a single bulb planted, named, ignored.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Summer. The father whose son died abroad. The ritual: a kite, every Sunday, in a park his son never visited.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The new client. The blank 'who died' field. Lila accepts. The mystery of the unnamed dead opens.

series.series_logline: Halle's thrift-store mirror shows her rooms her body knows but her mind has forgotten. Each room gives her one thing back.
series.series_description: Halle is twenty-four, in her first year of trauma-focused therapy, and on a rainy Sunday she buys a tall oval vanity mirror at a Brooklyn thrift store because the frame is pretty. That night the mirror shows her not her bedroom but a kitchen with a yellow refrigerator she has never seen. The kitchen is hers — her body knows the floor — but she has no conscious memory of it. Each volume she returns to one room: the kitchen, the church basement, the backseat of a green car, the back porch of a house in summer. She does not relive the trauma. She brings a small object into the room — a candle, a song, an adult she trusts — and reclaims one specific thing: her own laugh, her ability to taste, her preference for a color, her willingness to be held. The volumes are slow, gentle, never voyeuristic. The mystery: the mirror's frame has a maker's mark, and the maker is still alive. The long arc is finding her.
series.reader_promise: Your body remembers what your mind cannot, and your body is allowed to take back what is yours.
series.audience: Gen Z 22–28 trauma survivors in active therapy, body-literacy vocabulary
comp_titles: The Body Keeps the Score (van der Kolk)
What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: Body Keeps the Score for a generation that buys thrift-store mirrors and goes to trauma therapy.
series.long_arc_spine: A 24-year-old trauma survivor buys a thrift-store vanity mirror that reflects rooms she has never been in — rooms her body remembers but her conscious mind does not — and each volume she returns to one and reclaims one piece of herself from it.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The yellow refrigerator. The first room. Halle brings a candle. She reclaims her own laugh — she has not laughed without flinching in eight years.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The church basement. A song. The kindness of singing badly. She reclaims her voice for ten seconds at a stoplight a week later.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The backseat of the green car. Her current therapist's voice, on speaker. She reclaims the right to be held by a person she chose.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The summer porch. She is small. She is allowed to be small. She reclaims tasting strawberries.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The maker's mark on the frame. The first hint of where the mirror came from. Halle realizes someone made this mirror for someone like her.

series.series_logline: Tova restores old lamps. The lamps remember the light their last owner needed. Her customers are autistic adults who lost a parent who never learned to leave the light on.
series.series_description: Tova is twenty-five, autistic, and her hands know lamps. She runs a one-person restoration shop out of the basement of her aunt's house in Portland — rewiring, refelting, replacing shades, sourcing the exact bulb. Her customers find her by word of mouth. The third lamp she finishes — a green banker's lamp from 1962 — comes back two weeks later with a note: 'It glows like my mother used to forget to.' Tova realizes each lamp she restores carries a specific quality of light its previous owner needed but could not have. Her roster grows: a customer whose father refused to leave hall lights on. A customer whose mother used grief-fluorescent kitchen overheads as punishment. A customer who has not been in a fully-lit room since 1997. Each volume she restores one more lamp for one more autistic adult mourning a parent who did not — could not — learn how to be soft in light. The mystery: Tova's own mother is alive. Tova has not restored a lamp for herself. The aunt's basement is, in fact, very dimly lit.
series.reader_promise: Your parent never learned to leave the light on. Someone else will.
series.audience: Gen Z 23–29 autistic adults estranged or grieving a parent who could not regulate
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: An autistic adult-grief literature gap, filled by an iyashikei lamp shop.
series.long_arc_spine: An autistic 25-year-old runs a small lamp-restoration shop and discovers each restored lamp carries the specific kind of light its previous owner needed to survive — and her customers are mostly autistic adults grieving a parent who never learned to keep the lights on for them.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The banker's lamp. The first note. Tova realizes what she has been doing.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The hall-light customer. A wall sconce. The 7-watt bulb that her father refused for thirty-three years.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The fluorescent-overhead customer. A small dome floor lamp. Warm and below eye level.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The customer who has not been in a fully-lit room since 1997. A series of three small lamps, in increments. Tova learns about graded exposure to brightness.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Tova's own basement. She replaces one bulb. Her mother calls. Tova does not pick up. The arc opens.

series.series_logline: Margaux's grief podcast accepts one anonymous voicemail per week. Each week one song answers. Each volume one caller is found.
series.series_description: Margaux is thirty and runs a small grief-and-loss podcast out of a converted closet in Oakland. Her show has one segment: callers leave an anonymous 90-second voicemail with the thing they never said before their person died. Margaux does not respond. Instead her co-producer — a former music supervisor — finds one song that answers, and the song closes the episode. The show has 11,000 weekly listeners. In the season this volume covers, Margaux begins to receive answers back: a caller writes in, listened in the car, recognized themselves, and now wants to know if the song was right. Each volume Margaux meets one former caller and learns the thing not said, and the song that almost was. The cases vary — a son who could not say I forgive you, a mother who could not say I was wrong, a friend who could not say I loved you. The arc: Margaux has not left her own voicemail. Her producer is, gently, asking her to.
series.reader_promise: Every voicemail can be answered. Every song was almost the right song.
series.audience: Millennials 27–35 podcast-native grievers, music-literate
series.marketing_angle: S-Town meets Mitski, in manga, for a generation that learned to grieve through podcasts.
series.long_arc_spine: A 30-year-old grief podcaster receives anonymous voicemails from people who never said the thing aloud; her show airs the voicemail and a single answering song — and each volume one anonymous caller is found, alive or remembered, and given back the thing they could not say.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The son who could not say I forgive you. The song was Big Thief's 'Not.' He writes back. He is found. He tells Margaux the rest.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The mother who could not say I was wrong. The song was Mitski's 'I Bet on Losing Dogs.' Margaux meets her at a diner.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The friend who could not say I loved you. The friend is queer, the person was straight, the song was Phoebe Bridgers. The friend is fine, mostly, now.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A caller who is found only in records — they died between the voicemail and Margaux's outreach. The song is sung at their funeral. The first time Margaux cries on air.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Margaux's producer hands her the recording mic. Margaux does not leave the voicemail. The next episode airs without a voicemail. The download numbers double.

series.series_logline: Mira can pass through any door her late husband used and stand for one minute where he last stood. She is making a list.
series.series_description: Mira is thirty-four and a documentary translator, fluent in five languages and competent in three more. Her husband Yusuf — a violinist — toured. When he died his itinerary was forty-one cities. The first time Mira tries the trick is in the green room of the Boston Symphony. She breathes in. She passes through. She is briefly standing in the exact spot he last stood, rosin still on the floor, the green-room sandwich on the counter. She has sixty seconds. She brings back one specific thing: the way his sleeve fell when he reached for the case. Each volume she visits one more door — Lisbon, Mexico City, Seoul, Reykjavik, Tbilisi. She does not see him. She gets the room he was last in and the muscle-memory of his presence, and brings back one specific gesture, one specific posture, one specific note he used to warm up on. The long arc: forty-one cities. Forty-one minutes. The forty-second door, in their own apartment, she has not yet opened.
series.reader_promise: Every door your person passed through can be passed through again, for one minute.
series.audience: Millennials 30–38 widowed translators, traveling-class, bilingual+
comp_titles: The Year of Magical Thinking (Joan Didion)
What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: Year of Magical Thinking with one literal magical-realist door per city.
series.long_arc_spine: A 34-year-old widow can walk through any door her late husband ever passed through and briefly stand where he last stood; each volume she visits one door in a different country and brings back one specific thing he carried.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Boston Symphony green room. The sandwich on the counter. The sleeve. Mira learns the rules: sixty seconds, no more, one bring-back.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Lisbon. Backstage of the Coliseu. He warmed up on E minor. Mira brings back the sound of it, fully.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Mexico City. A pharmacy he stopped in for ibuprofen. The mundane door matters too. Mira brings back the smell of cherry cough drops.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Seoul. The Han River bridge he walked across to the venue. Mira brings back the rhythm of his footfall on cold metal.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Reykjavik. The forty-first city. The end of the tour. Mira realizes the forty-second door is their own apartment's bedroom, where he died. She is not yet ready. The volume ends with her hand on the handle.

series.series_logline: Beatrice's husband died. Asher's wife died. They are not replacing anyone. They are figuring out, in real time, what to do.
series.series_description: Beatrice is thirty-six. Her husband Daniel died of pancreatic cancer two years ago. Asher is thirty-eight. His wife Hannah died of an aneurysm three years ago. They meet at a Wednesday-night grief group in a Unitarian church basement in Asheville. They like each other immediately, which is alarming. The series is the first twenty-four months of their relationship — the awkward first dinner where they each accidentally reference their dead spouse, the harder dinner where one of them does not, the first night together (rules are negotiated, mostly successfully), the holiday with the dead spouse's family, the new ring decision, the photo on the dresser decision. The register is not Hallmark; it is honest. No magic, no second sight — the only ghosts here are the photographs and the leftover muscle memory. The portal_mechanic is the letter Asher writes to Hannah explaining Beatrice, that he never sends, and Beatrice's parallel letter to Daniel, also unsent. The long arc is whether they get to keep the letters.
series.reader_promise: Second love after first loss is awkward, real, and possible. The dead get a chair, but not the chair.
series.audience: Millennials 32–42 widowed and re-partnering, alt-spiritual, communicative
comp_titles: The Year of Magical Thinking (Joan Didion)
series.marketing_angle: Past Lives for the second-spouse cohort — A24-tender, no magic, fully earned.
series.long_arc_spine: A 36-year-old widow and a 38-year-old widower meet in a grief support group and slowly, awkwardly, decide to date — the series follows the first 24 months of being a second partner to a person whose first one died.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Wednesday-night group. The first coffee after. Beatrice references Daniel by accident. Asher does not flinch. Beatrice does.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The first dinner that doesn't reference either of them. Both notice. Both feel guilty. Both stay.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The first night. Hannah's photograph is moved from Asher's dresser to a side table — Asher's idea, Beatrice didn't ask.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The first holiday. Asher meets Hannah's parents at a Christmas Eve that includes Beatrice. The mother-in-law is gracious. The sister-in-law is not.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The unsent letters. Both write them in the same week. Both leave them open on the kitchen counter where the other can see, and neither does.

series.series_logline: Lena keeps her brother's community garden plot alive. Each year, one bereaved neighbor takes over one bed and plants one specific thing.
series.series_description: Lena is thirty-three and her brother Mateo died of an overdose four years ago. He had a community garden plot in their neighborhood in Oakland — four 4x8 beds, raised, on the southwest corner. Lena cannot tend the whole plot herself, and she cannot give it up. Each volume she gives one bed to one bereaved neighbor who applies. The neighbor chooses one plant. The plant names what they lost: a man planting his late wife's tomato variety, a teenager planting the basil her grandmother taught her, a widow planting one rose because her husband proposed at a rose garden in 1987. The plot becomes, slowly, the neighborhood's bereavement garden. The magical-realist register: the plants grow at a slightly different speed for each gardener — slower for the ones who need more time, faster for the ones who are ready to harvest. Lena does not explain this to anyone. The long arc is the fourth bed, the one she has not yet given out, the one her brother last planted, what is still growing in it.
series.reader_promise: There is a bed for your grief, and one plant that names what you lost.
series.audience: Millennials 30–38 sibling-bereaved, community-rooted, plant-literate
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: Sweet Bean Paste in a community garden, four beds, four bereavements at a time.
series.long_arc_spine: A 33-year-old runs the community garden plot her dead brother used to tend; each volume a new bereaved neighbor takes over one of his beds and grows one specific plant that names what they lost.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: First bed. The man planting his late wife's tomato. The variety has not been grown in this country in twenty years.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Second bed. The teenager and her grandmother's basil. The teen's first season tending anything.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Third bed. The widow and the single rose. The first frost arrives too early; the rose holds.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: An applicant is turned away — a man whose grief is for a person still living. Lena cannot explain why she said no. The garden agrees.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Lena weeds the fourth bed for the first time. Mateo's last seedling is still there, still alive. She does not know what it is. The plot opens.

series.series_logline: Theo runs a small archival service. He surfaces the last text thread with your dead. Sometimes there is one more message you did not know was waiting.
series.series_description: Theo is twenty-six, a former Apple Genius and current digital archivist. He runs a one-room operation in Somerville: clients bring him the phone of their dead person and ask him to recover, organize, and print the last text thread between them. Theo is precise and quiet. He returns the phone with a small printed book and one of three findings: (a) the thread is whole and beautiful, (b) the thread has been redacted by the dead's family and Theo restores it carefully, or (c) one new message has appeared since the death — short, in the dead's voice, in a syntax Theo recognizes only when he sees three of them. Theo does not tell clients which finding is which. Each volume is one client and one phone. The cases vary: a mother whose teenage daughter's thread is fourteen months long, a husband whose wife's last text was an emoji, a grandfather whose grandson texted him a TikTok the day he died. The arc: Theo's own phone has a thread with his older sister. He has not opened it in three years. The pinned message is new.
series.reader_promise: Your last text thread is bound, beautiful, and may have one more message than you thought.
series.audience: Gen Z 24–29 bereaved-by-phone — the generation whose intimacy lives in messaging apps
series.marketing_angle: Apple Genius as iyashikei: one phone at a time, one bound book, one possible last message.
series.long_arc_spine: A 26-year-old archivist runs a small service that takes a bereaved person's phone, surfaces the last text thread with their dead, and returns the phone with one message they did not know was already there.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The mother and the fourteen-month thread. Finding (a). The book is delivered. The mother sleeps that night for the first time in eight months.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The husband and the emoji. The emoji is a moon. Theo restores the missing meaning — the wife's last bedtime ritual.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The grandfather and the TikTok. The TikTok is no longer on the platform. Theo recovers a frame.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A father whose son's thread has been redacted by the boy's other parent. Theo restores it carefully. The boy comes through clearer.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Theo's own thread. A new pinned message. He does not open it. He prints the book for the next client. The volume ends with the unopened thread.

series.series_logline: Marjorie's husband died. She started walking the dog at 7. Two months in, there were five other widows on the same route.
series.series_description: Marjorie is fifty-two, a recently-widowed librarian in suburban Maryland. The first thing she did after the funeral was the only thing she could think to do: walk the dog. She walked at 7 a.m. because that is when Charles used to walk him. The second week, she passed another widow walking another dog. They nodded. The third week, they walked together. By month three, six widows are on the same loop — different ages, different lengths of widowhood, different dogs. Each volume the circle adds, loses, or holds someone. The register is realist — no magic, no oracles, just morning, dogs, weather, and the kind of frank conversation that is possible only when both parties are looking at a dog instead of each other. The cases are the women: a widow of forty years who reads novels and is the elder, a widow of three weeks who is still numb, a woman whose ex-husband died and complicated everything, a queer widow whose chosen-family situation does not fit the script, and a younger widow who is the only one with small kids. The arc is the dog — Charles's dog — slowing down. Marjorie has to decide what 7 a.m. looks like when he is gone.
series.reader_promise: There is a circle for you, on a sidewalk, at 7 a.m., with a dog.
series.audience: Gen X / older-Millennial 48–62 widows and chosen-family bereaved
comp_titles: The Year of Magical Thinking (Joan Didion)
series.marketing_angle: Olive Kitteridge as morning-walk manga — for the older-widow demographic underserved by Gen-Z register.
series.long_arc_spine: A 52-year-old widow accidentally founds a dog-walking circle of other widows in her neighborhood; the series follows the slow assembly of a six-person mutual-aid pack and the small ways grief becomes manageable when walked together at 7 a.m.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Marjorie starts walking. The first nod. The second nod. The first conversation about weather and the third about the dead.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The forty-year widow joins. Her elderhood is named. The circle is named.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The three-week widow. The circle holds her without speaking. The dog, somehow, leans into her leg.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The widow whose ex died. The complication is honored, not solved. The circle is bigger than it knew.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The queer widow. The chosen-family widow. The circle expands its vocabulary. Marjorie buys six matching leash-clips because she cannot help herself.

series.series_logline: Wren walks through the houses of the recently dead and names what is still there. The houses are listening.
series.series_description: Wren is thirty-five, formerly a hospice nurse, now a freelance grief-house specialist — she is hired by families clearing out the home of a recently-dead parent, spouse, or sibling and walks through with them, room by room, naming what the house is still holding. The register is occult-cosmic but small: each room contains one unfinished sentence the dead person did not finish, and Wren can hear them. She does not exorcise. She listens, says them aloud, and the family decides what to do. The cases vary: a kitchen still holding 'I never told you I'm proud of you,' a hallway still holding 'I forgive you,' a bedroom still holding 'I was wrong,' an attic still holding 'I love this house more than anyone in it.' The horror register is restrained — these houses are not menacing, they are too full. The volumes are about clearing. The long arc: Wren's own childhood home has just been sold. She has not been inside in eleven years. The new owners are asking her to come in.
series.reader_promise: The house you cleared out was holding the sentence your dead did not get to finish. Someone can hear it.
series.audience: Millennials 30–40 clearing out a dead parent's house, alt-spiritual, occult-curious
comp_titles: The Haunting of Hill House (Shirley Jackson)
series.marketing_angle: Mononoke's restrained occult register applied to American grief and house-clearing.
series.long_arc_spine: A 35-year-old grief specialist is hired by families to walk through the house of the recently dead and name what the house is still holding; the houses are quietly, specifically haunted — not by ghosts, but by unfinished sentences — and each volume is one house, one room at a time.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: A widow's house. The kitchen. The sentence 'I never told you I'm proud of you.' The widow's adult son hears it through Wren's voice.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: An estranged father's house. The hallway. 'I forgive you.' The estranged son refuses to accept it at first, then does, in the doorway.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: A mother's house. The bedroom. 'I was wrong.' The daughter laughs and cries at the same time.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A grandfather's attic. 'I love this house more than anyone in it.' The family is briefly furious, then quiet, then sits on the floor for an hour.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Wren's own childhood home. The new owners invite her in. She does not yet enter. The volume ends at the threshold.

series.series_logline: Imani's clients lose someone. They text her. She arrives within ninety minutes with a kit. The kit is different every time.
series.series_description: Imani is thirty-one, a former ICU social worker who now runs a small private practice: she is on call to her clients for the first seventy-two hours after a death. Clients have already paid the retainer. They text the word 'now.' Imani arrives within ninety minutes with a soft bag containing one specific kit assembled for that client and that death. The magical-realist register: Imani's bag always contains exactly the right kit, even when she did not know which client would text first. She has stopped trying to understand. Each volume is one call, one client, one kit. The cases: a mother whose adult son died and the kit is a small blue blanket; a husband whose wife died and the kit is a candle and a notebook; a daughter whose mother died on a Tuesday and the kit is a thermos of a specific tea. The volumes are slow and quiet — the seventy-two hours are not edited; the long, strange days are honored. The long arc: Imani's father is in hospice. She has not yet packed her own kit. The volume's deeper question is what she will put in it.
series.reader_promise: There is a kit for the first seventy-two hours. Someone who knows what it is.
series.audience: Millennials 28–38 paying clients of private bereavement services, urban, vocabulary-rich
series.marketing_angle: Hospice-social-work iyashikei: the ninety-minute, one-bag, full-presence model of acute bereavement support.
series.long_arc_spine: A 31-year-old runs a 24/7 on-call bereavement service — clients can text her in the first 72 hours after a death and she shows up with a small kit of specific objects (a blanket, a candle, a notebook, a particular tea); each volume is one client and one kit.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The mother and the blue blanket. The kit fits. The mother sleeps eighteen hours.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The husband and the candle. The notebook stays unopened for two days, then opens to a page he can write on.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The daughter and the thermos of tea. The tea is one her mother loved. Imani had never met the mother.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A client cancels mid-text. Imani goes anyway. The kit, this time, is empty. The volume teaches when the kit is empty.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Imani sits in her own kitchen and packs a bag for her father. She does not know what to put in it. The volume ends with one item — his reading glasses.

series.series_logline: The bokken her brother left her is too heavy. It grows lighter by exactly what she lets herself feel.
series.series_description: Ren is twenty-four, a third-year practitioner at a small kendo dojo in Sacramento, and her older brother Jin — who taught her — died of suicide three years ago. His bokken was left to her. The first time she lifts it she nearly drops it; it is too heavy, by a measurable amount. Each volume she trains, lives, grieves, and the bokken gets lighter only by the specific weight of one thing she has finally allowed herself to feel: anger, then betrayal, then envy of her friends with living siblings, then her own fantasy of joining him, then her gratitude that she is still here. The register is shonen — there are training arcs, there is a dojo, there is a tournament. But the escalation ladder is not power; it is permission. The cases are the matches: each tournament fight is also a fight with one stage of her grief. She is bad at it. She gets better. The long arc: the bokken's weight is supposed to drop to zero. Ren is starting to wonder if she wants it to.
series.reader_promise: What you cannot lift gets lighter only by what you let yourself feel.
series.audience: Gen Z 22–28 martial-arts-and-grief intersection, suicide-loss survivors
series.marketing_angle: Vinland Saga's grief-of-the-violent-brother in a Gen-Z kendo school. Shonen with grief as the leveling system.
series.long_arc_spine: A 24-year-old kendo student inherits her dead older brother's bokken; the wooden sword gets heavier each year by the weight of one specific thing she has not yet grieved — and grows lighter only as she does.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The bokken. The first lift. The first lesson. Ren cannot hold it for the full kata. Her sensei does not explain why.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Anger. A practice match. Ren disqualifies herself by hitting too hard. The bokken loses 200 grams. She does not understand.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Betrayal. Jin left a note. Ren reads it for the first time. She does not train for a week. The bokken loses 300 grams without being touched.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Envy. A childhood friend brings her older brother to a tournament. Ren breaks down. The bokken loses 150 grams. Sensei smiles for the first time.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: First tournament. Ren is in the bracket. The bokken is still too heavy. She makes it to the second round. The first hint of what 'zero weight' would mean — and whether she wants it.

series.series_logline: Nia teaches a Tuesday-night qigong class for the bereaved. Each student arrives with one grief. Each leaves with one breath.
series.series_description: Nia is thirty, a certified qigong instructor who studied in Taipei before pivoting back to Portland to open a small studio. Tuesday nights are her bereavement class — eight mats, eight people, ninety minutes, by appointment only. The register is supernatural-everyday: when a student breathes the right form for their specific grief, a small shape briefly appears in the breath visible to Nia. She does not say so. Each volume is one student and one form: the widow whose breath form is 'embrace the moon,' the parent whose breath form is 'roots descend,' the sibling whose breath form is 'crane sweeps water,' the friend whose breath form is 'mountain holds rain.' Each form is real qigong, taught accurately, and each is matched to one specific kind of loss. The volumes are quiet, mostly breath, mostly mats. The long arc: Nia's teacher in Taipei has just died. Nia does not yet know which breath form is her own.
series.reader_promise: There is a breath form for your grief. It is real, and it has been taught for a thousand years.
series.audience: Millennials 28–38 somatic-practice-curious bereaved, alt-spiritual, body-literate
series.marketing_angle: Mushishi's apprenticeship register applied to real qigong, with one breath-form per grief.
series.long_arc_spine: A 30-year-old qigong instructor teaches a small Tuesday-night class for the recently bereaved; each volume one student arrives with one specific kind of grief and learns one specific breath form that holds them through it.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The widow and 'embrace the moon.' The breath holds. The widow stays for tea afterward. Nia sees the small shape.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The bereaved parent and 'roots descend.' Slower form. The parent cannot do it for three weeks. Then can.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The sibling and 'crane sweeps water.' The sibling laughs mid-form for the first time in a year.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The friend who lost a chosen-family member. 'Mountain holds rain.' The friend's grief is unrecognized by the friend's biological family. Nia's class is the only space.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Nia's teacher in Taipei dies. Nia does not teach the next Tuesday. The studio sits dark. The volume ends with the empty mat that would be hers.

series.series_logline: After her sister died, Sayuri took a year. The mountain temple has twelve rooms, one for each month. Each room teaches one form of grief.
series.series_description: Sayuri is thirty-four, an ER doctor in Seattle who lost her younger sister Mio to ovarian cancer and worked her shifts the same week of the funeral. Eight months later she is told, kindly, that she is going to break. She takes a year. A senior nurse hands her a card with one address on it: a small mountain temple two hours outside Kyoto with no website, run by a woman in her seventies who accepts one student per year. The temple has twelve rooms. Each room is dedicated to one form of grief: the Room of Anger, the Room of Bargaining, the Room of Bargaining-That-Doesn't-Stop, the Room of the One Question, the Room of Forgetting, the Room of Remembering. Each volume Sayuri stays in one room for one month and learns its one practice. The register is soft-fantasy — the temple bends time slightly, lets one practice take however long it needs — but no isekai, no other world; it is, factually, a mountain temple. The arc: the twelfth room is locked. The woman in her seventies has not been in it in forty years.
series.reader_promise: There is a room for each form of grief. Stay one month. Learn the practice. Move.
series.audience: Millennials 30–40 burnt-out caregivers and ER/medical-cohort bereaved
series.marketing_angle: Mushishi's apprenticeship register at a mountain temple, with grief as the curriculum.
