series.long_arc_spine: A 34-year-old burnt-out ER doctor takes a year off after her sister's death and apprentices at a mountain temple where each room is dedicated to one form of grief; each volume she stays in one room for one month and learns its specific practice.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Arrival. The Room of Anger. The practice: chop wood. Sayuri spends three weeks unable to lift the axe.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The Room of Bargaining. The practice: write the bargain you would have made, then burn it. Sayuri writes nine bargains.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The Room of the One Question. The practice: ask Mio one question, every day, aloud, in the empty room. Sayuri asks the same one for two weeks before changing it.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The Room of Forgetting. The hardest. The practice: forget one specific thing about Mio on purpose. Sayuri refuses for three weeks, then forgets her toothbrush color on purpose.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The Room of Remembering. The practice: write down one specific thing Mio said. Sayuri fills a notebook in nine days. The twelfth room is mentioned for the first time.

series.series_title: The Year of the Gold Seam
series.series_logline: Ako apprentices to a kintsugi master who only repairs what got broken in the year after the death.
series.series_description: Ako is twenty-seven, formerly a ceramics graduate student in Boston, now apprenticed to Onishi-sensei, a kintsugi master in Kyoto whose practice has one rule: she only repairs objects broken in the year after a client's loss. The first thing a bereaved person breaks after the death matters. The second matters more. Each volume one client brings one object: a teacup the husband dropped two weeks after his wife died, a vase the mother broke six months after her son died, a small clay figure the daughter shattered the morning of the one-year anniversary of her father's death. Onishi-sensei does not ask why the object broke. She asks Ako to repair it, with gold, in a specific seam. Each seam is different. The magical-realist register: when the seam is right, the client briefly sees the moment the object first arrived in the dead person's hands. They get to see how it was loved. The volumes are slow, mostly hands, mostly gold dust. The arc: Ako broke a small mug of her grandmother's the month after her grandmother died. She has not yet repaired it. Onishi-sensei is, slowly, asking her to.
series.reader_promise: What you break after the death can be repaired with gold and a witness.
series.audience: Gen Z 25–29 craft-and-grief intersection, hands-on grievers
series.marketing_angle: Apprenticeship-kintsugi for the year-after-loss demographic. Real craft, real gold dust.
series.long_arc_spine: A 27-year-old apprentices to a kintsugi master who repairs only objects broken in the year after a death; each volume one bereaved client brings one broken object and learns one specific gold seam.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The husband and the teacup. The first seam. The wife's hands receiving it as a gift from her mother-in-law in 1989.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The mother and the vase. Six months in. The son's hands at age eight, painting it in summer camp.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The daughter and the small clay figure. The one-year anniversary. The father's hands at twenty-two, before the daughter was born.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A client whose object is not yet broken — she brings a teapot she is afraid of breaking. Onishi-sensei accepts it. The teapot becomes the next year's first seam.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Ako's grandmother's mug. Ako opens the box. Onishi-sensei waits. The volume ends with the first cracked piece in Ako's palm.

series.series_logline: Hana's father drowned at sea. Now she can breathe underwater. The sea is giving back, slowly, one belonging at a time.
series.series_description: Hana is twenty-six and a working pearl diver in a small coastal town in Oregon. Her father drowned at sea when she was nineteen. The first time she dives after his death she stays underwater for eleven minutes. She did not mean to. Each volume she dives, finds one small object the sea has kept from one specific drowning death, and returns it to the family. The register is supernatural-everyday — the sea is, gently, an entity that holds; Hana is, gently, its courier. She does not seek the families; they find her by word of mouth, by Reddit thread, by NPR segment. The cases: a fishing boat's lost wedding ring, returned to the widow. A surfer's wallet, returned to the mother. A child's bracelet, returned to the parents who said they did not want it back, until they saw it. The volumes are slow, salt-smelling, mostly underwater. The mystery: Hana has not found anything of her father's. She knows what she is looking for. She does not know if she would return it.
series.reader_promise: What the sea took, the sea can return. One small belonging at a time.
series.audience: Gen Z 24–29 coastal-Pacific-Northwest, drowning-loss survivors, ocean-grief intersection
series.marketing_angle: Pearl-diver iyashikei meets ocean-as-courier supernatural register. Ghibli-water, A24-coast.
series.long_arc_spine: A 26-year-old pearl diver in coastal Oregon can hold her breath impossibly long underwater after her father drowned at sea; she discovers the ocean is keeping small belongings from each of its dead and she can, slowly, return them.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The first object. A wedding ring. The widow lives in Astoria. Hana drives the ring up the coast. The widow does not cry until the next morning.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The surfer's wallet. The mother lives in San Diego. Hana flies. The wallet still has a coffee-shop loyalty card with one stamp left.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The child's bracelet. The parents say no. Hana keeps it for a year. The parents call. Hana drives back.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A fisherman comes to Hana and asks her to find his brother's tackle box. Hana dives. The tackle box is found. The brother is found too — not alive, but where he was.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Hana dives in the bay where her father drowned. She stays under for forty minutes. She finds something. She does not yet say what. The mystery opens.

series.series_logline: After three tours and the loss of his best friend, Wes teaches shop class. The teenagers arrive broken. He teaches them to build something that holds.
series.series_description: Wes is thirty-eight, a Marine veteran with three combat deployments and complicated grief from the loss of his best friend Hector in 2014. He moves to rural eastern Washington and takes the only job available: shop class at the local high school. He does not want it. He is bad at speeches and worse at feelings. He is, however, very good at hands. Each volume one student arrives — a girl whose father died in a logging accident, a boy whose mother left, a non-binary kid being harassed for the third year running, a senior who is failing every other class, a freshman who has not spoken to anyone since middle school. Wes does not counsel. He hands them a piece of wood, a square, a tool, and a plan. Each volume teaches one specific skill: a mortise-and-tenon joint, a hand-cut dovetail, a planed surface, a glue-up, a finish. The skill is the lesson. The register is realist, fully — no magic, no register-bending, only sawdust, school-day light, and a man with a grief he is, slowly, putting down. The arc is Wes's own woodworking project, started for Hector, never finished, in his garage.
series.reader_promise: A grown man with a grief can teach you to build something that holds, without ever once asking you about your feelings.
series.audience: Millennials and Gen X 32–48 veteran-grief, rural-grief, and teacher-bereaved
comp_titles: All Quiet on the Western Front
series.marketing_angle: Friday Night Lights coach-as-shop-teacher, for the Marine-veteran-grief and rural-teen-grief intersection.
series.long_arc_spine: A 38-year-old combat veteran with complicated grief reluctantly takes a job teaching shop class at a rural high school; each volume one student arrives broken and one specific skill he teaches them turns the breaking into a different shape.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: First semester. The girl whose father died in the logging accident. The skill: a mortise-and-tenon. She finishes a small box. Wes says four sentences total in two weeks.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The boy whose mother left. The skill: a hand-cut dovetail. The boy is bad at it for a month. Then he is not.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The non-binary kid. The skill: a planed surface. Wes calls them by their right name from day one. The shop class is the only class where they unmask.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The senior who is failing everything. The skill: a glue-up. The senior is teaching themselves to want to be good at something.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The freshman who hasn't spoken. The skill: a finish. The finish is silence — sand, wipe, repeat. The freshman speaks one full sentence by week eleven.

series.series_logline: Mei inherits her grandfather's sabre. It does not cut. It sings. Only the bereaved can hear it.
series.series_description: Mei is twenty-three, a fencing student at a small Seattle salle, and her grandfather Quan — who fenced for sixty years — has died. He leaves her his sabre. The first time she lifts it in salle she realizes it has no edge: the blade is mirror-smooth, the point is rounded. It is, technically, a practice sabre. The second time she lifts it she hears a very low, sustained, slightly metallic note. Nobody else hears it. The third time, a fencer across the strip starts crying without warning. Mei realizes the sabre sings, and only the bereaved hear it. Each volume she practices, fences, and encounters one new bereaved person — a competitor, a referee, a stranger on a bus carrying a foil case — and the sabre sings, and she learns one specific thing about the way each person carries their dead. The register is soft-fantasy — power-escalation, in this series, is the increasing precision of the sabre's note, from a generic hum to specific people's names. The arc: when the sabre sings her grandfather's name, what does she do? She has not yet heard it sing his name. She is not sure she wants to.
series.reader_promise: The blade does not cut. It names. Only your dead get named first.
series.audience: Gen Z 22–27 fencing-and-grief intersection, grandfather/grandmother bereaved
series.marketing_angle: Mushishi's listening-as-magic register applied to Olympic fencing and grandparent grief.
series.long_arc_spine: A 23-year-old fencing student in Seattle inherits her grandfather's sabre and discovers it does not cut — but it sings, in a frequency only the bereaved can hear, and each volume one new bereaved person hears it and is changed.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The salle. The first lift. The note. The competitor crying. Mei learns the rule.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: A tournament. A referee whose son died. The sabre sings the son's name. The referee disqualifies herself from the match, calmly, and thanks Mei.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: A stranger on the bus with a foil case. Mei does not know the stranger. The note is precise — a woman's name. The stranger says it aloud, weeping, in public, for the first time.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: An old friend of her grandfather's. The note is Quan-adjacent — close to him but not him. Mei learns the note can name the unsaid relationships.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Mei practices alone. The sabre hums, then begins to form a note. The note is starting to be her grandfather's name. Mei lowers the sabre. The volume ends with the unfinished note.

series.series_logline: After her teacher died, Etsuko visits seven kyudo dojos in North America. One shot per range. Each range is listening.
series.series_description: Etsuko is thirty-five, a fifth-dan kyudo practitioner whose teacher Akira-sensei died unexpectedly in Kyoto last winter. As her grief practice she plans a year-long trip: she will visit seven kyudo dojos across North America — Seattle, Vancouver, Berkeley, Boulder, Chicago, Toronto, Montreal — and take one ceremonial shot in each. The register is supernatural-everyday: each range is haunted by one specific unfinished form left there by a previous practitioner, and Etsuko, as a fifth-dan, can feel them. Her arrow either completes the form — and the range exhales — or her arrow refuses to fly, and the range asks her to come back. The volumes are slow, mostly silence, mostly breath. The dojos vary — old wooden floors, indoor concrete, an outdoor range at a lake. The arc: the seventh range is in Montreal, where Akira-sensei once taught a workshop in 1998. She does not yet know what her sensei left there. She is not sure her arrow will fly.
series.reader_promise: Each range your teacher touched is still waiting for one arrow. You are the one carrying it.
series.audience: Millennials 30–42 traditional-martial-arts practitioners, teacher-bereaved
series.marketing_angle: Mushishi's pilgrimage register applied to kyudo, with one arrow per range across a year.
series.long_arc_spine: A 35-year-old kyudo practitioner whose teacher died travels to seven kyudo dojos across North America to take one shot in each; each dojo's range is haunted by a different unfinished form, and her arrow either lands or asks her to come back.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Seattle. The shot lands. The range exhales. Etsuko meets the senior practitioner who has been carrying the unfinished form for fifteen years.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Vancouver. The shot does not fly. The range asks her to come back in spring.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Berkeley. The shot lands. The form is named. A young practitioner with the sensei's grief is, briefly, lifted.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Boulder. The range is outdoor. The shot lands at altitude. The form completes in wind.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Chicago. Halfway through the year. Etsuko is tired. The shot lands but the form is partial. Etsuko realizes one of these ranges will keep her overnight. She prepares to stay.

series.series_logline: Marisol's husband ran a boxing gym. He died. The gym is still open at 11 p.m. The heavy bags know.
series.series_description: Marisol is thirty-six and her husband Dom ran a small no-frills boxing gym in Bridgeport that opened at 6 a.m. and closed at midnight, every day, for twenty years. Dom died of a heart attack on a Tuesday. Marisol inherits the gym and does the only sensible thing: she keeps it open. She is not a boxer. She is a labor lawyer. She works the late shift — 7 p.m. to midnight — because nobody else will. Each volume one late-night client arrives with one specific grief: a man whose son died in Afghanistan, a woman whose sister overdosed, a teenager whose father is in prison and has just become uncontactable. The clients hit the heavy bags. The bags swing, in some volumes, on their own — when the grief is named, in the right rhythm, with the right combination. Marisol does not box, but she counts rounds. She wraps hands. She listens. The volumes are slow, sweat-smelling, fluorescent. The arc: Dom's own bag is still there, in the back, untouched. Marisol has not yet put on gloves.
series.reader_promise: Your grief has a combination. The bag will swing when you find it.
series.audience: Millennials 30–42 working-class urban bereaved, somatic-grief literacy
series.marketing_angle: Million Dollar Baby register meets late-night iyashikei boxing-gym manga.
series.long_arc_spine: A 36-year-old widow inherits her late husband's boxing gym in a small Connecticut town; the late-night clients arrive carrying specific griefs, and the gym's heavy bags swing on their own when the grief is named in the right combination.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The father whose son died in Afghanistan. Combination: jab-cross-hook. The bag swings on its own at the third hook.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The woman whose sister overdosed. She cannot make a fist for ninety minutes. Marisol wraps her hands four times before she can.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The teenager whose father is in prison. He is bad at boxing. The bag swings anyway. Marisol learns the bags know teenager-grief differently.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A repeat client. The father from vol_1 returns. The combination is different. He is naming the second son he didn't know he was grieving.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Marisol puts on gloves. She does not yet hit Dom's bag. She hits another. The bag does not swing on its own. She understands what Dom's bag is waiting for. The volume ends with her unwrapped hand.

series.series_logline: Kit runs ultramarathons. Her clients pay her to carry one name for the distance. The name decides how it's carried.
series.series_description: Kit is twenty-six, an ultra-runner from Flagstaff who ran her first 50K in college and has done one 100-mile race a year since her brother Cory died. She has stumbled into a small side practice: clients pay her to carry one name on a long run. The name is taped to the underside of her bib. Kit chants it, hums it, or simply listens to it for hours of running. The register is supernatural-everyday: each name reveals, by the end of the race, one specific thing about how it wants to be carried — some names want to be sung, some want to be cursed at on a long climb, some want to be whispered through the dawn miles, some want to be carried in silence and acknowledged at the finish. Each volume is one race and one name. The cases vary: a grandfather's name carried at Western States, a chosen-family member's name at a small local 50K, a name that turns out not to be the client's dead but the client's not-yet-born child's, never born. The volumes are mostly pavement, mostly trail, mostly silence. The arc: Cory's name is on every run, on the underside of Kit's own bib, but she has not yet taken a paid commission to carry him. She does not feel she has the right.
series.reader_promise: Your dead have a name and a distance. Someone is willing to carry both.
series.audience: Gen Z 24–29 endurance-athlete bereaved, suicide-loss and pregnancy-loss survivors
series.marketing_angle: Born to Run meets Frieren's vigil register. Ultra-running manga where every name is one race.
series.long_arc_spine: A 26-year-old ultra-runner carries the names of the dead on her long runs; she takes paid commissions to run a marathon or longer carrying one specific name, and each volume is one race, one name, one specific way the name asks to be carried.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The grandfather at Western States. The name wants to be sung. Kit sings for thirty miles.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: A chosen-family member at a small local 50K. The name wants to be cursed at on a climb. Kit yells.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: A whispered name through the dawn miles of a 100K in Vermont. Kit barely speaks. The client cries at the finish anyway.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The not-yet-born child. The client is a woman in her forties who lost a pregnancy at twenty weeks. The name was never registered. Kit runs and the name names itself by mile sixty.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: A new client offers to commission her to carry Cory. Kit refuses. The volume ends with Kit on a midnight run, alone, with Cory's name on the underside of her own bib, and the first hint of what would happen if she said yes.

series.series_logline: Sky keeps her door unlocked four hours a night for her chosen family; the apartment is starting to count the ones it loves.
series.series_description: Sky is twenty-six, queer, and a community-college academic advisor in Oakland. After her own family of origin went silent at twenty-one, she promised the small circle she built in college that her door would be unlocked from 7 to 11 every weeknight — anyone could land. By the third year, the apartment knows the regulars. The radiator clicks for Theo's allergies. The kitchen light goes soft when Marisol comes in after a bad shift. The couch gets one extra cushion the night Jules's father visits town. Sky doesn't fight it. She learns the apartment's grammar, which is the same grammar as her own nervous system: who is allowed close, what proximity is safe, what the body permits at the door. Each volume a new person tries to land — an estranged sibling, an ex's new partner, the bio-cousin who Sky hasn't seen since the funeral — and the apartment decides, slow and specific, what kind of welcome the room can hold. Frieren-tender. Heartstopper-warm. A door that has read every nervous-system theory Sky owns.
series.reader_promise: Your chosen family has somewhere to land, and the room is on your side.
series.audience: Gen Z 24–29 queer urban chosen-family hosts with nervous-system literacy
series.marketing_angle: Heartstopper for the apartment that learns who you let in.
series.long_arc_spine: A 26-year-old keeps her apartment door unlocked from 7 to 11 every weeknight so her chosen-family group chat has somewhere to land; she slowly discovers the apartment itself is keeping a roster of who has slept on her couch and adding them to a quiet, growing protection.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Theo's allergies. Marisol's bad shift. The first week the apartment runs itself. Sky names it 'the room.'
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Jules's father in town. The couch grows one cushion. Sky learns the room makes a place for the people she loves to grieve their own people.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The first stranger — a friend-of-a-friend in crisis. The door takes ninety seconds longer to open. Sky learns 'safe' is the room's decision, not hers.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: An ex's new partner asks to come in. The door does not open. Sky sits with the no on the inside floor and writes the ex a kind text.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Sky's estranged younger sister at the door. The radiator does not click. Sky has to decide whether the room learns a new person, or whether the room is right.

series.series_logline: Five friends. One kitchen table. A notebook that knows what each of them came to be told.
series.series_description: Lev is twenty-five, a junior brand designer in Brooklyn, and the one in the group chat with the biggest apartment, which means Sunday at seven is at Lev's. The five of them — Naya, Bea, Diego, Mira, and Lev — eat the same rotating menu and drink one bottle of the cheapest decent wine and write in a small green notebook that Lev's grandmother gave him before she died. The notebook lives on the table. Anyone can pick it up. The strange thing is that the sentence each person needs to hear that week is already written there before the dinner — in handwriting that belongs to none of them. They learn not to talk about it. They learn to read what is in the book at the right time. Each volume a different sentence belongs to a different friend, and the long arc is the Sunday someone new is brought to the table and the book writes them a sentence too. Spy x Family wholesome. Heartstopper specific. Quietly enchanted.
series.reader_promise: Your weekly group has a record that knows what each of you came for.
series.audience: Gen Z 24–28 first-job urban friend-group anchors
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: Spy x Family for the Sunday dinner that's the only thing holding your week.
series.long_arc_spine: A 25-year-old hosts the same five-person Sunday dinner group chat in person every week for four years; a small notebook on the table writes down, in nobody's handwriting, the one thing each person came to be told.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Naya's promotion she didn't take. The book says 'you can change your mind on Tuesday.' She changes it Wednesday.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Bea's mother is sick. The book says 'tell Diego.' Diego's father had the same diagnosis.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Diego brings his new boyfriend. The book writes a sentence for the boyfriend. Lev does not show it to him until vol 4.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The boyfriend reads his sentence. Decides to stay in the group. The notebook now holds six.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Mira moves to Berlin. The book writes her last sentence. The chair is kept anyway. The book keeps writing for her.

series.series_logline: Iris runs the library lost-and-found; the objects belong to friendships that misplaced themselves.
series.series_description: Iris is twenty-two, a junior at the state university, and works twenty hours a week at the public library lost-and-found, which is a fluorescent room behind the circulation desk. She is socially anxious in the textbook way: she has rehearsed the sentence 'do you have an ID' two thousand times. Each volume she returns one object to one person, and each object is the residue of a friendship that the person let go of. A high-school best friend's mixtape. A roommate's plant. A camp friendship bracelet. Iris does not lecture. She gives the object back. She watches the person decide whether to text the friend, or not. Some texts get sent. Some don't. The long arc is the object that ends up in the lost-and-found that is Iris's own — a sketch from a former best friend in high school — and what Iris decides to do with it. Quiet, anxious, tender. Heartstopper but for the friend you used to be inseparable from.
series.reader_promise: The friendship you let go of left its receipt at the lost-and-found.
series.audience: Gen Z 20–24 university socially anxious readers with one specific lost friendship
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library, but the librarian is twenty-two and just as anxious as you are.
series.long_arc_spine: A socially anxious 22-year-old works the lost-and-found counter at a community library; each lost object turns out to belong to a friendship that is also lost, and returning the object returns the friendship — slowly, awkwardly, never on schedule.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The mixtape. The customer is now thirty. She does not call the friend. She listens in the parking lot.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The roommate's plant. The two roommates haven't spoken in three years. The plant is dying. Iris finds the right water schedule.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The camp bracelet. The friend is dead. The customer does not know. Iris tells her by being quiet long enough.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Iris's own sketch arrives in the lost-and-found. Iris does not check the name on the slip yet.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Iris reads the slip. Calls the former best friend. Says hello. The episode ends mid-sentence. The library closes.

series.series_logline: Two strangers, one lease, twelve months of small rituals. By month six they need a name for it.
series.series_description: Tess is twenty-four and just out of a four-year relationship; Owen is twenty-six and just out of grad school. They both need a room. The Craigslist sublease is one year, ground-floor Somerville, two bedrooms, shared kitchen. By month two they have invented the Wednesday-night-pasta protocol and the Sunday-quiet-hour and the unspoken rule about which of them buys the toilet paper. By month four they are texting each other from the same couch. By month six neither of them is dating anyone, and they're not dating each other either — they are doing the thirty-six small rituals that look like a relationship and feel like a friendship and have no name in the language. Each volume is one month. The series is twelve. The long arc is what they call it when the lease ends. Romance in the slow-build register. No sex on page until they decide there's a name. Honey-lemon-soda warmth with grown-up rent.
series.reader_promise: The friendship-that-isn't has rituals that count, even before they have a name.
series.audience: Gen Z + young-Mill 23–28 first-apartment urban roommates in slow-build adjacency
series.marketing_angle: Honey Lemon Soda meets the New York Times 36 Questions, on a Craigslist sublease.
series.long_arc_spine: Two ex-strangers move in together on a one-year sublease and accidentally invent a thirty-six-step relationship that has no name; the series tracks each month of the year, the small rituals they invent, and the question of what to call it when the lease ends.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: January. The lease signs. The first Wednesday-night pasta. Owen does not eat onions; Tess learns this on Tuesday.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: February. The first argument — about whose turn it is to text the landlord. They invent the Sunday-quiet-hour.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: March. Tess's ex texts. Owen makes the pasta alone that Wednesday. Tess comes home anyway.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: April. Owen's mother visits. She asks if they're dating. Neither of them answers. The mother takes both of them to brunch.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: May. They build a bookshelf together. The shelf has both of their books. The shelf does not have a category for what they are.

series.series_logline: June found her seventh-grade chat logs. Her present-day friends start writing to their thirteen-year-old selves, and the thirteen-year-old selves write back.
series.series_description: June is thirty-three, a school librarian in Portland, and cleaning out her parents' house when she finds a box of printed-out AIM chat logs her mother saved from 2006. June scans them and sends them to the group chat that now contains the same four friends, twenty years older. They read them out loud over wine. Then the strange thing: when they each text a sentence in reply to one of the printed lines, the next time they open the chat, the seventh-grade version of the friend has answered. The series is each friend's correspondence with their younger self — each volume one friend, one specific knot they did not get to soothe at thirteen. The arc is the one friend in the chat who died at nineteen, whose seventh-grade logs are in the box too, and whether the chat lets her write back. Slow, very specific, Millennial-tender.
series.reader_promise: Your thirteen-year-old self is still on AIM and they have one thing to ask you.
series.audience: Millennials 30–38 with a saved chat-log box and a long friend-group
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: Before the Coffee Gets Cold for the Millennial group chat that was originally a 2006 AIM thread.
series.long_arc_spine: A 33-year-old finds her old middle-school AIM chat logs printed out in a box at her parents' house; she re-reads them with her present-day group chat — and the past versions of her friends start writing back.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Brett's logs. Brett at thirteen was being bullied for his stutter. Brett at thirty-three writes him the sentence he needed.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Layla's logs. Layla at thirteen was the bully. Layla at thirty-three apologizes. Layla at thirteen takes a week to reply.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: June's logs. June at thirteen was queer and didn't know the word. June at thirty-three teaches her the word.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Devon's logs. Devon at thirteen wanted to be a chef. Devon at thirty-three is an accountant. They negotiate.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The dead friend's logs surface. The chat goes silent for two days. Brett types the first sentence. The reply takes a week.

series.series_logline: Cami can't tell Jordan she likes her, but she can add the song. The playlist is up to forty-seven.
series.series_description: Cami is twenty, a Black queer second-year at a small liberal-arts college in upstate New York, and the newest member of the all-femmes a cappella group on campus. She is sure they made a mistake letting her in. Jordan is the alto she sits next to, a junior, easy-shouldered, kind. Cami cannot say a word to Jordan that does not get rehearsed in advance. The first week she adds a song to the shared 'rehearsal warmups' playlist that means 'I'm here because of you.' Jordan adds a song back. By month two the playlist is the relationship. The series is one school year. Each volume is one month and three songs and one small thing Cami learns to say out loud. The long arc is the spring concert where Cami has the solo — and whether she sings it to Jordan, or to herself, or to both. Shojo-tender. Honey Lemon Soda. Heartstopper at a women's college.
series.reader_promise: The playlist is saying it for you until you can say it yourself.
series.audience: Gen Z 19–23 queer college students with severe imposter-syndrome dating energy
series.marketing_angle: Heartstopper at an a cappella group, in a shared playlist that becomes the relationship.
series.long_arc_spine: A 20-year-old college sophomore with severe imposter syndrome falls for a junior in her a cappella group; they invent a shared playlist that is the only place she can say how she feels — and slowly, song by song, builds the version of herself that can say it out loud.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: September. First rehearsal. The first song goes on the playlist. Jordan adds Solange. Cami does not breathe for a verse.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: October. The Halloween concert. Cami's costume is a disaster. Jordan fixes the seam. The playlist gets the song from the costume.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: November. Cami's family Thanksgiving. The playlist is the only home she has on a hard week. She adds a song. Jordan answers same day.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: December. The winter concert. Cami's mother comes. The solo audition is announced. Cami auditions out of spite and gets it.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: January. The new semester. Jordan asks Cami to coffee. The playlist freezes for a week. Then a song goes up that is not a code.

series.series_logline: Six interns. One printer. Eleven months of figuring out the boss is the imposter, not them.
series.series_description: Anya is twenty-three and one of six unpaid interns at a small nonprofit in DC. They all share one basement office with one window that does not open. Each of them arrived ninety-percent sure they were the dumbest person hired. The series tracks the eleven-month internship: one volume per month, one new shared discovery per month, one shared protection the six of them build for each other. By month four they have a private group chat called THE PRINTER ROOM. By month seven they realize the executive director is gaslighting all six of them and have begun comparing notes. By month nine three of them have job offers and are reading each other's negotiation emails. By month eleven the unit they have become is the actual job. Found-family slow build. Workplace ensemble. No villain except the org chart. Heartstopper energy if Heartstopper had a Slack.
series.reader_promise: The basement coworkers are the first family who agrees you're not the problem.
series.audience: Gen Z 22–26 first-job interns with imposter syndrome and a group chat
series.marketing_angle: Heartstopper meets early-career Severance, in the basement of a flailing nonprofit.
series.long_arc_spine: Six unpaid interns at a flailing nonprofit share one basement office, one printer, and the slow collective discovery that they are not the problem — the org is — and that they have already become each other's first real coworkers.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Day one. Six introductions. Anya assumes everyone else went to a better school. By Friday she learns three of them didn't go at all.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The printer breaks. They solve it together. THE PRINTER ROOM group chat is named.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The first 'are we doing this right?' moment — Mateo's manager yells at him for something his manager forgot. The six of them write the timeline together.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The first 'we are being gaslit' moment. They compare what each was told the budget was. Three different numbers.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Halftime check-in. Anya cries in the bathroom and Priya holds the door. Anya names the imposter feeling out loud.

series.series_logline: Liana's grandmother left her a shrine. It gives you the courage for one specific no. The line outside is forming.
series.series_description: Liana is twenty-four, mixed Filipino-American, a first-year high-school art teacher in San Diego. Her grandmother, who raised her, dies in the spring and leaves her a small wooden shrine the size of a shoebox, which sat above the family kitchen for forty years. The shrine has one function: if you sit in front of it for ten quiet minutes and speak the no you cannot say, you walk out with the strength to say it once. Just once. Liana opens the shrine to anyone who knows about it. The first week it is two of her seniors. By month two it is half her chosen-family group chat. Each volume is one person's no — to a boss, a parent, a partner, a friend, a version of themselves. The long arc is Liana's own no, which the shrine has been waiting for since she inherited it. Heartstopper warmth. The Apothecary Diaries' competent-protagonist register. A small wooden box that does not lecture.
series.reader_promise: Your one specific no is sitting in front of the shrine that has been waiting for you.
series.audience: Gen Z 22–28 women and queer first-job teachers and caregivers with one specific no overdue
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: Apothecary Diaries meets Heartstopper — the grandmother's shrine that gives Gen Z the strength to say no once.
series.long_arc_spine: A 24-year-old high-school art teacher inherits a tiny grandmother's shrine that grants strength to say one no per visit; she opens it to her students and chosen family — each episode is one person's first no — and slowly learns the shrine has a ledger she is also on.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Maya the senior says no to her parents about pre-med. The shrine clicks once. Liana sits with her after.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Pris from the group chat says no to her landlord. The shrine clicks twice. Pris is shaking. The chat throws her a pizza.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Liana's vice principal asks her to teach an extra class without pay. The shrine clicks for Liana. She doesn't say her no yet.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A stranger — Maya's mother — comes to the shrine. The shrine works for people Liana hasn't met. The mother says no to her own marriage.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Liana finally says her own no — to the inheritance condition her grandmother left. The shrine closes. Liana opens it again.

series.series_logline: Ren finds missing closeness for a living. Her magnifying glass shows her every time she's undersold herself this week.
series.series_description: Ren is twenty-six and runs a one-room detective agency on the second floor above a Thai grocery in Queens. Her clients are not looking for missing people — they're looking for missing closeness. The roommate who stopped saying hello. The cousin who unfollowed. The friend who has the same five inside jokes with someone else now. Ren's grandmother's brass magnifying glass — which Ren keeps on her desk and which is the agency's only mystical asset — shows her two things: where the closeness leaked, and where Ren herself is undervaluing what she did this week. Each volume is one case and one shrinking moment Ren has to face. Mystery-cozy. Imposter-syndrome-cozy. Apothecary-warm. The cases are small and specific and not preachy. The arc is Ren's own missing closeness — with her brother — and whether the glass works on the woman who holds it.
series.reader_promise: The closeness that quietly leaked has an address, and the detective is the one with imposter syndrome.
series.audience: Gen Z 24–28 first-job urban with one specific 'where did that friendship go' question and chronic underselling
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: Apothecary Diaries meets a Queens detective agency for the friendship that quietly faded.
series.long_arc_spine: A 26-year-old who runs a tiny one-room detective agency for missing relationships — not missing people, missing closeness — solves a different case each week with a magnifying glass that shows her her own worth at the size she undervalued it that week.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The roommate who stopped saying hello. Ren finds the moment — a passive-aggressive Venmo memo. The glass shows Ren undercharging for the case.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The unfollowed cousin. Ren finds the political fight that wasn't said out loud. The glass shows Ren second-guessing the bill.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The five-inside-jokes friend. Ren maps the new friend group. The glass shows Ren accepting a free coffee in lieu of payment.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The mother who texts only on holidays. Ren takes the case from a man older than her. The glass shows Ren refusing to charge by the hour.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Ren's brother is the case Ren cannot take. She hires a junior detective. The glass shows Ren admitting she needs help.

series.series_title: Books From the Kids Who Grew Up
series.series_logline: Wren is twelve, helps at the library, and just found a box of books written by adult versions of her classmates.
series.series_description: Wren is twelve, in seventh grade, in a small town in central Pennsylvania, and the favored volunteer at the public library's children's section. In the returns box one Saturday she finds eleven books that should not be there — none of them are in the catalog. Each is by an adult name she recognizes from her own seventh-grade class, twenty years from now: 'How I Stopped Apologizing,' 'The Year I Said No to Soccer,' 'My Mother Was Wrong About Me.' Wren does not show the librarian. She quietly lends one to each classmate. Each volume is one classmate finding their own future memoir and deciding what to do with the news. The long arc is whether Wren's own future book is in the box — and what she does if it is. Spy x Family wholesome, no parents-as-spies — just a seventh-grader and the eleven books that were waiting. Gen Alpha middle-grade register.
series.reader_promise: The adult version of you wrote a book about the year you're in right now.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 11–13 middle-school readers with one self-image they're already negotiating
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: Spy x Family for the middle-school library where the future-you already published a memoir.
series.long_arc_spine: A 12-year-old librarian's-helper finds a returns box of books from the future — books written by adult versions of the local kids — and quietly lends them out to the kids they belong to, who decide what to do with the news.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Maya gets 'The Year I Said No to Soccer.' Maya hates soccer. Maya tells her parents on Tuesday. The book updates.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Jonah gets 'My Mother Was Wrong About Me.' Jonah is twelve. Jonah does not show his mother. Jonah keeps it under the mattress.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Sasha gets 'How I Stopped Apologizing.' Sasha doesn't notice she apologizes. Wren teaches her by counting.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A new kid arrives at school. A new book appears in the box. Wren learns the box updates.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Wren's own book is in the box: 'The Year I Stopped Helping at the Library.' Wren does not read it yet.

series.series_logline: Andre clears estates for a living. Each one has a relative left over, with one sentence still to say.
series.series_description: Andre is thirty-five, divorced, mildly autistic, and runs a one-man estate-sale clearance service in Atlanta. He prefers houses to people. Each estate he clears has, somewhere in it, one relative who never said goodbye — sometimes the dead, sometimes the estranged-and-still-living, sometimes the one who left at twenty and never came back. The relative speaks to Andre once, through one object in the house, in the half-hour before the sale opens to the public. Andre delivers the sentence to whoever needs it — a daughter, an ex, a cousin — and then closes the sale. He does not advertise. People find him. Each volume is one estate. The arc is Andre's own brother, who left at nineteen and is alive somewhere, and whose estate has not yet appeared but is being assembled, slowly, in Andre's own kitchen. Quiet seinen. Heartstopper for the man who didn't know he was lonely.
series.reader_promise: The relative who never said goodbye is waiting in one object, at exactly the right estate.
series.audience: Millennial 32–40 quiet readers with one estranged-or-dead relative whose last sentence is missing
series.marketing_angle: Mushishi for the man who clears your grandmother's house and hears the sentence she meant to say.
series.long_arc_spine: A 35-year-old socially anxious man who runs a small estate-sale clearance service learns each estate carries one relative who never said goodbye; the relative speaks once, through an object, before the sale closes — and Andre is the only one who can hear them.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: An estate in Decatur. The grandmother's lampshade. The sentence is for her daughter — 'I forgive you for the prom.'
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: An estate in Marietta. The father's tool chest. The sentence is for his son — 'I knew you were gay. I was waiting.'
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: An estate in Buckhead, of a man who is still alive in a memory facility. His record player speaks. The sentence is for his wife.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The estate of Andre's old neighbor. The neighbor was Andre's mother's friend. The sentence is for Andre — about Andre's brother.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Andre's own kitchen begins to fill with objects nobody put there. Andre does not yet open the drawer.

series.series_logline: Henry runs the diner. The same six people sit Saturdays. Nine years. He's never said the word family.
series.series_description: Henry is thirty-eight, divorced, with a low-key social anxiety he calls 'preferring the grill,' and runs Henry's Diner on the main street of a small town in upstate New York. Saturday breakfast is the same six regulars — a retired teacher, a postal carrier, a single father and his nine-year-old, a widower, and a community-college sophomore who shows up because her grandfather used to. Henry has cooked their Saturday breakfast for nine years. He knows what each of them will order before they sit. Each volume is one regular and one quiet year of the small thing Henry did that held them. Henry never says the word family. The arc is the Saturday two of the six don't come, and what Henry decides about whether to call. Quiet seinen, slice-of-life, the most explicitly chosen-family series in the cluster. Frieren-tender. American small-town.
series.reader_promise: The diner has been doing the work of family for nine years. You only have to sit.
series.audience: Millennial + Gen X 32–55 small-town readers with one weekly anchor that quietly counts
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: Sweet Bean Paste for the small-town diner where Saturday breakfast is the only thing holding six lives.
series.long_arc_spine: A 38-year-old socially anxious diner cook in upstate New York runs the same Saturday breakfast for the same six regulars for nine years; each volume is one regular's life under the cook's quiet stewardship — and the slow becoming of a family the cook never named.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The retired teacher's Saturday. Her order has changed once in nine years. Henry fixed it without saying.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The postal carrier's Saturday. He's been carrying mail for forty years. Henry pours him decaf for the first time after the cardiac scare.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The single father and the nine-year-old. The nine-year-old learns to make pancakes at the counter. Henry teaches by silence.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The widower's Saturday. He stops coming for three weeks. Henry visits. He comes back.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The community-college sophomore's grandfather has died. She sits in the grandfather's chair. Henry serves her grandfather's order.

series.series_logline: Diego is forty-one. His twenty-three Gen Z reports call him 'office uncle.' The badge in his desk shows him why.
series.series_description: Diego is forty-one, a senior project manager at a mid-size healthcare tech company in Charlotte, quiet, never the loudest person in the room. He has somehow become the man twenty-three Gen Z direct reports call 'office uncle.' He didn't ask for it. In his bottom desk drawer is the badge of his own uncle Hector — the one who showed up at every school event Diego's father missed — and when Diego holds the badge in his hand, just for himself, he sees one specific thing about each report: which adult was missing for them at fifteen. Each volume is one report and one small adult-missing-at-fifteen thing Diego quietly stands in for — a notarization, a moving van, a 'come to dinner Sunday.' The arc is the report whose uncle was Diego, twenty years ago — a kid from Diego's old neighborhood — and what Diego decides to do with the recognition. Quiet seinen. Spy x Family without the spies. The dad-energy show for Millennial managers.
series.reader_promise: The office uncle sees which adult you were missing at fifteen, and quietly fills in.
series.audience: Millennial 35–45 middle-managers who have become accidental Gen Z mentors
series.marketing_angle: Spy x Family but the dad-energy is a forty-one-year-old PM doing the thing your uncle didn't.
series.long_arc_spine: A 41-year-old quiet middle-manager has slowly become 'the office uncle' to two dozen Gen Z direct reports; an old company badge in his desk lets him see, once per report, the family they did not have — and quietly fill in for it without ever saying so.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Priya needs a notary. The badge shows Diego her father was deployed her whole childhood. Diego notarizes. Doesn't say.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Marc needs a moving van. The badge shows Diego his stepfather refused to lift his stuff at sixteen. Diego rents the van.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Jasmine cries in a meeting. The badge shows Diego her mother is dying. Diego covers her quarter. Doesn't say.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A new hire, Eduardo, is Diego's old neighborhood. The badge shows Diego that Eduardo's missing uncle was Diego himself, twenty years ago.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Diego invites Eduardo to dinner Sunday. The badge is in the drawer. Diego does not need it anymore.

series.series_logline: Mara maps your living cousins as a star chart. The atlas tells you which cousin to write to first.
series.series_description: Mara is thirty-four, second-generation Filipino-American, a genealogist with a one-room office in Daly City. Her side business: for a hundred and twenty dollars she will research your cousins — second, third, removed, scattered — and produce a small hand-drawn star atlas with each cousin placed by relational distance, age, and one detail of who they used to be to you. The atlas glows, just faintly, on one cousin per chart — the one the client should write to before the year ends. Mara delivers the atlas. The clients write. The cousins, slowly, write back. Each volume is one chart, one cousin found, one reply. The arc is Mara's own atlas, which has been on her wall for three years with one steady glow on a cousin she has not yet written. Soft fantasy. Heritage register. Quiet seinen with a global diaspora. The most explicitly extended-family series in the cluster.
series.reader_promise: Your living cousin who would answer a letter is already on the atlas.
series.audience: Millennial 30–42 diaspora and mixed-heritage adults with one cousin overdue a letter
series.marketing_angle: Mushishi for the diaspora — the atlas of cousins you haven't yet written to.
series.long_arc_spine: A 34-year-old Filipino-American genealogist runs a small mail-order service that maps a client's living cousin network onto a star atlas; each chart reveals one cousin the client should write to before the year ends — and the cousins, slowly, are writing back.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: A client in Sacramento gets her atlas. The glow is on a cousin in Cebu. She writes. Reply takes ten weeks.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: A Korean-American client gets his atlas. The glow is on a cousin in Toronto he last saw at twelve. They meet on Zoom.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: A Black client from Mara's neighborhood gets her atlas. The glow is on a cousin in Houston she didn't know existed.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A client who is the last living member of her line gets her atlas. The atlas glows on Mara herself. Mara does not yet understand.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Mara's own atlas. The glow has been steady three years. Mara writes the letter. The reply arrives one week later.

series.series_logline: Naomi mediates workplace conflicts. The brass bell rings once and she hears the one sentence each person was holding.
series.series_description: Naomi is thirty-six, a freelance workplace mediator based in Minneapolis. She is hired by HR departments to solve small relational fires before they become wrongful-termination suits. Her grandmother left her a brass conference-table bell. When Naomi rings it once in a closed room, each person in the room has, for one moment, the option to silently speak the one sentence they were withholding — and Naomi is the only one who hears it. She uses the sentences not to expose but to redirect: she asks the right next question and the conversation gets honest enough to land. Each volume is one case — a co-founder split, a manager-report dynamic, a peer-rivalry, a return-from-leave, a layoff conversation. The arc is Naomi's own conflict with her business partner, whom she has not yet brought into the room with the bell. Mystery-cozy register. Apothecary-Diaries-competent. Seinen quiet.
series.reader_promise: Every workplace conflict has one sentence each person is holding. The mediator can hear it.
series.audience: Millennial 32–42 HR / managers / quiet workplace fixers
series.marketing_angle: Apothecary Diaries for the workplace mediator with the bell that hears the sentence everyone meant to say.
series.long_arc_spine: A 36-year-old workplace mediator solves a different employee-relationship conflict each week using a small brass conference-table bell that, when rung, makes each person in the room speak the one sentence they were holding back — once, and only the bell-holder hears it.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Co-founder split at a Series A. The bell reveals one of them is sober. The conversation pivots.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Manager-report. The report is being undermined. The bell reveals the manager is grieving. Naomi asks one different question.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Peer rivalry on a four-person engineering team. The bell reveals one of them is leaving. Naomi reframes the project.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Return-from-leave. The bell reveals the team is afraid of her. Naomi runs a small reintegration ritual.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Naomi's business partner, finally, in the room. The bell rings. Naomi hears the sentence. She decides to say it out loud.

series.series_logline: Cleo inherited the bookstore and the three dogs. Each book is for a friendship the customer needs to mend.
series.series_description: Cleo is twenty-two, a recent literature graduate, and just inherited her aunt's used bookstore in Asheville along with three rescue dogs — Magnolia (the senior), Toast (the chaos), and Pepper (the watcher) — who came with the shop. The shop has its own customers: each one walks in and pulls one book off the shelf without thinking. The book is always one Cleo's aunt would have placed for them, and it is always about one specific friendship the customer needs to repair. Pepper, the small black dog with the white chest, always knows whose friendship it is — and sits near the customer until the customer admits it. Cleo learns to read the dogs the way her aunt did. Each volume is one customer, one book, one repair attempt. The arc is Cleo's own three friends from college, who are slowly drifting, and the book Pepper has placed on the counter for her three months running. Shojo-tender. Heartstopper-warm. The book-store iyashikei subgenre at full strength.
series.reader_promise: The book on the shelf is for the friendship you've been quiet about. The dog knows.
series.audience: Gen Z 20–26 women and queer university readers with one fading friendship and a soft spot for dogs
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: Days at the Morisaki Bookshop but with three rescue dogs and a friendship to mend in every chapter.
series.long_arc_spine: A 22-year-old runs her late aunt's used bookstore with three rescue dogs that came with the shop; each book a customer is drawn to belongs to a friendship the customer needs to repair — and one of the three dogs always knows whose.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: A retired teacher pulls a Le Guin off the shelf. Pepper sits at her feet. The friendship she needs to repair is forty years old.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: A college sophomore pulls a Sally Rooney off the shelf. The friend lives in her dorm and they haven't spoken in six weeks.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: A divorced father pulls a Banana Yoshimoto off the shelf. The friend is his ex-wife. The dogs don't push him. They wait.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A man who looks like he doesn't read pulls a Heartstopper off the shelf. The dog at his feet is Toast. Toast does not usually pick.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Cleo finally picks up the book Pepper has been leaving on the counter. It is the Mary Oliver. She texts the friend that night.

series.series_logline: Four eighth-graders. One garage. Twelve months of figuring out the band is the family.
series.series_description: Hana, Sage, Bee, and Quinn are thirteen, in eighth grade, in suburban Denver, and have decided to start a band — because one of their grandfathers had a guitar in the basement, because a K-pop group they all stream made it look easy, and because the four of them needed a reason to be in one room every Saturday. The series is one school year. One volume per month. They are terrible at first. They get less terrible. One of them is going through a hard time at home. One of them has a parent who is leaving. One of them is realizing she's queer and doesn't know who to tell. One of them is the one keeping it together. The band is the family. They do not say so out loud — they're thirteen — but the reader knows. Heartstopper for middle school. The most explicitly Gen-Alpha series in the cluster. Persona allocation peak.
series.reader_promise: Your eighth-grade band is the family you're allowed to invent before you have words for it.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 11–14 middle-school readers with friend groups that already feel like more
series.marketing_angle: Heartstopper for the suburban Gen Alpha eighth-grade garage band that's actually a family.
series.long_arc_spine: Four thirteen-year-old girls in suburban Denver start a band in one of their garages because the K-pop group they all love brought them together; the year is one volume per month, the friendship is the band, and the band is the family they're allowed to invent.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: August. The first practice. They cannot play. Hana's grandfather teaches them the C chord.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: September. Quinn's father moves out. The Saturday rehearsal happens anyway. Quinn's mother brings cookies.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: October. Bee is being bullied. Sage punches the bully on Tuesday. The garage discusses better strategies on Saturday.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: November. Sage comes out to the band first. The band says 'okay' and tunes.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: December. The winter talent show. They are still terrible. They play anyway. The school cheers. The band is the family.

series.series_logline: Niko's upstairs is haunted by every aunt she's ever had — and every aunt she chose. Each one has one specific answer.
series.series_description: Niko is twenty-one, queer, mixed Korean-American, a third-year at a small college in western Massachusetts. The upstairs of her cheap duplex started filling up in September with women she has known: her great-aunt Yoo-mi who died when she was nine, her college mentor's mother whom she met once, her best friend's mom who is alive and lives in California, the bartender at the queer bar in town who she has never spoken to but who is, somehow, in the line-up. Each volume Niko climbs the stairs and asks one specific question about being a grown queer woman — about money, about coming out at work, about a girlfriend's mother, about her own mother — and one of the aunts answers. The roster grows. The arc is Niko's own mother, who is alive and does not speak to her, and whether the upstairs lets her in. Shojo-tender. Found-family at full strength. The aunts roster is the chosen-family canonical engine.
series.reader_promise: Every aunt you've ever had has one specific answer waiting upstairs.
series.audience: Gen Z 19–24 queer university women with one specific 'how do I do this part of being grown' question
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: Mushishi meets Heartstopper for the queer college student whose duplex upstairs is full of aunts.
series.long_arc_spine: A 21-year-old queer college student finds that the upstairs apartment of her duplex contains a slowly assembling roster of her own dead and living aunts — biological and chosen — each of whom answers one specific question about how to be a grown queer woman.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Great-aunt Yoo-mi. Question: 'How do I tell my girlfriend's mother my pronouns?' Answer: a story from 1971.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The mentor's mother. Question: 'How do I afford grad school?' Answer: a different scholarship.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The bartender (still alive). Question: 'How do I be in a queer bar without panicking?' Answer: a drink order and a chair.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The best friend's California mother. Question: 'How do I tell my dad?' Answer: 'You don't have to yet.'
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Niko's own mother is not yet upstairs. Niko asks the aunts why. The aunts say: 'Because she's not ready.' Niko writes her a letter.

series.series_logline: Eight kids on the third floor. One book club. Every book is by a neighbor they haven't met yet.
series.series_description: Mei is twelve, in seventh grade, and lives on the third floor of a six-story rent-stabilized building in Astoria. There are eight kids her age in the building. She founds a book club. The strange thing the first week: the book she picks at random from the lobby's free-bin turns out to be by the eighty-six-year-old man in 5C, who Mei has nodded at for years. They go meet him. He makes them lemon cookies. The next week: the book is by the woman in 2A — a memoir of being a Filipino nurse in the seventies. They go meet her. By month two the book club is the building. Each volume is one neighbor and one book and one quietly-becoming-aunt or uncle. Spy x Family wholesome. Heartstopper for the apartment-building chosen family. Gen Alpha middle-grade peak.
series.reader_promise: Every grown-up in your building is a story you haven't read yet.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 10–13 apartment-building middle-grade readers with a sense that the adults are all stories
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: Spy x Family for the seventh-grade book club that adopts the whole apartment building.
series.long_arc_spine: A 12-year-old founds a book club for the eight kids on her apartment building's third floor; each week one kid brings a book, and the book turns out to be by someone in their building — a neighbor they didn't know was a writer — who they then meet.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The 5C man's slim novel from 1972. He makes lemon cookies. Mei learns he was a fire captain.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The 2A woman's memoir. The kids learn the building's seventies history. They write her a thank-you.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The 4F couple's poetry chapbook. The two of them read aloud. Half the book club has never heard a chapbook.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The basement super has a self-published cookbook. The kids cook one recipe per kid for the building potluck.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Mei's own mother has a book she wrote in 2009. The kids find it in the free-bin. Mei does not know what to say.

series.series_logline: Tova helps adults make the weekly call to their grandmother. Her clients are starting to recognize each other.
series.series_description: Tova is thirty-three, a former social worker, and runs a small subscription text-line — twenty-five dollars a month — for adults who have one elder they're supposed to call weekly and cannot make themselves dial. Most are estranged or anxious or both. She texts them on Sunday at five with a custom-built opener and a closer, and a list of three questions to lean on. Each volume is one client's elder and one week the call gets made — sometimes well, sometimes not. By month three Tova realizes the clients are starting to message each other; she's built, accidentally, a chosen-family of granddaughters and grandsons for whom the weekly call is the same Sunday afternoon. The arc is Tova's own grandmother, whom she has not called in two years, and the Sunday she finally builds her own script. Heartwarming. Shojo-tender. The most explicitly weekly-ritual series in the cluster.
series.reader_promise: Sunday at five, the call gets made. You are not the only one being walked through the script.
series.audience: Millennial 30–38 grandchildren of one specific overdue elder
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: Before the Coffee Gets Cold for the Sunday phone call you've been postponing for two years.
series.long_arc_spine: A 33-year-old runs a small text-line for adults who have one elder they're supposed to call weekly but can't make themselves; she works out the script and the routine and slowly discovers her client list is a found-family of granddaughters and grandsons who are, quietly, becoming each other's cousins.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Priya's grandmother in Pune. The opener works. The call lasts twenty minutes. Priya cries after.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Marc's grandfather in Brooklyn. The opener fails. They try a different one. The grandfather says 'I missed you.'
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: A new client whose grandmother has dementia. Tova writes a different protocol — three minutes, one question.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The clients discover each other in the group SMS Tova accidentally CC'd. The group survives. Tova names it 'Sunday at five.'
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Tova writes her own script. Calls her grandmother. Her grandmother answers on the second ring.

series.series_logline: Six neighborhood mothers swap childcare on Saturdays. By month six, they're each other's first family.
series.series_description: Liv is thirty-eight, a Chicago social worker with a four-year-old and an eight-year-old, and dead-tired. She posts in the neighborhood listserv asking if any other parents want to swap one Saturday afternoon a month. Five women answer. They meet at the corner playground. By the third Saturday the swap is a system: one woman takes all eleven of their kids to the children's museum, the other five rest. Each volume is one woman's Saturday — her shift, her rest, the small revelation about her own mother she works out while sitting in her apartment alone for the first time in three years. The arc is the seventh woman who asks to join, who is not from the neighborhood — and what the co-op decides about whether the trust scales. Slow, communal, Millennial-tender. No magical register. Pure realist family register. Heartstopper for mid-thirties exhausted parents.
series.reader_promise: The Saturday off is not the gift; the five women who hold your Saturday are.
series.audience: Millennial 33–42 mothers and primary caregivers with toddlers/elementary-age kids and one neighborhood listserv
series.marketing_angle: Spy x Family for the six neighborhood mothers who became each other's first family.
series.long_arc_spine: Six women between 34 and 41 form a 'grandma co-op' in their Chicago neighborhood — each Saturday afternoon one of them watches all of their kids while the others rest — and slowly discover the co-op is the family they all needed at thirty-three.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Liv's first solo Saturday. She does laundry. She cries. She names the feeling: she has not had three hours alone in four years.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Priya's shift at the museum with all eleven kids. She learns each kid's name by lunch. She is exhausted but not alone.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Naomi's solo Saturday. Her mother visits. Naomi tells her mother about the co-op. Her mother cries.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Devi's shift. One of the kids has a seizure. The five other mothers arrive at the ER within forty minutes.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: A new woman, Adia, asks to join. The co-op debates. Liv says yes. They learn the co-op can grow.

series.series_logline: Jordan inherited Grandma's house. Each room remembers a Sunday. He starts hosting again.
series.series_description: Jordan is thirty-seven, a divorced Black father of one in Memphis, who just inherited his grandmother Iris's house — the four-bedroom shotgun on Pearce Street where every Sunday dinner for the family happened from 1965 to 2019. Iris is gone. The cousins are scattered. Jordan moves in with his eight-year-old daughter and discovers each room of the house remembers one specific Sunday dinner — what was served, who was angry, whose baby was crawling, which uncle had bought the new car. Each volume is one room and one Sunday memory and one cousin Jordan finally calls. By month four he has hosted his first Sunday in five years. By month seven the house is full again — different people, same shape. The arc is Jordan's older sister, who has not spoken to him since the divorce, and the Sunday she walks back in. Magical realism. Family-of-origin recovery. The most explicitly bio-family series in the cluster.
series.reader_promise: The house your grandmother held is still holding. You just have to host.
series.audience: Millennial 33–42 Black and brown adult children inheriting a family hub house
series.marketing_angle: Pachinko meets Sweet Bean Paste — the shotgun house in Memphis that's been waiting for the Sunday you cook again.
series.long_arc_spine: A 37-year-old single father inherits his grandmother's house in Memphis — the family hub for sixty years — and learns each room remembers one family Sunday dinner; he begins, slowly, to host one Sunday a month for the relatives he hasn't seen since his divorce.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Jordan and his daughter move in. The kitchen remembers his grandmother's gumbo. He cooks it. His daughter eats two bowls.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The dining room remembers the Sunday his uncle James got the lung-cancer news. Jordan calls James. James is alive in Atlanta.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The back porch remembers his cousin Tasha's wedding rehearsal. Jordan calls Tasha. Tasha drives in for a weekend.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The upstairs room remembers his own childhood. Jordan sits with his daughter and tells her about his own father, who is also alive and estranged.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Jordan's older sister Renee, finally, at the door. The kitchen remembers her birthday Sunday from 1997. She walks in.

series.series_logline: Vera runs the unofficial dispatch for her block. The ledger on her wall keeps track in someone else's handwriting.
series.series_description: Vera is forty-one, a school nurse, and lives on the same Philadelphia rowhouse block she grew up on. Over twenty years she has become the unofficial dispatch: which neighbor has the casserole, which one has the working ladder, which one will sit with a kid at urgent care while a single mother gets to her job. She runs it from a wall-mounted dry-erase board in her kitchen, except the strange thing is the board keeps track of the favors in handwriting that is not hers — small ledger entries that update overnight. Each volume is one favor and one neighbor and one piece of the block's history Vera learns from the ledger. The arc is the new family that moves into the rowhouse two doors down — the granddaughter of the woman who used to run the dispatch in 1977 — and how the ledger passes on. Mystery-cozy. Neighborhood register. The competent-protagonist iyashikei subgenre.
series.reader_promise: Your block already knows. The dispatcher is on the wall in handwriting older than you.
series.audience: Millennial 35–45 long-tenure neighborhood adults running an informal mutual-aid network
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: Apothecary Diaries for the Philadelphia rowhouse dispatcher who runs the neighborhood on a kitchen wall.
series.long_arc_spine: A 41-year-old runs a quiet text-dispatch service in her Philadelphia rowhouse block — one woman who knows who can lend a casserole, watch a kid, drive to urgent care — and a small ledger on her kitchen wall keeps track of the favors in handwriting that is not hers.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The single mother on the corner. Her car breaks down. Vera dispatches the ladder-neighbor's husband. The ledger logs the favor and notes it is the third time he's helped her.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The widower on 4th. Vera dispatches a casserole. The ledger notes the woman who made the casserole grew up next door to the widower's first wife.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The teenager with the bad week. Vera dispatches the retired-teacher neighbor. The ledger notes the teacher used to teach the teenager's mother.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A new family moves in. The granddaughter sees the ledger on day one. She knows what it is. She offers to take a turn.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Vera shows her how to read the handwriting. The ledger names every dispatcher since 1977. The block names her successor.

series.series_logline: Yvette inherited a Kingston kitchen. Each recipe brings back the great-aunt who taught it. The family tree is being re-stitched.
series.series_description: Yvette is thirty-six, a food writer in Brooklyn, and just inherited the working kitchen of her great-grandmother in a small house on the outskirts of Kingston, Jamaica. The kitchen has fifty recipes hand-written in a leather binder. Each time Yvette cooks one, the great-aunt or great-uncle who originally taught her great-grandmother the dish appears in the kitchen — translucent, kind, fully present — and stays for the duration of the cooking. Yvette has missed three generations of family. The family tree is broken at three points: a great-aunt's husband disappeared in 1958, a great-uncle was disinherited in 1971, a cousin emigrated in 1989 and was never spoken of. Each volume is one recipe and one rupture and one piece of the tree Yvette quietly re-stitches by listening. Soft fantasy. Diaspora register. Family-of-origin recovery at scale. The most explicitly heritage-recovery series in the cluster.
series.reader_promise: Your great-grandmother's kitchen still holds the people she cooked with. They are waiting on the recipe.
series.audience: Millennial 32–42 diaspora adults inheriting a family kitchen and a broken family tree
series.marketing_angle: Like Water for Chocolate meets Pachinko — the Kingston kitchen that re-stitches a broken family tree.
series.long_arc_spine: A 36-year-old food writer in Brooklyn inherits the working kitchen of her late great-grandmother in Kingston, Jamaica; each dish she cooks summons the great-aunt or great-uncle who taught the great-grandmother the recipe — and slowly the family tree, which was broken at three points, gets re-stitched.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Brown stew chicken. Great-aunt Doris at fifty appears. She teaches the dish. She mentions her husband who left.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Curry goat. Great-uncle Vernon at thirty-five appears. He is the one who was disinherited. Yvette asks why.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Bun and cheese. Great-grandmother's own mother appears. She tells Yvette the cousin who emigrated is still alive in Toronto.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Ackee and saltfish. Three relatives at once. The first family argument in fifty years gets aired in Yvette's kitchen.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Yvette flies to Toronto. The cousin opens the door. The next recipe is one Yvette will learn from a living person.

series.series_logline: Olive matches adult nieces and nephews with a chosen aunt. One letter a month. The almanac becomes a family.
series.series_description: Olive is thirty-eight, a former librarian in Madison, Wisconsin, and runs The Chosen-Aunt Almanac — a thirty-dollar-a-year subscription that pairs an adult niece or nephew (a subscriber without a living aunt, or with one they cannot talk to) with a stranger who agrees to be their aunt for the year. The stranger writes one letter a month. Olive vets both sides carefully — there is a long application — and each match is one volume. Each chosen aunt is older, has lived through something, and is willing to mail twelve letters. The arc is the third year of the almanac, when subscribers begin asking Olive if their chosen aunt can become a chosen aunt for life, and what Olive decides about whether the almanac scales. Soft fantasy in register only — the magic is the mailbox. Heartwarming. The most explicitly chosen-family-as-system series in the cluster.
series.reader_promise: The aunt you didn't have is one application away. The mailbox does the rest.
series.audience: Millennial 30–42 adult nieces and nephews with one missing aunt and a willingness to write back
comp_titles: What You Are Looking For Is in the Library
series.marketing_angle: Spy x Family meets a literal pen-pal almanac — the chosen-aunt subscription that becomes a family.
series.long_arc_spine: A 38-year-old runs a small almanac-by-mail subscription that pairs each subscriber with a chosen aunt — a stranger, vetted, who writes one letter a month for the year — and slowly the almanac becomes an actual network of aunts and adult nieces and nephews.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Subscriber 014, a twenty-six-year-old in Tucson, is matched with chosen-aunt Beatrice, a seventy-one-year-old retired pediatrician. First letter is about the year Beatrice's own mother died.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Subscriber 027, a forty-year-old in Boston, is matched with chosen-aunt Marisol. Marisol's letters teach him how to be a good uncle.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Subscriber 008, a queer thirty-year-old in Houston, is matched with chosen-aunt Linda, a sixty-eight-year-old lesbian. Linda's first letter is the coming-out at fifty story.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A subscriber's chosen aunt dies mid-year. Olive learns how to write the eleven remaining letters in the aunt's voice.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Subscriber 014 asks: 'Can Beatrice be my aunt for life?' Olive talks to Beatrice. Beatrice says yes.

series.series_logline: A sophomore runs the after-school stream from the computer-lab; her overlay app flags the friend in chat who is about to crash.
