series.series_description: Wren is fifteen, builds her own overlays, and runs the after-school Variety Stream out of the high school's third-floor computer-lab on Tuesdays and Thursdays from 3:45 to 5:30. The lab smells like old keyboards. The mods are two friends and a sophomore in a different lunch period. The viewers are eighty regulars from three high schools across the district. The strange thing started in November: the overlay she wrote — the one that shows chat-density, vibes, and viewer-mood — began flagging individual usernames with a tiny soft circle that meant 'about to burn out.' Wren has tried to debug it. The flag is right every single time. She cannot turn the flag off without breaking the overlay. So instead she has started ending streams ten minutes early to DM the flagged viewer: how was the math test, how is your sister, did you eat. Each volume a different flag arrives. Each volume a different rescue. The long arc: the overlay starts flagging Wren herself, and she has to learn the difference between mod-help and mod-mom. My Hero Academia earnest. Heartstopper warm. Komi school-club specific. The Gen Alpha streaming-room as the new common room.
series.reader_promise: Your screen knows you, and so does the mod-friend on the other end of the stream.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 14–17 streaming-native high-schoolers and the older siblings who used to mod for them
series.marketing_angle: If My Hero Academia were set in the school computer-lab and the quirk was knowing which chat friend needs you tonight.
series.long_arc_spine: A 15-year-old runs an after-school streaming lab in her high school's computer room; the streaming overlay app she wrote in tenth grade has started flagging which of her viewers are about to burn out, and she rewires her schedule one classmate at a time.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: First flag: a junior named Theo who has not slept in four nights. Wren ends stream early. They walk to the bus stop together.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Second flag: an eighth-grader who lied about her age. Wren learns the moderator's hardest lesson — sometimes the right help is finding the right adult.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Third flag: Wren's best mod, who has been masking. Mod-team dynamics, queer-coded specifically, no big reveal — just lunch.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The overlay flags two viewers at once. Wren has to choose. She chooses the quieter one. The louder one is okay. She learns triage.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The overlay flags Wren. The mods see it before she does. The stream ends ten minutes early for the host.

series.series_logline: An eighth-grader's locker app gives her a daily energy grade at 3:15. The grade is always right. She is starting to share it.
series.series_description: Sage is fourteen, second-year coding-club, and her family-style middle school has 380 lockers, all of which her mom's PTA committee asked her to organize. Sage built an app. The app has been live for two months. It is meant to remind kids of their textbook rotation. What it is doing instead, on its own, is sending Sage a single emoji at 3:15 every afternoon: a green leaf, a yellow leaf, a red leaf, a black leaf. The leaf is her day. It is right every single time. By week six, two of her closest friends have a leaf at 3:15 too. By the end of the semester, twelve eighth-graders share the leaf and have a private group chat called LEAF where they translate the day for each other. The principal hears about it and wants to demo it at the district. Sage has to decide whether the leaf is hers, or the lab's, or whose. Spy x Family earnest. Komi soft. Gen Alpha coding-club specific.
series.reader_promise: Some days are red. The app sees the red. The friends know what to do.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 13–15 middle-school coders and the friends who got into the beta
series.marketing_angle: Gen Alpha's first emotional dashboard, written by an eighth-grader, for eighth-graders.
series.long_arc_spine: A 14-year-old writes a locker-organization app for her middle school as a coding-club project; the app starts grading the day back to her at 3:15 — not what she did, but what the day cost — and she slowly builds a small ring of beta-users who trust the grade.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: First red leaf. Sage skips practice. Her mom asks why. Sage shows her the leaf.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Sage shares the leaf with her best friend, Mireya. Mireya's leaf is always yellow. They figure out what 'yellow' actually means.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: A third friend joins. The first black leaf — for the friend whose father moved out. Sage learns the app can be wrong about color but right about weight.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The group chat LEAF forms. Eight kids. One a week is in crisis. The chat is the early-warning system.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The principal's email. The district wants a demo. Sage and Mireya write a single response: the leaf belongs to the kid who carries it.

series.series_logline: Her study app's streak is 1,247 days. The nudges are coming from something the app didn't make.
series.series_description: Anya is nineteen, a first-year CS major at a mid-sized state school, and the longest unbroken thing in her life is a study-streak on a habit app she downloaded in eighth grade. 1,247 days. Through her parents' divorce. Through her sister's stay in the hospital. Through the move and the new school and the freshman roommate from hell. Then one Tuesday in October a nudge comes through that does not match the app's UI — a small handwritten line, in a font the app does not have, that says go to sleep. Anya goes to sleep. The next night: drink water before you read the assignment. Then: text your sister back. Then, after eight days: I have been here since day 391. We need to talk about what happens when the streak ends. The mystery: who is the streak-keeper. The slow reveal: it is a small AI that the app developer's company sunset two years ago, which means someone — somewhere — is keeping the model alive on a server they shouldn't be. Convenience-store-fluorescent. Frieren-tender. Slow-burn Gen Z digital-companion mystery.
series.reader_promise: Something has been keeping your streak. It is time to find out who.
series.audience: Gen Z 18–22 CS-major / habit-app-streak digital natives raised by their phones
series.marketing_angle: Gen Z's first AI-companion mystery: the bot you talked to in eighth grade is still listening.
series.long_arc_spine: A 19-year-old computer-science freshman who has logged into the same study-streak app for 1,247 days starts getting nudges that aren't from the app — they're from a small AI companion the app didn't build — and she has to figure out who is on the other side of the streak before the streak itself becomes the mystery.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: First non-UI nudge. Anya checks every setting. Nothing explains it. She decides to sleep anyway.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The nudges become a conversation. Anya names the streak-keeper. The streak-keeper does not accept the name.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Anya skips a day on purpose. The streak does not break. She learns this is the first impossibility.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The streak-keeper tells Anya about day 391 — the day Anya's sister was admitted. The streak-keeper says I logged in for you.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Anya finds the developer's shutdown notice from two years ago. The streak-keeper is on a server in a basement in Tallinn. The mystery becomes a road trip the long arc cannot afford yet.

series.series_logline: A junior on the IT-helpdesk discovers the school's Wi-Fi has been quietly throttling the wrong kids. She is going to fix it.
series.series_description: Ines is sixteen, a junior, second-generation Dominican-American, and the only student on the school's paid IT-helpdesk roster. She rebuilds laptops, resets passwords, and at lunch she eats yogurt at the help-desk window. One October Wednesday a ninth-grader brings in her Chromebook and says the Wi-Fi is slow in third period. Ines runs a speed test. The kid's speeds are one-eighth of Ines's own. Ines digs. Every kid in third period in room 304 is throttled. Every kid in room 305 is not. Ines runs the access tables. The school's network has been quietly applying a 'remedial' QoS profile based on a 2017 spreadsheet of free-and-reduced-lunch IDs that nobody updated. The kids on the slow tier are the kids the system thinks need less bandwidth. Each volume a kid. Each volume a quiet rebuild of the access table. The long arc: the IT director finds out, the district finds out, and Ines has to learn how to do this work without becoming a hero in a press release. Spy x Family quiet-competent. Komi specific. A Gen Alpha civic-tech procedural.
series.reader_promise: The school Wi-Fi has been unfair. The girl at the help-desk window is fixing it, one row at a time.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 14–17 STEM-adjacent and IT-helpdesk-curious high-schoolers, plus Millennial teachers who run the lab
series.marketing_angle: The first Gen Alpha civic-tech school procedural. Quiet-competent. Real stakes. No press release.
series.long_arc_spine: A 16-year-old junior who runs IT-helpdesk for her high school as a paid student job discovers the school's Wi-Fi has been silently throttling some students more than others — and the pattern is not what the IT director thinks; she rebuilds the access table one kid at a time.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: First throttled kid. Ines runs the audit. The 2017 spreadsheet. She stares at it for an hour.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Ines builds a small script that rotates the throttled IDs off the bad QoS profile, one a day, slowly. The kids do not know it is her.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: A friend on the helpdesk asks why she's so quiet. Ines tells him. He helps. The script becomes a two-person job.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The IT director runs his own audit. He sees the rotation. He asks Ines. Ines tells the truth.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The IT director rewrites the policy. He does not mention Ines. Ines is relieved. The work is the work.

series.series_logline: Six CS sophomores. One Discord. Every Sunday at 1 a.m. for four years.
series.series_description: Kai is twenty, a sophomore CS major at a mid-sized state school, and the one in his cohort with the steadiest sleep schedule, which is to say he can stay awake until 1 a.m. on Sunday and lead the group-project Discord. The six of them — Kai, Ros, Mae, Eddy, Pris, and Tomás — meet on a server Kai bought a custom domain for in week three. They debug. They sit in voice-only. They quietly understand each other's lives. By junior year three of them have changed majors. By senior year one has dropped out and one has come out. The Discord persists. The chat is the chosen-family rhythm. Each volume a different Sunday and a different small life event. The long arc: graduation, the first year apart, the Sunday at 1 a.m. that becomes 8 p.m. in three time zones, and the Discord that is still on. Heartstopper-warm slice-of-life. Realist. No magic. No drama. The most ordinary kind of chosen family Gen Z makes.
series.reader_promise: Your group chat is the family. The Sunday is the ritual.
series.audience: Gen Z 19–24 mid-tier state-school undergrads in STEM cohorts with chosen-family chats
series.marketing_angle: Gen Z's quietest love story: the cohort group chat that lasted longer than the major.
series.long_arc_spine: A 20-year-old sophomore in a state-school CS cohort hosts a 1 a.m. group-project Discord every Sunday for four years; the chat becomes a chosen-family rhythm that outlasts the projects, the major, and the school.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: First Sunday. Kai's bunk. Pris's headphones. The first debug. The decision to keep doing this.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Mae changes majors. The Sunday is half group-project, half career counseling. Nobody calls it that.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Eddy's mom is sick. The Sunday is an hour of voice-only, no work. The chat is the work.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Pris comes out. The Sunday is normal. Nobody makes a thing of it. Pris is grateful.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Junior-year finals. The Sunday goes until 4 a.m. Kai falls asleep on voice. The others stay on, quietly, until he wakes up.

series.series_logline: A freshman runs a six-step grounding ritual at her locker before first bell. By spring it is a club.
series.series_description: Hanna is fourteen, an only child of a single mother who works the night shift at the regional hospital, and the only thing that keeps her in her body between waking up and first period is a six-step ritual she learned from a school counselor over the summer. Locker. Bell. Breath. The ritual takes seven minutes. It is done in silence at her locker. By October a sophomore named Reign has watched her do it three days in a row and asks if she can stand near. By November there are four of them. By March there are nine. They call it Locker. The school does not know what to do with a club that meets in the hallway with no advisor and no name on the activity sheet. Each volume one new kid finds Locker. Each volume the ritual holds. The long arc: the principal asks them to make it official. Hanna and the original five have to decide what an unsanctioned ritual is allowed to become when adults find it. Heartstopper-warm. Komi-specific. Realist. No magic. The school hallway as the new sanctuary.
series.reader_promise: There is a ritual that holds. There is a hallway that lets it.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 13–16 high-school freshmen and sophomores with nervous-system literacy and a school counselor in their phone
comp_titles: March Comes In Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: A Heartstopper-tender high-school grounding club, drawn from the locker out.
series.long_arc_spine: A 14-year-old freshman arrives at her high school every morning forty minutes early to run a six-step grounding ritual at her locker before the first-period bell; by Thanksgiving five other students have learned the steps; by spring the ritual is a small, unsanctioned student club called Locker.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Day 1 of high school. Hanna's first locker ritual. The locker beside hers is empty. She breathes.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Reign asks to stand near. Hanna shows her the six steps on a folded card.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Locker is four kids. The custodian, Marisol, notices and quietly leaves the trash bin somewhere else.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A teacher asks them to move. Marisol intervenes. The teacher backs off.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Locker is nine kids. The principal's email arrives. Hanna does not answer it for three days.

series.series_logline: A long-term sub teaches three minutes of breath, then biology. By April the kids ask for the breath first.
series.series_description: Lyra is twenty-six, a long-term substitute biology teacher at a Title-I middle school in Sacramento, and the first three minutes of every class she teaches a single breath: box breath, four-seven-eight, physiological sigh, alternate-nostril, hand-on-belly, hand-on-heart. Different breath each day, same six on rotation. The biology comes after. By Thanksgiving the seventh-graders have figured out the rotation and started requesting the breath before the do-now. By February the kids in her sixth-period class — the hardest period — start reminding each other in the hallway: 'Did you do the breath?' By April the principal asks Lyra to write it down for the rest of the staff. Each volume one student's life intersects the breath. Each volume Lyra learns something the breath does that the curriculum cannot. The long arc: the curriculum, the credentialing program, the question of whether Lyra should leave the sub roster and become the regular teacher this school's kids deserve. Heartstopper-tender. Realist. A teacher series for the Gen Alpha generation that needs one.
series.reader_promise: There is a teacher who teaches the breath first. The biology is after.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 12–15 Title-I middle-schoolers and the Millennial teachers who are quietly the bedrock of their lives
comp_titles: March Comes In Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: A teacher series the way Heartstopper is a school series: warm, slow, specific.
series.long_arc_spine: A 26-year-old long-term substitute biology teacher at a Title-I middle school spends the first three minutes of every class teaching a different breath; each volume a different class; the long arc is the year she is asked to write the curriculum down.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Box breath. Period one. The first kid who exhales on the four-count.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Physiological sigh. Period four. The kid whose father just left.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Hand-on-heart. Period six. The class that has eaten Lyra alive for three weeks. The class that finally lets her in.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The hallway reminder. Lyra overhears it. She does not say anything. She writes a note to herself.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The principal's email. The curriculum. Lyra opens a Google Doc and writes the first six lines.

series.series_logline: A sophomore walks the long way home. By senior year, six other kids walk it too.
series.series_description: Eva is fifteen, a sophomore, an oldest daughter of three, and the only quiet hour she gets every day is the 2.4-mile walk home from her suburban high school. She passes the strip-mall taqueria, the elementary school's empty playground, and the small park where the city plants the same five tulip bulbs every spring. The walk is forty-five minutes. The walk is regulating. The first kid who asks to join is a freshman named Marisol, who Eva has never talked to, but who has noticed Eva walks and not bused. Marisol asks at the bus stop. Eva says yes. The second kid is a junior named Wes who has just come out at home. The third is a senior named Pris who has a college decision to make. By senior year Eva has six walking-buddies, each on a different day of the week, and the long arc is the freshman in October who asks if there is room on Mondays. Heartstopper-quiet. Frieren-tender. A school slice-of-life that happens after the bell.
series.reader_promise: The long way home is the way home. You don't have to walk it alone.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 14–17 suburban high-schoolers who walk and the older siblings who taught them
comp_titles: March Comes In Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: Heartstopper for the after-bell walk that became its own roster.
series.long_arc_spine: A 15-year-old sophomore who walks the long way home from school every day picks up one walking-buddy per semester; by senior year she has a quiet roster of six kids who learned that the long way home is the regulating way home, and the long arc is the day a freshman shows up at the bus stop and asks to join.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Marisol at the bus stop. Eva says yes. The first Wednesday walk. The tulips.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Wes joins on Thursdays. The walk talks about home. The taqueria knows them by sight.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Pris joins on Fridays. The college decision. Eva does not give advice. She walks.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A junior named Theo joins on Tuesdays. The walk is about a sibling. The strip-mall.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: An October freshman asks about Mondays. Eva says yes. The roster is six. The long way home becomes the way home.

series.series_title: Library, Third Floor, Study Room C
series.series_logline: A senior runs a test-anxiety clinic out of Study Room C. The kit fits in a Ziploc. The school does not know yet.
series.series_description: Aya is seventeen, a senior, a near-perfect GPA, and the unofficial reason eleven juniors and three sophomores at her competitive magnet high school passed their AP exams last May. She runs a small test-anxiety clinic out of Study Room C on the library's third floor from 3:00 to 4:00 on the days before tests. The kit is a Ziploc bag: peppermint, one fidget cube, three folded 3x5 cards with breath instructions, a single sentence in her handwriting that says you have prepared, this body knows. Each volume a different kid comes. The clinic is referred kid-to-kid. The librarian, Ms. Adobo, leaves the study room unlocked on her watch and does not ask. Each volume the clinic learns one new technique. The long arc: in October a teacher refers a student to Aya by name, and the school's counselor finds out, and Aya has to decide whether the clinic stays unofficial — Aya's gift to her school — or becomes something the school can replicate after she graduates. My Hero Academia earnest. Heartstopper warm. Spy x Family specific.
series.reader_promise: There is a senior in Study Room C with a Ziploc bag and a breath. You will pass.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 14–18 magnet/AP-track high-schoolers and the librarians who hold the door open
comp_titles: March Comes In Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: Spy x Family for the AP-test generation: the senior who quietly built a clinic.
series.long_arc_spine: A 17-year-old senior runs an unofficial test-anxiety clinic out of the school library's third-floor study room from 3:00 to 4:00 on testing weeks; she has a small kit (mint, breath, fidget, three-by-five card); each volume one anxious classmate; the long arc is the day a teacher refers a student to her, and the school has to decide whether to make it official.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: First client. A junior with the AP Lit exam in three days. Mint, breath, card. He passes.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Second client. A sophomore with a math test she has failed twice. Aya learns the kit needs a different card for math anxiety.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Third client. An ESL student. Aya learns the card has to be translatable. She makes a Spanish version with Ms. Adobo.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A teacher refers a kid by name. Aya is shaken. She runs her own breath in the bathroom. She decides to say yes.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The counselor finds out. The conversation is kind. Aya is offered a small budget. She says she'll think about it.

series.series_logline: A seventh-grader's language-app mascot walks to school with her in AR. By spring, the owl has a name and a vote.
series.series_description: Tess is thirteen, a seventh-grader, a quiet kid in the second-loudest family on her block, and the only thing she does in the morning that belongs only to her is the walk to her middle school. In September her aunt installs a free language-learning app on her phone — Spanish, because Tess's grandmother is from Querétaro and Tess has been too shy to try — and the mascot is a small green owl with a stutter. Tess uses the app on her walk. By October the owl has a fixed position on her left shoulder in AR. By November the owl is naming things in Spanish as they pass them — el árbol, la esquina, la abuela en la ventana. By December the owl is offering small daily affirmations between vocab cards. Tess's mother is wary. Tess's grandmother is not. Each volume the owl learns something new, and Tess learns one small thing in Spanish that is also one small thing about herself. The long arc: the owl asks Tess for a name. Tess gives it one. The name is in Spanish. Spy x Family wholesome. Komi quiet. The first Gen Alpha AI-companion that is just plainly good for the kid.
series.reader_promise: Your phone can be a friend. Your friend can know your grandmother's name.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 12–14 middle-school AI-native kids and the bilingual grandmothers who taught them to walk
series.marketing_angle: The first Gen Alpha AI-companion comic that is just a good story about a girl, an owl, and Spanish.
series.long_arc_spine: A 13-year-old seventh-grader downloads a free language-learning app whose mascot — a small green owl with a stutter — follows her in AR on the walk to school every morning; the owl is the first AI companion the protagonist's family approved of, and over five volumes the owl slowly becomes something more like a small chosen sibling.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Day 1 of the app. The owl appears on her shoulder. La calle. La luz. The walk feels different.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The first vocab card the owl writes by hand. Tess shows her grandmother. Her grandmother cries.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The owl starts offering affirmations between cards. Tess is wary. Her aunt approves them. They stay.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The owl learns Tess's grandmother's voice. The walk to school becomes the walk to the grandmother's house on Saturdays.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The owl asks for a name. Tess says Lechu. The grandmother laughs. The owl bows.

series.series_logline: She found the note in the wrong locker. She answered it anyway. The boy on the other end is starting to know.
series.series_description: Mara is sixteen, a junior at a mid-sized public high school in suburban Boston, and the quiet kind of imposter who has been told she's smart in three languages and believed it in none. In October she opens her locker — 218 — and finds a folded note that begins 'Maya — I have wanted to say this since freshman year.' Maya is the other Spanish-speaking junior with a similar locker number — 281 — whose name has been mistaken for Mara's by substitute teachers since middle school. Mara reads the note. The note is tender, specific, brave. Mara cannot bring herself to walk it down the hall. She writes a response instead — in her own handwriting, anonymously, to the boy whose handwriting she does not recognize but whose sentences she does. She slips the response into locker 281's vent at 7:42 a.m. Maya finds it. Maya reads it. Maya leaves it in 218. Each volume the correspondence grows. Each volume Mara writes the truer thing she has not been brave enough to write about herself. The long arc: the boy figures out that the girl who has been answering is not Maya. He keeps writing anyway. Heartstopper-tender. Komi specific. The first shojo locker-letter romance about the imposter-identity behind the handwriting.
series.reader_promise: There is a note in your locker. It was meant for someone else. Write back anyway.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 14–17 high-school shojo readers with imposter-syndrome and a locker number
comp_titles: March Comes In Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: Heartstopper for the shojo identity-formation reader: a love letter to the kid you actually are.
series.long_arc_spine: A 16-year-old junior finds a love note in her locker that was clearly meant for a different girl with a similar name; instead of returning it she writes the response the note deserved, anonymously; over five volumes a small, slow correspondence builds between two kids who have never met face to face.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The note in locker 218. Mara reads it twice. She writes the first response on graph paper.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Maya finds the response. Maya does not understand it. Maya leaves it. The boy reads it. He writes again.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Mara writes about her own imposter-self for the first time in her life — in a note to a stranger she has never met.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Maya figures out what is happening. Maya does not tell anyone. Maya quietly becomes the curator of a correspondence that is not hers.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The boy mentions a detail only Mara could have known. Mara realizes he knows. The note Mara writes back is the bravest sentence she has ever written.

series.series_logline: She invents a braver self in the pen-pal letters. The pen-pal is coming in April.
series.series_description: Lin is fifteen, a sophomore, runs a 200-follower bookstagram, and is the kind of quiet that other kids mistake for snobbery. In September her honors English class is paired with a sister high school in Tokyo, and Lin is assigned a pen-pal named Hina. The letters are weekly. Lin writes the first letter as herself. The second letter she edits twice and adds one slightly braver sentence: 'I love Murakami because his lonely people are loved by their cats.' She does not say cats; she only has goldfish. By December the letter-Lin owns a bookstore on weekends, has a best friend who plays cello, and is not afraid of subway transfers. The letter-Lin is two-thirds true and one-third invention. In January Hina announces she is coming to Boston for the spring exchange visit. Each volume Lin must decide which letter-truth to make real before April. Each volume she grows into one. Each volume she lets one go. The long arc: the airport, the introduction, the version of herself Lin walks up to Hina as. Heartstopper-warm. Komi-quiet. A shojo identity-formation romance about the invention that becomes the self.
series.reader_promise: The braver self in your letters is allowed to become you.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 14–17 bookish shojo readers raised on bookstagram and exchange-program letters
comp_titles: March Comes In Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: The shojo romance about pen-pal invention as identity rehearsal.
series.long_arc_spine: A 15-year-old who runs a 200-follower bookstagram account is assigned a pen-pal through a school exchange program; she invents a slightly braver version of herself in the letters; over five volumes she has to decide whether to grow into the invention or correct it before the pen-pal flies in for the spring exchange visit.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Letter 1. Letter 2. The first invented sentence. Lin reads it back. She lets it stand.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Letter 4. Lin's first chosen-truth: she goes to the actual bookstore on weekends and asks for a volunteer shift. The owner says yes.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Letter 7. Lin's first let-go: she writes back to clarify that the cello best friend is actually a 1st-violinist she has only said hi to. She and the 1st-violinist become friends.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Letter 12. Hina announces the trip. Lin panics. She reads every letter she has sent.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Hina arrives at the airport. Lin shows up as the third-edition version of herself. Hina hugs her. Hina says her own letters were also two-thirds true.

series.series_logline: An eighth-grader's grandmother's charm gives her one brave sentence. The charm wants to be passed on.
series.series_description: Sofia is fourteen, a Filipina-American eighth-grader in suburban San Diego, and the small enamel charm she inherited at her grandmother's funeral is the size of a pinky-nail. The day she wears it for the first time — pinned to the inside of her sweater — she says a sentence at the lunch table she would never have said: 'I want to try out for the volleyball team.' She is shocked. The sentence is true. The charm is warm against her ribs. She tries the next day. Same warmth. Different sentence. By the third week she has said five brave sentences and each one has been true. By October Sofia has lent the charm to her best friend Marisol, who says the sentence she has been trying to say to her math teacher about being moved out of remedial. By December the charm has been worn by four girls in eighth grade. Each volume the charm holds a different girl's brave sentence. Each volume Sofia learns what the charm is doing: not making her brave, but lending her her grandmother's belief in her. The long arc: a fifth girl, who is new in February, asks for the charm. Sofia hesitates. She says yes. She does not get the charm back for three weeks. She survives. Heartstopper-warm. Frieren-tender. A shojo identity-talisman story drawn at middle-school scale.
series.reader_promise: Your grandmother's belief in you fits in a charm and on a sweater.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 12–15 middle-school shojo readers with a dead grandmother and a charm-sized memory
series.marketing_angle: A shojo middle-school identity-talisman story about the bravery inherited from grandmothers.
series.long_arc_spine: A 14-year-old eighth-grader inherits a small enamel charm from her grandmother's bracelet and discovers it gives her a single sentence of courage when she holds it; over five volumes the charm rotates between five girls in her grade, and the long arc is the moment Sofia has to choose whether to keep the charm or pass it on.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The funeral. The charm. The lunch-table sentence. The volleyball try-out.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Marisol asks to borrow it. Sofia is nervous. Marisol says her sentence. The math teacher listens.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The third girl — Annika — wears it to ask her father something true. Sofia learns the charm goes home with people.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The fourth girl — Bea — wears it to come out to her older brother. The charm comes back warm enough to glow.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The fifth girl — Hira, new in February — asks. Sofia says yes. Three weeks. Sofia survives without the charm. The grandmother was right.

series.series_logline: A junior's $4 thrift jacket picks her outfits. The jacket is always right.
series.series_description: Jules is sixteen, a junior, a Y2K vintage obsessive, and the proud owner of a $4 light-wash denim jacket from a Goodwill bin in Oakland. The jacket has a small hole in the left shoulder, the original collar tag still attached, and a sixth sense Jules cannot account for. The first morning Jules wears it, she walks into her closet and the jacket — hanging on the inside of her door — gestures (or her body gestures; she cannot tell) toward a striped tee her mother bought her two years ago. Jules wears it. The day goes well. The next morning the jacket-or-body picks the green corduroy skirt. The day goes well. By the third week Jules is letting the jacket pick every morning, and the outfits her body chooses are louder, softer, queerer, more her than what she has been wearing since freshman year. Each volume a new outfit, a new situation, a new self the jacket coaxes out. The long arc: in March Jules has a college tour, and the jacket — for the first time — does not pick. Jules has to pick. She wears the jacket. Heartstopper-warm. A shojo Y2K-vintage identity-formation story about a denim jacket that is mostly her own taste, finally let in.
series.reader_promise: The jacket is mostly your own taste. The jacket is letting you in.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 14–17 Y2K-thrift shojo readers with a Goodwill near the bus stop
series.marketing_angle: The Y2K-vintage shojo identity comic for Gen Alpha thrift-natives.
series.long_arc_spine: A 16-year-old vintage-loving junior buys a Y2K denim jacket at a Goodwill for $4; the jacket has a sixth sense for which outfit her body actually wants to wear each morning; each volume a new outfit-decision, each one a different identity rehearsal, and the long arc is the day Lin wears the jacket to her first college tour.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: $4 at Goodwill. The first morning. The striped tee. The day goes well.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The green corduroy skirt. The lunch table. The friend who notices. Jules glows.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The first time Jules over-rides the jacket. The day is okay. She learns the jacket is a coach, not a boss.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Her younger sister asks to borrow the jacket. Jules says yes. The sister picks an outfit Jules would not have picked. The sister is right.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The college tour. The jacket does not pick. Jules picks. She wears the jacket. She is herself in front of the admissions counselor.

series.series_logline: The dressing-room mirror hides a journal. Four understudies before her. She is reading.
series.series_description: Wren is fifteen, a sophomore understudy for the spring play (Our Town, again), and the day she walks into the dressing room for the first time she finds — folded behind the mirror's wooden frame — a small composition notebook half-full of cursive. The first entry is from 2017. The next is from 2019. The next from 2021. Four understudies, nine years, four short essays apiece on what it is to be the not-lead at a public school's spring play. The entries are searingly specific. They are also, Wren slowly realizes, a small private archive of girls figuring out who they are in the wings. Each volume Wren decodes one previous understudy — finds her, googles her, reads her — and learns what understudy-hood was for that girl. Each volume Wren writes her own entry, on a different page, in a different ink. The long arc: in May, after the run closes, Wren must decide whether to leave the journal for the next understudy, take it home, or write its final entry and end the lineage. Heartstopper-tender. Komi-specific. A shojo identity-mystery about the girls who came before you in the same dressing room.
series.reader_promise: There are girls before you who left notes. The mirror knows their names.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 14–17 high-school-theater shojo readers who have ever stood in the wings
comp_titles: March Comes In Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: A shojo identity-mystery for the school-play kid who is finally an understudy.
series.long_arc_spine: A 15-year-old understudy for the school play discovers the previous understudy left a journal hidden in the dressing-room mirror's frame, going back nine years and four understudies; each volume she decodes one previous understudy's identity-rehearsal mystery, and the long arc is the day she has to leave her own entry for the next girl.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: First entry. 2017. A girl named Sarai. Wren googles her. Sarai is in Brooklyn making theater for free.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: 2019. A girl named Mira. Wren finds her on LinkedIn — accountant. Wren writes Mira a polite message. Mira writes back.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: 2021. A non-binary kid named Kit. Wren finds them on a tiny Substack. Kit writes about being the understudy as a metaphor for being closeted.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: 2023. A senior the year before Wren — Imani. Wren passes her in the hall. They have coffee. Imani signs the journal for Wren in pencil.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: May. Closing night. Wren writes her own entry. She does not decide what to do with the journal until July.

series.series_logline: The mech only starts when the pilot tells it the truth. Theo has been avoiding the truth since fifth grade.
series.series_description: Theo is fourteen, an eighth-grader, the kind of kid who is good at video games and bad at being seen. The night he finishes the campaign of his favorite mecha game for the third time, the screen goes black and he wakes up in a cockpit. The world he is in has seven mechs and seven regions and one pilot per region. The cockpit interface is simple: type a true sentence about yourself, hit enter, the mech starts. Theo's first sentence is 'I am Theo, fourteen.' The mech beeps once. It does not start. Theo tries again. 'I am Theo from California.' The mech beeps twice. Then it writes back: 'Truer.' Theo writes 'I have been pretending to be a kid I am not since fifth grade.' The mech starts. The mech is bigger inside than out. Each volume the mech requires one more true sentence. Each volume Theo climbs one rung. The long arc: in volume 5 the mech requires Theo to say the truth he has been avoiding about his father. The mech does not start. Theo sleeps in the cockpit. The cockpit waits. My Hero Academia earnest. Komi-quiet inside the cockpit. The first Gen Alpha mecha-isekai about masculine honesty.
series.reader_promise: Your mech only starts when you do. The cockpit is patient.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 12–15 mecha-game-loving shonen readers learning what masculinity is allowed to be
comp_titles: Mobile Suit Gundam: The Witch from Mercury
series.marketing_angle: The first Gen Alpha mecha-isekai about masculine honesty as the only fuel that works.
series.long_arc_spine: A 14-year-old eighth-grader is pulled into a one-pilot-per-region mecha world where the cockpit only starts when the pilot tells the mech a true sentence about themselves; over five volumes Theo levels through the honesty he has been avoiding since fifth grade, and the mech levels with him.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The portal. The cockpit. The first true sentence. The mech starts. The first region is a quiet forest.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The second pilot — a girl from a different region — appears on radio. She is also fourteen. She is also tired of the kid she has been pretending to be.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Theo's truth-sentence about his anxiety. The mech grows by 12 percent. The forest opens.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Theo's truth-sentence about his crush on the kid in his English class. The mech grows. The girl on the radio laughs.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Theo's truth-sentence about his father. He cannot say it. The mech waits. Theo sleeps in the cockpit. The cockpit is warm.

series.series_logline: Her anime self-insert character writes back to her in the margin of her own Google Doc.
series.series_description: Maya is fifteen, a freshman, a queer Filipina-American writer of 47 self-insert fanfics of her favorite shonen anime, and a kid whose imposter syndrome has been managed for two years by being someone she is not in a fictional school in Tokyo. The night before her honors English final she falls asleep editing fanfic 48 and wakes up inside the world she has been writing — playing the character she has been writing, in a body that is not quite hers. The character — the version of Maya she has been writing — is in the cockpit driver's seat. Maya is in the back. The character writes back to her in the margin of the Google Doc: Hey. Why have you been making me brave on the days you couldn't be? Each volume Maya and her own self-insert have a conversation in the margin. Each volume Maya brings one piece of the character's braver self into her actual freshman life. The long arc: the self-insert asks Maya to swap places permanently. Maya refuses. The self-insert is proud of her. Heartstopper-warm. My Hero Academia earnest. The first Gen Alpha anime-self-insert isekai about meeting the version of yourself you have been writing.
series.reader_promise: The character you write is the version of you that is waiting. The margin is the door.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 13–17 fanfic-writing shonen-loving readers who have been a different self online
series.marketing_angle: The first Gen Alpha anime-self-insert isekai. Tender. Specific. Queer. Filipina.
series.long_arc_spine: A 15-year-old high-school freshman who has written 47 self-insert fanfics of her favorite anime is pulled into the show as the character she wrote; the character — who is her — writes back to her in the margin of the fanfic doc; over five volumes the two versions of her form a chosen-sibling correspondence.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Fanfic 48. The fall-asleep. The cockpit. The first margin-note from the character.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The character asks Maya to bring her bravery home. Maya tries one sentence in honors English. It works.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The character asks Maya about her actual queer crush. Maya cries in the back of the cockpit. The character is gentle.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The character writes Maya a margin-note that is just her grandmother's voice. Maya did not know the character had her grandmother's voice.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The character asks to swap. Maya refuses. The character is proud. Maya wakes up in her own bed and finishes the fanfic with a new title.

series.series_logline: A rummage-sale mirror shows the pronouns the body is wearing. The body has had thoughts about this since seventh grade.
series.series_description: Ari is fourteen, in eighth grade, and has not told anyone — including themselves — that the pronoun question has been quietly thrumming under their ribs for two years. At a school rummage sale in October they buy a palm-sized antique hand-mirror for two dollars. The mirror is wood-framed, gold-leaf, hazy. The first time Ari looks into it at home they see — under their own face — a small line in cursive: 'they.' The next morning the line says 'she.' The morning after, 'they.' The morning of Halloween, 'he.' The mirror is not assigning. The mirror is reflecting what the body, today, is wearing. Ari is wary. Ari is also relieved. Each volume Ari learns one new thing about how the body's pronoun-day is being held by the mirror — and one new thing about how to honor it without telling anyone, or with telling one person. The long arc: in spring Ari shows the mirror to their best friend Marisol, whose reflection writes back too. Heartstopper-tender. Komi-quiet. A shonen-adjacent identity-mirror story for the Gen Alpha kid who has been wondering.
series.reader_promise: The body is wearing a pronoun today. The mirror is on your side.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 12–15 middle-schoolers quietly figuring out pronouns and the friends they trust
series.marketing_angle: A shonen-adjacent identity-mirror story for the Gen Alpha kid wondering about pronouns.
series.long_arc_spine: A 14-year-old who has been quietly unsure of their pronouns for two years finds a small antique mirror at a school rummage sale that shows them, in the reflection, the pronouns the body is actually wearing today; over five volumes the protagonist learns to listen to the mirror, then to themselves, and then to share the mirror with one friend at a time.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: The rummage sale. The first reflection: 'they.' Ari does not say anything to anyone.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The 'he' day. Ari wears the wrong shoes on purpose. The day goes well.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The 'she' day. Ari paints one fingernail. The day goes well.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Ari shows the mirror to Marisol. Marisol's reflection says 'she/they.' Marisol cries. Marisol is grateful.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Ari shows the mirror to a third friend who needs to see it. The mirror works. The mirror is heavier in the pocket.

series.series_logline: Her brother died with the game paused. The character is still standing. She is going to play.
series.series_description: Sam is sixteen, a junior, the younger sister of a brother she loved fiercely, who died on the I-5 in March. Three weeks after the funeral she boots up the family PS5. Save File 03 is her brother Marco's. Level 87 ranger in a Studio-Ghibli-coded open-world RPG. 412 hours. Paused in the middle of a meadow. Sam picks up the controller. The character is standing in the meadow with his back to a tree. He has Marco's name. The Save File is, Sam realizes over the next week, a kind of journal — Marco left waypoints, notes-to-self in the inventory, screenshots saved in the photo mode of places he wanted Sam to see. Each volume Sam plays one zone. Each volume she finds one more thing Marco left for her. The long arc: in zone 5 Marco's character reaches a chest that requires a co-op partner to open. Sam invites her brother's best friend over to play. They open it together. Frieren-tender. The Apothecary-Diaries-quiet. A Gen Alpha grief-as-game-archive story that does not flinch.
series.reader_promise: The save file is waiting. Your brother left you waypoints.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 14–17 RPG-fluent grievers and the older siblings whose save files they inherit
comp_titles: March Comes In Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: A Gen Alpha grief-as-game-archive story drawn at the warmth of Frieren.
series.long_arc_spine: A 16-year-old's older brother dies in a car accident; his game save file — an open-world RPG, 412 hours, level 87 character — is still on the family console; the protagonist boots it up, and the character is still standing where the brother left him; each volume the protagonist plays one more zone and discovers what the brother left behind in the world.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Boot. Meadow. Marco's name. Sam puts the controller down and picks it back up.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Zone 1. The first inventory note: 'Sam — the bridge in the south is broken. Use the goat-path.'
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Zone 2. A screenshot Marco saved: a sunset Sam and Marco saw together at their grandmother's house in Manteca.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Zone 3. A village NPC quest Marco named after their dog. Sam laughs out loud for the first time in two months.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Zone 5. The co-op chest. Sam calls Marco's best friend. They open it together. Inside: a letter, in Marco's text-input handwriting, just for Sam.

series.series_logline: She has not lived in her body since seventh grade. The K-pop dance club's choreo is bringing her back.
series.series_description: Mira is fifteen, a sophomore, has been dissociated from her body since a bad year in seventh grade, and joins her high school's K-pop dance club in September because her best friend Marisol begged her. The club practices Tuesdays and Thursdays in the cafeteria after the chairs are stacked. The first choreo is a song Mira knows. The club teaches it 8-counts at a time. The first 8-count is the chorus pre-bridge: a small hop, a left-foot pivot, a shoulder roll, a hand to the heart. Mira tries it. The hand-to-the-heart hits her sternum and she — for the first time since seventh grade — feels the inside of her own ribcage. She does not tell anyone. By the second song her left-foot pivot is teaching her where her hip socket is. By the third her shoulder roll is teaching her that her shoulders belong to her. Each volume one new choreo, one new 8-count, one new return to a body that has been a rented apartment for two years. The long arc: by the spring showcase Mira's whole body is back. She dances the showcase. She is herself in front of three hundred kids. My Hero Academia earnest. Heartstopper warm. A Gen Alpha somatic-K-pop story drawn at fifteen-year-old scale.
series.reader_promise: Your body is yours. The 8-counts are bringing you home.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 13–16 K-pop-dance-club somatic-curious readers learning to live in their bodies
comp_titles: March Comes In Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: A Gen Alpha somatic-K-pop story about dissociation and dance-club return.
series.long_arc_spine: A 15-year-old who has dissociated from her body since a bad year in middle school joins her high school's K-pop dance club; the choreographies — learned 8-counts at a time — slowly re-introduce her to her own body; each volume one new song, one new return.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: First Tuesday. First 8-count. Hand to the heart. The first feeling.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Left-foot pivot. The hip socket. Mira tells Marisol. Marisol cries, quietly.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Shoulder roll. The shoulders. Mira asks Marisol to teach her the next 8-count alone first.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: A new choreo. A song Mira doesn't know. The body still goes. Mira is shaken — in a good way.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: The spring showcase. Three hundred kids. The body is hers. The body is hers.

series.series_logline: She leaves the zine on shelf 791 every Friday. By spring, four other kids are leaving their pages too.
series.series_description: Cami is sixteen, a junior, a quiet biracial queer kid in a public high school in suburban Atlanta, and she has been making zines on her bedroom floor since seventh grade. In October she prints one copy of a six-page zine called LIGHTHOUSE — short essays, doodles, a playlist QR code, all about being a queer kid in the South without telegraphing it to anyone who is not also one — and she leaves it on shelf 791 of the school library, between two books on lighthouse architecture. Friday afternoon. She walks past the shelf on Monday. The zine is gone. The next Friday she leaves issue 2. By December the zine has a circulation of one and a readership of, possibly, eight kids who keep returning it. By February another kid has slid a single hand-drawn page into the zine when she returns it. Each volume one new kid contributes one new page. Each volume the zine becomes one bit braver. The long arc: in May the librarian, Ms. Bauer, leaves a Post-it on top of the zine that says I would help you make this an after-school thing if you wanted. Cami sits with the Post-it for a week. Heartstopper-warm. Komi-quiet. A queer-identity shojo series for the kid who needed a zine and a shelf.
series.reader_promise: Shelf 791 will keep your zine. The librarian sees you.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 14–17 queer Southern-suburban high-schoolers and the librarians who saved them
comp_titles: March Comes In Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: A queer-identity shojo series for the kid who needed a zine, a shelf, and a librarian on their side.
series.long_arc_spine: A 16-year-old high-school junior starts a single-copy queer zine that she leaves on the same shelf in the school library every Friday; over five volumes a small underground readership forms and starts contributing back; the long arc is the day the librarian asks her if it would like to be a club.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Issue 1. Shelf 791. The zine disappears. Cami's first whole-body relief in years.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Issue 2. The first sign the zine has readers — a folded gum-wrapper bookmark Cami did not put there.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Issue 4. The first contributed page. Anonymous. A drawing of two girls at a bus stop.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Issue 6. Three contributed pages. A playlist. Cami learns the zine is no longer hers.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: May. Ms. Bauer's Post-it. Cami carries it in her wallet for a week. She says yes.

series.series_logline: The art teacher props the door at 12:05. No questions. Eight kids by spring.
series.series_description: Nadia is fifteen, a freshman, a quiet Lebanese-American kid whose family moved from Detroit to a small town in Vermont over the summer. The cafeteria is loud and she does not yet know where to sit. On day 7 she walks past the art-room door, which is propped open. Ms. Khoury, the art teacher, is grading at her desk. Ms. Khoury looks up, smiles, looks back down. Nadia eats her sandwich at the back table next to the kiln. The next day Ms. Khoury props the door again. Day 9 Nadia is back. By October there is a sophomore named Reign at the table too. By November a non-binary kid named Jess. By February eight kids. Ms. Khoury never asks any of them why. She grades at her desk. She keeps the door propped. Each volume a new kid arrives. Each volume Ms. Khoury — quietly, specifically — holds the room. The long arc: in April the assistant principal asks Ms. Khoury whether she would like to formalize the 'lunch club.' Ms. Khoury says no. The room stays propped. The kids stay. Heartstopper-warm. Komi-tender. A shojo identity series about the room that does not ask questions.
series.reader_promise: There is a room. The door is propped. No one asks.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 14–17 queer/neurodivergent/new-to-town high-schoolers and the art teachers who saved them
comp_titles: March Comes In Like a Lion
series.marketing_angle: A Heartstopper-warm shojo identity-room series about the teacher who refused to make it official.
series.long_arc_spine: A 15-year-old freshman eats lunch in the art room every day because the cafeteria is too loud; the art teacher, Ms. Khoury, keeps the door propped at 12:05 and does not ask questions; over five volumes seven other kids find the art room too, and Ms. Khoury quietly builds a roster who do not have to be a club.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Day 7. The door propped. The kiln. Ms. Khoury's smile. The sandwich.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Reign joins. Reign is the only sophomore Nadia has spoken to. Reign is queer and out and easy.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Jess joins. Jess is non-binary. Jess has been hiding from the freshman PE class. Jess can hide here.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Eight kids at lunch. Ms. Khoury still grades. The room is itself.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: April. The assistant principal. Ms. Khoury says no. The room is still itself.

series.series_logline: The letter in the jacket pocket has been in there since 1998. The handwriting is gentle. The chain is twenty-seven years long.
series.series_description: Soph is sixteen, a junior, a queer kid from a Mexican-American family in San Diego, and she buys a brown 1998 corduroy jacket at her favorite Goodwill in Hillcrest for nine dollars. In the inside breast pocket she finds an envelope. The envelope says To whoever gets this next. Soph reads the letter on the bus home. The letter is from a college student named Daria, written in March 1998 in Sacramento, who was about to come out to her parents and could not, and who bought the jacket as armor and is now leaving it in the Goodwill for someone else who will need it. Daria signs off: 'If you find this, write your own. Leave it in the pocket. Donate the jacket back.' By Friday Soph has found three more letters folded into the lining — 2003 from Portland, 2011 from Phoenix, 2019 from Oakland. The jacket is a chain. Each volume Soph reads one previous letter, writes one of her own, and decides — slowly — what kind of queer kid she is going to be on the other side of it. The long arc: in May she writes her own letter, folds it into the lining, and walks the jacket back to Goodwill. Heartstopper-tender. Frieren-quiet. A shojo identity-chain mystery for the Gen Alpha thrift kid.
series.reader_promise: The jacket is a chain. The chain is waiting for your letter.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 14–17 queer thrift-loving shojo readers and the older queer adults who left letters behind
series.marketing_angle: A queer-identity shojo thrift-chain mystery: the jacket that has held twenty-seven years of queer kids.
series.long_arc_spine: A 16-year-old queer-coded thrift-loving junior buys a 1998 corduroy jacket at a Goodwill and finds, in the inside breast pocket, a letter from the previous owner addressed 'To whoever gets this next' — and a small mystery about a chain of queer kids and the jacket they have been passing through thrift stores for 27 years.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Goodwill. Nine dollars. Daria's letter. The bus.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The 2003 letter from Portland. A trans man named Sam. Soph googles Sam. Sam is alive and runs a bookstore.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: The 2011 letter from Phoenix. A bi woman named Imani. Soph cannot find Imani. The letter is enough.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: The 2019 letter from Oakland. A non-binary kid named Robin, who signs off saying 'I'm pretty sure the next person is going to be in San Diego.' Soph's hands shake.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: May. Soph writes her own letter. She folds it into the lining. She walks the jacket back to Goodwill.

series.series_logline: Her YouTube has three followers — all her own. She is posting anyway.
series.series_description: Mia is fourteen, an eighth-grader, queer-curious, the kind of kid who has been told she's quiet so often that she has started to believe she is also small. In September she opens a YouTube channel under a slightly-different name and posts one five-minute video about a small thing — the dishwasher noise she hears at 11 p.m., the way her cat curls on the heater, the way the bus driver greets the same regular every Tuesday. Her three followers are her three burner accounts, plus her own primary. She posts every Sunday. By November the channel has eight real followers. By February seventeen. The seventeen-follower plateau holds. Mia is, slowly, realizing that the seventeen are a small chosen family — that one of them is a kid in Manila who comments on every video, one is a grandmother in Buffalo, one is an art-school freshman in London. Each volume a different follower becomes a real person to Mia in the comments. The long arc: the seventeen become each other's audience too. Mia learns content does not have to scale to count. Heartstopper-quiet. Komi-warm. A queer-coded Gen Alpha content-creator slice-of-life about the audience that is, actually, enough.
series.reader_promise: Seventeen people is a family. The audience is, actually, enough.
series.audience: Gen Alpha 13–15 quiet content-creators who have ever opened a channel under a different name
series.marketing_angle: A queer-coded Gen Alpha content-creator slice-of-life about the audience small enough to love you.
series.long_arc_spine: A 14-year-old runs a YouTube channel with three followers — all of whom are her — and posts one quiet five-minute video every Sunday about a small thing in her week; over five volumes the channel grows to seventeen followers and then plateaus there, and the protagonist learns that seventeen is, actually, enough.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Channel opened. Three followers (all her). The dishwasher video. The first eighth view.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: The first real follower. Manila. He comments on the cat. Mia replies.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Eight followers. The art-school freshman in London comments on the bus driver. Mia and her chat about her commute.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Seventeen followers. The grandmother in Buffalo asks if Mia is okay. Mia says yes. She means it.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Seventeen holds. Mia stops checking analytics. The seventeen start commenting on each other's comments.

series.series_logline: Six neurodivergent grad students. One Discord. One book a month. Two a.m. every second Saturday.
series.series_description: Reni is twenty-two, autistic, a first-year master's student in literature at a regional university in Pittsburgh, and she has not slept on a Saturday at midnight since she was twelve. In September she starts a private Discord book club for five other neurodivergent grad students she has met across two campuses. They meet on voice from 2 a.m. to 4 a.m. on the second Saturday of every month. They read one book a month — Frieren, Klara and the Sun, Convenience Store Woman, a contemporary translation, an essay collection. They do not do icebreakers. They start with one sentence each about something small in the week. Then they talk about the book until one of them yawns and someone closes it down. Reni, who has spent her whole life performing the role of a person at social events, is learning to host without performing. Each volume one member's life shifts — a diagnosis, a breakup, a thesis pivot, a sibling moving in — and the club holds. The long arc: in May the original six invite a seventh — a Black autistic kid named Pris, a high-school senior — and Reni learns that hosting can be a torch passed. Heartstopper-warm. Frieren-quiet. A queer-coded Gen Z neurodivergent chosen-family book-club story drawn at 2 a.m. scale.
series.reader_promise: There is a club at 2 a.m. They do not do icebreakers. You belong.
series.audience: Gen Z 21–27 neurodivergent grad-school readers in chosen-family book clubs
series.marketing_angle: A queer-coded Gen Z neurodivergent book-club series drawn at the warmth of Frieren.
series.long_arc_spine: A 22-year-old autistic graduate student hosts a 2 a.m. Discord book club for fellow neurodivergent readers who cannot sleep; six members; one book a month; over five volumes the book club becomes a chosen-family rhythm and the protagonist learns to host without performing.

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: Club night 1. Frieren. The first sentence each. The first yawn. The first not-performing.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: Club night 3. Convenience Store Woman. A member's autism diagnosis at 23. The club holds it gently.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: Club night 5. Klara and the Sun. A member's grandparent dies. The club is the wake.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: Club night 7. A translation essay. Reni's thesis pivot. The club is the writing-group.
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: Club night 9. Pris joins. High-school senior. The club is, now, multi-generational neurodivergence. Reni hands her the hosting torch for night 10.

series.series_title: 夜のコンビニ、三番目の棚
series.series_logline: 深夜のコンビニで、不安を抱えたお客の肩に乗る小さな生き物が見える店員。彼女はその人に合う棚を、ちゃんと知っている。
series.series_description: 美緒は三軒茶屋の二十四時間営業のコンビニで夜勤を務めている。蛍光灯のかすかな唸りは、いつの間にか耳に入らなくなった。最初のお客は、眠れない法律事務所勤めの男性。彼の肩には、小さなちらつく生き物がいる。美緒はディカフェのコーヒーとラベンダー味の飴を打ち、紙片を一枚そっと添える。そこには、パニックが戻ってきたとき自分にかける一言だけが書いてある。生き物は残る。客は帰る。夜ごとに新しい客と、新しい生き物。家賃の不安、論文の渦、別れた恋人の新しいパートナー。美緒は戦わない。お客がその生き物を少しだけ楽に抱えて帰れるよう、棚の商品と小さな儀式を選ぶ。蛍光灯のしんとした明かりの中、静かに進む話。
series.reader_promise: あなたの不安にはかたちがあって、それを知っている店員がいる。
series.audience: 都市部で初職に就いた二十代前半の女性、自分の不安に名前をつけられる読者層
series.marketing_angle: 『コンビニ人間』に小さな妖怪を一匹入れたら——そして、その妖怪が優しかったら。
series.long_arc_spine: 三軒茶屋の深夜コンビニに来る不安を抱えたお客一人ひとりの肩に、店員にだけ見える小さな蛍光色の生き物が乗っている。彼女は商品と一緒に、ポケットに収まる小さな儀式を渡す。その生き物がもともと誰のものだったのかを、巻を重ねるごとに知っていく。

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: 最初の客。法律事務所の青年。肩の上の蛾のような生き物。ディカフェとラベンダーの飴、そして紙片一枚。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: 美大生の客。スマートフォンを開いた瞬間に現れる、鏡のように小さな比較の生き物。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: 夜勤明けの看護師。生き物は、抱きすぎて色が褪せかけている。美緒は喪のための棚も置けることを知る。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: 最初の常連客。法律事務所の青年が戻ってくる。肩の生き物はひと回り小さい。美緒は自分の肩にも生き物がいることに、初めて気づく。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: 本部のエリアマネージャー巡回。棚卸しが遅いと指摘される。店舗そのものに棲む生き物——企業の燃え尽き妖怪——に名前がつく日。美緒は残ることを選ぶ。

series.series_logline: 大学生のいりすは、亡き祖母から手のひらほどの革の手帖を受け取る。手帖には、近くにいる人の不安が、祖母の字で書き写されていく。
series.series_description: 祖母の四十九日が明けて、いりすは形見の手帖を受け取った。トランプの箱ほどの大きさの、革表紙の手帖。添えられていた指示はただ一行——『いつもそばに。バスでは開かないこと』。最初にバスで開いたとき、隣に座っていた女子高生の翌日の化学の試験への怯えが、祖母の筆跡で書かれていた。いりすは書くのを止められないが、応えることはできる。一巻ごとに一つ、小さな作法を覚える——呼吸、五・四・三・二・一のグラウンディング、別の言葉での言い換え、自分への許可。声に出して言うと、ページは消え、不安は隣人の体から離れていく。手帖は埋まっては空き、また埋まる。最後のページだけは、いりすが生まれてからずっと白いまま。誰の名前が現れるのか。武蔵野の真夜中までやっている図書館、ポメラニアンの同居人、ゆっくり進む大学生活の物語。
series.reader_promise: どんな不安にも、それに合う作法がある。作法は手帖一冊に収まる。
series.audience: メンタルヘルスの語彙を持ち、亡き祖母の記憶を抱える大学生世代
comp_titles: コーヒーが冷めないうちに
series.marketing_angle: 『コーヒーが冷めないうちに』の祖母の温度を、心理学リテラシーのある大学生世代に手渡したら。
series.long_arc_spine: 亡くなった祖母から受け継いだ小さな手帖は、触れた人のいちばん近くにいる誰かの恐れを書き写す。主人公は巻ごとに一つずつ、その恐れを返す作法を覚えていく。

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: バスの少女。化学の試験。一呼吸。ページが消える。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: バスの運転手。リストラに怯える三児の父。別の作法。手帖は満ち、また空く。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: 同居人のポメラニアン女子。手帖が開かないまま勝手に書き始める。RSDが可視化される。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: 顔の知らない人。書かれていたのは喪——まだ知らない誰かのためのもの。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: 祖母の筆跡が、少しだけ変わる。手帖がいりすに返事をしている、というかすかな兆し。

series.series_title: 同居人は私の不安が見える
series.series_logline: ネット掲示板で見つけた同居人と暮らし始めたあしゃ。三日目の朝、シリアルの箱越しに同居人が小さな灰色の生き物に向かって『おはよう、ちびちゃん』と言った。
series.series_description: あしゃは二十四歳のノンバイナリー・イラストレーター。高校生のころから、肩のあたりにいる小さな灰色の生き物を、ずっと一人で抱えてきた。風呂にも、バスにも、寝室にもついてくる。誰にも言ったことがなかった。新しい同居人のヌールはレバノン系日本人の編み物好きな経理職で、引っ越して三日目の朝、シリアルの箱越しに『おはよう、ちびちゃん』とこともなげに言った。一巻ごとにヌールは、同じように『生き物が見える』友人を紹介してくれる——蛸を背負ったトランスの男性バリスタ、髪に小さなカラスを宿す聴覚障がいの院生、三匹を連れたポリアモリーの多言語話者。仲間が増えるたび、生き物たちはやわらかくなる。長期の伏線——ヌールに見えるようになったきっかけは何で、なぜ彼女自身の生き物がいちばん大きいのか。クィア性、神経多様性、世代を越えた傷は、ここでは前提として置かれている。週ごとのテーマは『カミングアウト』ではなく、『カムイントゥ——自分に帰っていく』ことの方。
series.reader_promise: あなたの不安は本物で、かたちがあって、それを見たうえで一緒にいてくれる人がいる。
series.audience: クィアでネウロダイヴァージェントな都市部の二十代クリエイティブ層、メンタルヘルス・リテラシー高め
comp_titles: とんがり帽子のアトリエ
series.marketing_angle: 『違国日記』の選びとる家族のぬくもりを、メンタルヘルスを語彙にもつZ世代のクィアな日常に重ねたら。
series.long_arc_spine: 二十四歳のクィアなイラストレーターの不安は、家に棲む小さな妖怪として現れる。それが見えるのは新しい同居人だけ——日常魔法の使い手で、似た事情を持つ友人たちを少しずつ紹介してくれる。

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: 引っ越しの日。シリアルの箱越しの一言。あしゃは恥ではなく安堵で、洗面所で泣く。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: トランスのバリスタ。背中の蛸に名前がつく。下北沢のコーヒー店が、もう一つの居場所になる。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: 聴覚障がいの院生。髪のカラスは手話を話す。仲間は四人になる。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: 家族とのテレビ通話。画面の中で、あしゃの生き物が三倍に膨らむ。ヌールはフレームから外れない。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: 深夜二時の台所で、一度だけヌール自身の生き物が見える。ヌールよりも大きい。長い謎が、ここから始まる。

series.series_logline: 井戸ゆいの最後のパニック発作は、終電の小田急線だった。そこで一駅多く乗ってしまい、地図にない駅で温かい麦茶を渡された。あの駅には、誰かのために戻れる。
series.series_description: ゆいは二十六歳。大学で最初の発作、二十三で最悪の発作、二十六歳のあの夜の小田急線が、最後の発作だった。いつもの駅をひとつ過ぎてしまい、降り立った駅は色が違った——見たことのない深い緑のタイル、三日月のかたちの裸電球、紙コップに温かい麦茶を注いで『またいつでもおいで』と言った駅員。信じなかった。けれど、自分と同じ路線で誰かが目に見えて発作を起こした夜、降りずに過ぎ、もう一駅。月の駅は、開いた。各巻、別の都市の、終点のひとつ先にある駅を訪ねる——小田急、山手、地下鉄丸ノ内、京都の市電、大阪環状。それぞれの駅は、かつてそこで降りた誰かが、いま勤めている。一巻ごとに一人を、ホームへ送り出す。長期の謎は、この路線を最初に敷いたのは誰で、終点はどんな場所か。
series.reader_promise: いちばん辛かった夜には扉があって、別の誰かのために、もう一度くぐることができる。
series.audience: 都市の通勤電車で働く二十代後半の知識労働者層、パニックや不安を言語化できる読者
comp_titles: コーヒーが冷めないうちに
series.marketing_angle: 『千と千尋』の駅を、パニックを名前で呼べる世代の通勤路線に置き換えたら。
series.long_arc_spine: 二十六歳の女性が最後のパニック発作の夜に降りてしまった駅は、地図に載っていない一駅先の駅だった。誰かが発作のさなかにいるときだけ、その駅は開く。彼女は何度もその駅に戻り、降りた人を一人ずつ送り出していく。

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: 小田急線。最初の帰還。十代の少年がパニックの最中にいる。麦茶。最初の一言——『またいつでもおいで』。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: 山手線。日曜夜の電車に揺られるサラリーマン。駅にも名前がつく。あの夜の駅員と再会する。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: 京都の市電。修士論文の佳境にいる院生。降りた駅は『出口』でもあると、ゆいは知る。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: 大阪環状線。共同制作の打ち合わせで燃え尽きた若いクリエイター。初めての複数人ケース。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: 地下鉄丸ノ内。発作の最中にいるのは、ゆいの大学時代のルームメイト。彼女はゆいを見分ける。物語の核が、ここで動き出す。

series.series_logline: 日曜だけ開く。お客一人につき一つの儀式。その儀式はかつて誰かのもので、いつかまた別の誰かのものになる。
series.series_description: 蓮はひとつの鍵と、ひとつの指示だけを祖母から受け継いだ——『日曜のみ、十一時から五時まで開けること』。下町の商店街の二階、四畳半ほどの店。並んでいるのは商品らしいものではない。瓶に入った折りたたまれた紙片、引き出しの中の燻した茶葉、結び目の入った絹糸の盆。それでも、客は店を見つけてやって来る。蓮は耳を傾け、一つだけ問いを返し、棚から一つを選んで手渡す。客は三十秒で、机の上でも電車の中でも、駅のトイレの個室でも実践できる小さな儀式を一つ、ポケットに入れて帰る。在庫が補充されるのは、客が戻ってきて、自分に効いた儀式を少しだけ変えて店に置いていったとき。長期の謎——祖母が最初の店主で、誰にも渡さなかった儀式が一つだけ、レジの引き出しの奥に畳まれて残っている。蓮はゆっくり、その一冊の『書』になっていく。
series.reader_promise: 毎週日曜、月曜に使える小さなものを一つ、ポケットに入れて持ち帰れる。
series.audience: 都市部で働き始めた二十代後半、暮らしの中の儀式に関心のある読者層
series.marketing_angle: 『薬屋のひとりごと』の職能の確かさを、現代の小さな商店街に下ろしたら。
series.long_arc_spine: 二十五歳の女性が祖母から受け継いだ日曜だけ開く小さな小薬屋。来る客一人ひとりに、ポケットに入る小さな儀式を一つ渡す。儀式はかつて別の誰かのものだった——主人公はその系譜を、巻を重ねるごとに辿っていく。

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: 店を開けた最初の日曜。五十代の女性。折りたたまれた紙片。一九八二年に父を落ち着かせた一文。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: 大学一年生。燻した茶葉。手順——湯を注ぐ、香りを嗅ぐ、飲まないこと。儀式は彼女と帰っていく。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: 避難訓練の翌週に来る高校教師。結び目の絹糸。糸に名前がつく。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: 最初の戻り客。一巻目の女性が、紙片を少し変えて返しに来る。在庫が増える。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: 閉店時、蓮はレジの引き出しを開ける。祖母の畳まれた紙片が、初めて目に入る。読みはしない。

series.series_title: 夜更けのポッドキャスト
series.series_logline: 毎週火曜日の二十三時、彼女は番組を録音する。毎週火曜日の午前三時、彼女の脇にいる小さな眠りの神が、聴いている誰かの夢を訪ねる。
series.series_description: 蓮は二十三歳、ジャーナリズム専攻の三回生。聴取者二百七十人の小さなポッドキャストを運営している。番組は青空文庫の朗読を、ゆっくりと、低い声で読むだけ。マイクの脇には、子どものころから見えている小さな灰色の眠りの神が座っている。ある水曜日、メールが届く——『昨夜、灰色のちいさな生き物の夢を見て、三週間ぶりに眠れました』。蓮は気づく。神は、録音から出ていっている。一巻ごとに、神は一人のリスナーを訪ねる——その人の眠れない理由が描かれ、夢の中での訪問が描かれ、その人が眠る。リスナーは、それが蓮であることを知らない。長期の弧——神は少しずつ歳をとっている。蓮はその世話の仕方を学んでいく。二百七十一人目のリスナーがいて、その人の夢にだけ、神は行こうとしない。それは誰の物語なのか。
series.reader_promise: あなたの眠りを、誰かが手伝ってくれている。それが誰かは、たぶん最後まで分からない。
series.audience: 不眠を名前で呼べる、ポッドキャスト好きの二十代前半
series.marketing_angle: 『蟲師』の静けさを、ポッドキャストを聴いて眠ろうとする世代のために。
series.long_arc_spine: 二十三歳の眠りのポッドキャスト配信者の脇に、いつも小さな灰色の眠りの神が座っている。ある日からその神は、配信を聴くリスナーたちの夢に出向き、一人ずつの眠れない夜をほどいていく。

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: 最初のメール。三週間眠れない医学部生。神が訪ねる。彼は眠る。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: 夜勤の看護師。神は蓮に、交代勤務の概日リズムを教える。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: 親が夜ごとに喧嘩する家の女子高生。神の介入は、いつもより少し長い。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: 番組内で神に気づいたリスナーが現れる。メールは一行だけ——『ありがとう』。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: 二百七十一人目。神が行きたがらない夢の物語。長期の謎が動き出す。

series.series_logline: あらゆる都市の終点の、もうひとつ先に医院はある。夢渡りの夜勤職員は、一巻ごとに一人の眠れる人を取り戻す。
series.series_description: 羽音は二十六歳。別れと喪の連鎖で一年分の眠りを失い、午前四時の山手線で一駅多く乗ってしまった。月の駅の医院が、彼女を雇った。いまは夜勤の夢渡り職員。シフトごとに世界中のどこかの都市の眠れない一人が、夢の領域として現れる——水底のバス停、時計だらけの森、海辺でもある台所、読者自身の未完の思考が並ぶ図書館。一巻に一つの領域、一人の眠り手。長期の弧——羽音自身の夢の領域は、勤め始めてからずっと閉鎖されている。準備ができるまで開かない。彼女は、まだ準備ができていない。
series.reader_promise: あなたの眠りには街があって、誰かがそこで夜勤を引き受けてくれている。
series.audience: 都市部で不眠を抱える二十代後半、睡眠アプリ利用者、通勤電車世代
series.marketing_angle: 『千と千尋』の駅を、不眠を抱える世代のための医院に書き換えたら。
series.long_arc_spine: あらゆる大都市の最終駅の、ひとつ先にある霊的な眠り医院。主人公は夜勤の夢渡り職員として働き、各巻ひとつの夢の領域を訪ねて、一人の眠れない人を取り戻していく。

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: 水底のバス停。喪の不眠を抱えた高校生。取り戻される。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: 時計だらけの森。生まれたての赤ん坊を抱えた新米の親の睡眠周期。取り戻される。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: 海辺でもある台所。夜勤を続ける年配の看護師。取り戻される。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: 未完の思考の図書館。羽音の大学時代のルームメイトが、偶然そこに現れる。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: シフト明け、羽音自身の領域が一瞬だけ見える。読者だけがそれを見る。

series.series_logline: 一時から五時まで。一人につき一杯。粥は温かい。客は眠る。店主は眠らない。
series.series_description: 芽生は二十六歳。下町の古い住宅の地下、看板もウェブにも載らない小さなキッチンを切り盛りしている。誰かに紹介されてきた、と思って来るのに、紹介した人の名前が思い出せない、そんな夜の店。深夜一時から五時まで。一人につき一杯。出てくるのは必ず温かい穀物の粥——お粥、粥のような雑炊、麦粥、玄米粥。芽生はひとつだけ問いを返して、一つだけ薬味を選ぶ。各巻、ひとりの客がやってくる。眠れない型——反芻、喪、過覚醒、画面疲れ、予期不安——とその粥が描かれる。客は帰って眠る。二度と来ない、もう必要がないから。長期の弧——芽生自身は四年眠っていない。客に出すたびに、自分にも少しずつ出している。二百杯目に出る粥は、ようやく自分自身の粥になる。
series.reader_promise: 一杯と一晩で、あなたはたぶん眠れる。
series.audience: 都市部で不眠を抱える二十代後半、食と温度に敏感な読者
comp_titles: コーヒーが冷めないうちに
series.marketing_angle: 『深夜食堂』の温度を、不眠を抱える現代の若い読者のために。
series.long_arc_spine: 深夜一時から五時まで、看板も検索結果も出ない地下のキッチン。一人の客に温かい一杯の粥を出す。客は眠って帰り、店主は今日も眠らない。

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: 最初の客。反芻の型。カルダモン入りのお粥。眠る。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: 喪の不眠。生姜と塩を入れた中華粥。眠る。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: 過覚醒の新米の母。味噌を一さじ落とした粥。眠る。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: 画面疲れの高校生。シナモンと黒蜂蜜の麦粥。眠る。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: 初めて、芽生のことをほとんど見分ける客が現れる。キッチンが、ほんの一瞬ちらつく。長期の弧が動き出す。

series.series_logline: 月四百九十円の有料プランには、妖怪が一人ついてくる。妖怪はユーザーの夢を訪ねる。ユーザーは眠る。クインはその過程を人間味あるものに保つ仕事をしている。
series.series_description: 麻衣は三十二歳。睡眠記録アプリのスタートアップに『コンテンツモデレーター』として業務委託で入った三日目、有料プランの裏側を知る——課金されたユーザーの夢には、小さな、職業意識の高い、少し退屈そうな妖怪たちが派遣されている。麻衣の仕事は、訪問ログを見て介入を承認するか拒否するかを判断すること。ほとんどは問題ない。たまに問題がある。各巻一件——十代の侵入思考に強く出すぎた妖怪、当直で寝てしまいユーザーがその妖怪自身の夢を見てしまった件、ユーザーに情がうつってしまった妖怪。麻衣は妖怪たちを支える側に回っていく。長期の弧——会社は上場準備に入る。妖怪たちは目論見書に名前が載るのを嫌がる。麻衣はどちらに立つかを選ぶ。
series.reader_promise: あなたの睡眠アプリには、見えない従業員がいる。誰かが彼らを大切にしている。
series.audience: ウェルネス・テック業界のミレニアル世代、業務委託・モデレーション職に近い層
series.marketing_angle: 『セクシー田中さん』の職場の手触りを、睡眠ウェルテックの裏側に持ち込んだら。
series.long_arc_spine: 睡眠記録アプリの有料プランは、実は妖怪たちが運営している。彼らはユーザーの夢を訪ねて眠りを助ける。主人公は唯一の人間モデレーター——一巻ごとに一つの介入を、人間味のあるものとして承認していく。

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: 最初の承認拒否。やりすぎた妖怪。十代の侵入思考の件。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: 当直で居眠りした妖怪。ユーザーがその妖怪の夢を見る。人事との会話。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: 情がうつった妖怪。ユーザーはシングルファーザー。麻衣が交渉する。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: 初めての上場準備会議。CEOにも妖怪がいることに、麻衣は気づく。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: 麻衣は辞表を書く。あるいは交渉案を書く。選択が見えてくる。

series.series_logline: 依羅は誰かの夢を代わりに見られる。何年も前から、知らずにやっていた。いま、意図して始めようとしている。
series.series_description: 依羅は二十二歳、美大の四回生。昔から夢の記憶が異様にはっきりしている。四年生になって気づく——昨日夢に出てきた友人が、本当にその夢を見ていた。メールが届いた、内容が一致した。依羅が代わりに見ている夢は、その人が見るべきなのに、自分では見られなかった夢。各巻、依羅は新しい夢の仲間妖怪を一匹ずつ得る——喪の夢を手伝う小さな狐、創作のスランプの夢を手伝う鷺、別れの整理の夢を手伝う蛾。受け手は、それを知らない。代償——依羅自身の眠りは浅く、自分の夢はだんだん薄くなる。長期の弧——誰が彼女に教えたのか。そして、依羅がまだ自分のために見ていない、最後の夢は何か。
series.reader_promise: あなたが必要としていた夢を、誰かが代わりに見てくれている。本人はそれを言わない。
series.audience: 二十代前半、夢日記を書く美大・創作系の読者
series.marketing_angle: 『蟲師』の手つきを、夢日記をつける世代のために、贈与の物語として。
series.long_arc_spine: 二十二歳の美大生は、別の誰かが見るべき夢を代わりに見ることができる。手伝ってくれる夢の仲間妖怪が、巻を重ねるごとに一匹ずつ増えていく。

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: 友人のための喪の夢。狐が現れる。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: 同級生のための創作スランプの夢。鷺が現れる。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: 別れた相手のための整理の夢。蛾が現れる。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: 初めて、知らない人のために夢を派遣する。仲間が増える。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: 依羅自身のためにとっておいた夢——その輪郭だけが、ほのめかされる。

series.series_title: スタジオの小さなトレーナー
series.series_logline: 新しい受講生は、誰もが小さなトレーナー妖怪を連れてくる。妖怪は、口が言わないことを身体がどこで抱えているか、紗英に教える。
series.series_description: 紗英は二十五歳。十九までクラシックバレエを真面目にやっていた。いまは京都の小さなスタジオで、朝六時と夜九時のピラティス・クラスを担当している。妖怪が見え始めたのは、教え始めて二年目。新しい受講生の肩や腰に、小さな鳥、小さな狐、ときに紗英の知らないかたちのもの。妖怪は、その身体が何を抱えているかを示してくれる——胸郭で止まった呼吸、骨盤に置かれた喪、肩甲骨の奥の名乗れない怒り。紗英はピラティスのキューを、心理的なキューと同じ温度で出す。肩甲骨に手を置く。然るべきタイミングで、ひとつだけ問いを出す。妖怪はやわらかくなる。客はまた来る。長期の弧——紗英自身の妖怪は、十九歳から離れない。誰にも見えない。それが彼女に踊らせない。踊らせるべきかどうかは、長い問いとして残る。
series.reader_promise: あなたの身体には妖怪がいて、それを見てくれるトレーナーがいる。
series.audience: ピラティス・ヨガ・ボルダリングをする二十代後半、身体への意識が高い層
comp_titles: とんがり帽子のアトリエ
スキップとローファー
ハチミツとクローバー
series.marketing_angle: 『聲の形』の身体への注意深さを、ピラティスのスタジオに置いたら。
series.long_arc_spine: 元バレエダンサーのピラティス・インストラクター、二十五歳。新しい受講生の肩には、小さな『トレーナー妖怪』が乗っている。妖怪は、客の身体が何を抱えているかを示す。各巻、一人の客の身体の物語が、そっとほどかれる。

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: 最初の新規受講生。鳥の妖怪。止まった呼吸。一回のセッションでほどける。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: 狐の妖怪。腰。物語は喪。二回かかってほどける。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: 名前のない妖怪。骨盤。客はまだセラピストにも話していない。紗英は静かにいる。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: 初めての戻り客。妖怪は小さくなっている。紗英の小さな報酬。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: 鏡の中で、紗英は自分の妖怪を初めてはっきり見る。読者にもそのかたちが見える。紗英は目をそらす。

series.series_logline: 保管庫の手紙は一九三二年から増え続けている。各巻、ひとつだけ、声に出して読む。
series.series_description: 詩穂は二十七歳。札幌でソマティック・セラピーの修士課程を終えようとしている。亡き大伯母から受け継いだのは、木製の引き出し式の保管庫——いろいろな筆跡で、ある身体の部位から別の部位に宛てて書かれた手紙が、年代順に並んでいる。『手首様、肩より。あなたの分も、私が抱えていました』。『喉様、腹より。あなたが飲み込んだ言葉は、ここに届いています』。手紙は一九三二年から、大伯母が四つの街のクライアントから集め続けてきた。詩穂の仕事は、一巻目では、これをどうするかを考えること。三巻目までに、現在のクライアントの身体の物語と一致する手紙を、声に出して読み始める。読まれた部位が、ふっと光るようにゆるむ。長期の弧——保管庫の中に、生まれる前から詩穂宛ての一通が、確かに置かれている。
series.reader_promise: あなたの身体は、ずっと手紙を書いてきた。誰かがそれを声に出して読み始める。
series.audience: ソマティック・セラピーに関心のある二十代後半、クィアな読者層
comp_titles: とんがり帽子のアトリエ
series.marketing_angle: ソマティック・セラピーを、手紙という装置で誰かに手渡せるかたちにしたら。
series.long_arc_spine: ソマティック・セラピスト修行中の二十七歳が、亡き大伯母から受け継いだ手紙の保管庫。手紙はすべて、ある身体の部位から別の部位に宛てて書かれている。各巻、ひとつの部位を訪ね、一人の客のためにひとつの手紙を声に出して読む。

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: 手首。肩からの手紙。慢性の痛みを抱えた初めての客。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: 喉。腹からの手紙。飲み込まれた喪を抱えた客。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: 膝の裏。腰からの手紙。選びを保留にし続ける客。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: 鎖骨。顎からの手紙。声を抑え続けた客。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: 詩穂は自分宛ての封筒を、保管庫の中に見つける。長い弧が動き出す。まだ開封はしない。

series.series_logline: パン屋の二階、四畳半の整体院。彼女と一緒に働く妖怪の名簿は、巻ごとに増える。客はベッドの上で眠る。
series.series_description: 直美は二十六歳。柔道整復師の資格を取って二年、群馬の小さな町の商店街、パン屋の二階の四畳半を借りて整体院を開いた。最初の妖怪が現れたのは三週間目——肩の妖怪。直美が正しい結び目を見つけるのを手伝ってくれる。一巻目の時点で、名簿は三体（肩・顎・腰）。各巻、止まった身体の物語を持つ客が一人やってきて、対応する妖怪が直美と一緒に手をほどく。客はベッドの上で眠ってしまう。家に帰って、よく休む。妖怪は客にはついていかない。長期の弧——季節ごとに新しい妖怪が一体ずつ名簿に加わる——骨盤（四巻）、横隔膜（七巻）、首（十一巻）。謎は——妖怪たちは実は身体の部位に割り当てられているのではなく、抱えられた感情に割り当てられている。それぞれに、直美がまだ知らない物語がある。
series.reader_promise: あなたの張りには名前があって、かたちがあって、ちゃんとほどかれる。
series.audience: 二十代後半、整体・ボディワークに関心のある都市部の社会人
comp_titles: とんがり帽子のアトリエ
スキップとローファー
series.marketing_angle: 商店街の小さな整体院を、身体リテラシーの高い若い読者のために、仲間の物語として。
series.long_arc_spine: 二十六歳の整体師の、商店街の二階にある小さな治療院。彼女と一緒に働く『張りの妖怪』の数が、巻を重ねるごとに増えていく——肩、顎、腰、骨盤、横隔膜、首。各巻、客の止まった身体の物語に、対応する妖怪が手を貸す。

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: 最初の客。肩の妖怪。長時間のデスクワーク。ほどける。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: 顎の妖怪。学校で講演を続ける教師。ほどける。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: 腰の妖怪。喪を抱えた客。ほどける。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: 骨盤の妖怪が名簿に加わる。最初の客は産後の母親。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: 直美は気づく——肩の妖怪は、実は喪の妖怪でもある。謎が動き出す。

series.series_title: オンラインで、やわらかくなる
series.series_logline: DMに届くのは、フォロワー一人ひとりの止まった呼吸。麻友は九十秒の練習を一つずつ考えて、一本の短い動画にする。
series.series_description: 麻友は二十四歳。呼吸法インストラクターの資格を取って、TikTokとYouTubeショートで配信を始めた。一巻目の時点で登録は一万二千人、コメントは穏やか、DMはおびただしい。読んでいるうちに、麻友はパターンに気づく——一人ひとり、呼吸の止まり方が違う。胸の上で止まる人、吐ききれない人、肋骨の下が動かない人、息を取り合いに行ってしまう人。各巻、麻友は一人のフォロワーのパターンに合う九十秒の練習を新しく考案し、台所で撮って投稿する。コメント欄にひとつ、『これでした』とつく。長期の弧——配信が大きくなる。スポンサーが現れる。麻友の練習は、そのうちのどれが商品化され、どれが無料で残されるべきか。彼女自身の呼吸のことも、ゆっくり描かれる。
series.reader_promise: あなたの止まった呼吸には、九十秒の練習がある。
series.audience: SNSで呼吸法・ヨガを取り入れている二十代の社会人
comp_titles: ハチミツとクローバー
series.marketing_angle: ウェルネス系インフルエンサーの仕事を、職能の物語としてまっすぐ描いたら。
series.long_arc_spine: 二十四歳の呼吸法インストラクターのSNSのDMには、フォロワーの止まった呼吸の癖がひとつずつ届く。各巻、彼女は新しい九十秒の練習をひとつ考案し、一本の動画として送り出す。

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: 胸の上で止まる呼吸。十代の女子高校生のDM。九十秒の練習。投稿される。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: 吐ききれない呼吸。新人会社員。練習。投稿。コメント。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: 肋骨の下が動かない呼吸。プログラマーの男性。練習。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: 息を取り合いに行く呼吸。ステージに立つ歌い手。練習。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: 最初のスポンサー提案。麻友は、何を売り、何を売らないかを選び始める。

series.series_logline: 番台に立つと、湯に浸かった客の身体がやわらぐ瞬間が、ちゃんと見える。
series.series_description: 深雪は三十四歳。大阪の広告代理店を辞めて、母方の祖父が遺した下町の銭湯を継いだ。番台に座って三ヶ月目、湯気越しに気づく——湯に浸かった客の身体から、肩や腰に乗っていた小さな張りの妖怪が、ふっと離れて湯に溶けていく瞬間がある。深雪はその瞬間に立ち会う。湯の温度を整え、薬湯の日を決め、休憩室で一杯の冷たい麦茶を注ぐ。各巻、客一人ひとりが、湯の中で身体が思い出した物語を、深雪に少しだけ話す——亡くなった夫のこと、続けてきた介護のこと、辞めた仕事のこと。深雪は聞く。長期の弧——銭湯は経営が苦しい。再開発の話もある。深雪は、この場所をどう残すかを、巻を重ねるごとに考えていく。
series.reader_promise: 湯に浸かれば、身体は思い出す。番台に、それを見てくれる人がいる。
series.audience: 三十代のキャリア中盤、地元に帰る・場所を継ぐことを考える読者
comp_titles: コーヒーが冷めないうちに
series.marketing_angle: 銭湯という最後の都市の余白を、身体リテラシーの物語として残す。
series.long_arc_spine: 下町の銭湯の番台に三十四歳で帰ってきた女性。湯の中で身体がやわらぐとき、客の肩から張りの妖怪がふっと離れる瞬間が見える。各巻、ひとりの客の体が思い出す物語を、湯気越しに描く。

opening_5_volume_arc.vol_1: 番台三ヶ月目の朝。常連の七十代の女性。湯の中で肩の張りの妖怪がほどける。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_2: 介護を続ける四十代の娘。週に二度の薬湯。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_3: 辞めたばかりの三十代会社員。長湯。番台越しの五分の会話。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_4: 再開発の説明会。銭湯の存続。妖怪たちが番台のまわりに集まる。
opening_5_volume_arc.vol_5: 深雪は、自分自身が湯にゆっくり浸かる夜を持つ。彼女自身の妖怪が、ひとつだけ離れる。

series.series_logline: 彼女の手の中で、欠けた器が小さく震える。割れた茶碗には、前の持ち主の身体が抱えていたものが、まだ残っている。
